Noah works at a company that scientifically sizes men for condoms — a topic Conan had many questions about
The measurement process turned out to involve considerably more dignity than Conan had assumed
Conan navigated the entire conversation without once making it about himself, which is a personal best
We Have A Condom For That.fanQuestion: Do you think knowing your correct condom size would change your life?
We Have A Condom For That.conanResponse: Conan said yes, absolutely, and expressed deep personal regret that this technology wasn't available when he was younger, before catching himself and changing the subject.
We Have A Condom For That.summary: Conan speaks with Noah from Woburn, Massachusetts, who works as a condom size specialist — an actual professional role at a company that applies scientific measurement methodology to ensure proper condom fit. The premise is inherently comedic and Conan leans into it, but Noah handles the subject with a matter-of-fact professionalism that creates a running contrast throughout the episode. The job exists because ill-fitting condoms are a genuine public health concern, and Noah frames it in those terms before Conan can fully establish the joke, which in its own way is funnier. Conan has a long list of questions, most of which orbit the central issue of how the measurement process actually works and what the client experience is like. Noah explains the procedure in a way that preserves considerably more dignity than Conan had anticipated, and Conan notes this explicitly — it is both a punchline and a genuine observation that the company has thought carefully about removing stigma from the experience. The conversation moves into the latest developments in condom technology, an area where Noah has real expertise and genuine enthusiasm, and Conan discovers that there is substantially more innovation happening in this space than he would have guessed. The episode earns its suggestive title without relying purely on double entendres, instead finding comedy in the gap between the awkwardness of the subject and the complete normalcy with which Noah discusses it.

highlights_v2.title: The Measurement Process Explained
highlights_v2.summary: Conan asks Noah to walk through exactly how the sizing process works, and the answer is both more clinical and more humane than the question implied. Noah explains that the company has designed the experience to feel more like a medical consultation than anything embarrassing, which catches Conan slightly off guard. Conan observes that the company has clearly put significant thought into the dignity question, then admits he was hoping for more chaos.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: It is a lot more dignified than you are clearly hoping it would be.

highlights_v2.title: Condom Technology Frontiers
highlights_v2.summary: Noah pivots to discussing genuine innovation in condom design and materials science, areas where he has real expertise, and Conan discovers that the field has moved considerably beyond what most people assume. The conversation becomes unexpectedly substantive before Conan steers it back toward comedy, noting that he now knows more about latex engineering than he ever anticipated learning on a Thursday.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: There is a surprising amount of R&D happening in this space.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's Unprecedented Restraint
highlights_v2.summary: A recurring meta-joke throughout the episode is Conan's visible effort to not make the conversation entirely about himself — a characteristic he acknowledges directly while still nudging everything back toward his own anxieties and worldview. Noah's grounded professionalism makes Conan's attempts at self-insertion land as comedy rather than rudeness. By the end, Conan remarks that this may be one of the few conversations in his career where he showed genuine journalistic discipline.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I have not made this about me once. I deserve some kind of award.

highlights: Conan asks fan: 'What do you think you'd do if you caught a hotel guest doing something truly outrageous?'
Trisha runs a small hotel in the Philippines and moonlights as its in-house detective
The hotel detective role involves solving disputes, missing property cases, and a surprising amount of drama
Conan and Trisha bonded over their shared belief that most people are up to something
The House Dick.summary: Conan speaks with Trisha from the Philippines, who operates a small hotel and functions as its in-house detective — a role that makes her responsible for resolving disputes among guests, investigating missing property claims, monitoring suspicious behavior, and generally functioning as a one-person security and conflict-resolution apparatus for an entire hospitality operation. The premise gives Conan everything he needs: an unusual job title, a setting full of strangers behaving badly, and a fan whose natural disposition seems to involve a healthy suspicion of human nature. Conan asks Trisha to walk him through the range of situations she has encountered as a hotel detective, and the answers reveal that the job is equal parts tedious bureaucracy and genuinely dramatic interpersonal crisis. Missing wallets and noise complaints form the baseline; the occasional incident that escalates past those categories provides the more interesting stories. The two bond over a shared philosophical premise — that most people are probably up to something — and Conan runs with this worldview, speculating about what Trisha would make of hotel guests she has not yet met. The episode's standout moment involves Conan asking Trisha to confront a hypothetical scenario: if she caught a guest doing something truly outrageous, what would she actually do? The question generates a response that is both pragmatic and quietly revealing about the emotional demands of working in a role that requires constant vigilance without constant escalation. Conan then speculates about whether he would be an asset or a liability as a hotel detective, and Trisha diplomatically declines to answer directly.

highlights_v2.title: A Day in the Hotel Detective Life
highlights_v2.summary: Trisha walks Conan through the actual daily texture of being a hotel detective — the missing items, the noise complaints, the guest disputes that require mediation rather than enforcement, and the occasional situation that does require enforcement. Conan discovers that the ratio of mundane to dramatic is not what the job title implies, though the dramatic cases are genuinely dramatic. The contrast between the glamorous TV version of detective work and the hotel reality provides most of the comedy.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Ninety percent of it is someone's wallet being exactly where they left it.

highlights_v2.title: What Would You Do With the Truly Outrageous
highlights_v2.summary: Conan poses the hypothetical: if Trisha caught a hotel guest doing something genuinely beyond the pale, something that exceeded all standard protocol, what would she actually do? The question is designed to get at the human reality of a role that requires enforcing norms against people who have decided norms don't apply to them. Trisha's answer is pragmatic and reveals the emotional discipline the job requires — the ability to stay calm when the situation is anything but.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You take a breath, you stay professional, and you figure out what the situation actually needs.

highlights_v2.title: Everyone Is Probably Up to Something
highlights_v2.summary: Conan and Trisha discover they share a worldview: given the right circumstances, most people are capable of minor transgression, and the hotel is a setting that tends to bring this out. The philosophical alignment between a hotel detective and a late-night talk show host — both of whom make their living reading people quickly — generates genuine warmth. Conan tests the theory against himself and concludes he would be a highly suspicious hotel guest.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You assume the best and prepare for the rest.

highlights: Josh co-founded a company selling direct-to-consumer caskets and urns — cutting out the funeral home markup
The casket industry's pricing turned out to be even more eyebrow-raising than Conan had expected
Josh believes that shopping for your own casket is actually an act of love for your family
Putting The Fun Back In Funeral.fanQuestion: Have you given any thought to what you want for your own funeral?
Putting The Fun Back In Funeral.conanResponse: Conan said he wants something 'theatrical and confusing,' possibly involving his body being launched from a catapult while an orchestra plays, to ensure everyone is too bewildered to grieve.
Putting The Fun Back In Funeral.summary: Josh, a casket company founder from Bellevue, Washington, calls in to tell Conan about his direct-to-consumer casket and urn business — a venture designed to disrupt the traditionally opaque and overpriced funeral industry. The conversation quickly becomes a masterclass in unexpected entrepreneurship as Josh walks Conan through exactly why funeral homes mark up caskets so dramatically, and why he decided to do something about it. Conan, characteristically unprepared for this level of specificity about death-related commerce, cycles through genuine surprise, morbid curiosity, and eventual admiration. Josh explains that the markup funeral homes apply to caskets is frequently staggering — often several hundred percent — and that his company lets families shop directly, cutting out the middleman and saving thousands of dollars during one of the most emotionally vulnerable moments of their lives. What catches Conan off guard most is Josh's framing: that buying your own casket in advance isn't morbid, it's actually a gift to your family, sparing them from making expensive, grief-clouded decisions. Conan pushes back with the comic incredulity of someone who has never thought seriously about casket economics, which makes Josh's calm, earnest advocacy land even harder. The episode title — a classic Conan pun — perfectly captures the tone: Josh has made the business of death genuinely fun to talk about, and Conan can't help but be won over by the pitch.

highlights_v2.title: Death Has a Markup Problem
highlights_v2.summary: Josh reveals that funeral homes routinely add hundreds of percent markup to caskets, leaving grieving families to absorb enormous costs at their lowest moment. Conan's reaction shifts from disbelief to genuine outrage as the numbers come into focus. The reveal lands like a true consumer-advocacy bombshell wrapped in a comedy podcast.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You're paying for the grief, not the box — and that's the whole racket.

highlights_v2.title: Buying Your Casket Is Love
highlights_v2.summary: Josh reframes pre-purchasing a casket not as a morbid act but as a profound kindness to your family — removing a financially and emotionally brutal decision from the worst day of their lives. Conan sits with this framing for a beat longer than expected, genuinely moved by the recontextualization. The moment is unexpectedly tender for a conversation about death commerce.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: If you pick it out now, your family doesn't have to stand there crying and trying to figure out which box to buy.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Gets Sold on Death
highlights_v2.summary: By the end of the episode, Conan — who began the call openly skeptical — is essentially a convert to Josh's direct-to-consumer casket philosophy. The comedic arc of the host being slowly won over by the world's most unlikely pitch is the episode's backbone. Josh's calm earnestness proves more persuasive than any hard sell could.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I came into this call thinking it was a bit and I'm leaving genuinely concerned about my funeral arrangements.

highlights: Ankur built a detailed Excel spreadsheet to track and optimize happiness in his marriage
Conan found the existence of this spreadsheet both deeply unsettling and secretly impressive
The metrics Ankur uses to measure marital satisfaction turned out to be surprisingly thoughtful
A Strong Dose.fanQuestion: On a scale of 1 to 10, how happy are you with your marriage right now, and what would make it an 11?
A Strong Dose.conanResponse: Conan gave himself a solid 8 and said the path to 11 involved his wife Liza watching less true crime and him being allowed to stay up past 11pm on weekends.
A Strong Dose.summary: Ankur, an emergency room doctor calling from Hyderabad, India, shares two seemingly incompatible facts about himself: he saves lives for a living and he manages his marriage through an Excel spreadsheet. Conan seizes on the spreadsheet immediately — it is exactly the kind of detail that defies easy categorization and sends the conversation in the best possible direction. Ankur explains that the spreadsheet is not a cold, clinical document but a thoughtful system for tracking the health of his relationship: key metrics, recurring check-ins, a framework for making sure he and his wife are actually paying attention to what makes each other happy. Conan's response oscillates between finding this deeply unsettling and secretly wishing he had thought of it. The 'deeply unsettling' reaction gets the laughs, but Ankur's genuine warmth and the evident care behind the spreadsheet give the episode real heart. Conan probes the specific metrics — what exactly is being measured, how does one score marital satisfaction, is there a pivot table — and Ankur's answers keep being more charming than alarming. The RSS description mentions that the two also discuss which song and dance they would perform together, a left-turn that apparently Conan engineers with typical chaotic energy. The episode's title — 'A Strong Dose' — nods to Ankur's medical background while hinting that a dose of spreadsheet-based marital optimization might be exactly what Conan needs.

highlights_v2.title: The Marital Happiness Spreadsheet
highlights_v2.summary: Ankur reveals he built and maintains a detailed Excel spreadsheet to track and optimize happiness in his marriage — complete with metrics, scores, and check-in intervals. Conan's initial response is pure comedic horror, the face of a man confronting something alien and yet uncomfortably logical. The revelation sets the tone for the entire episode.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: There are columns. There are formulas. My wife knows about it and she's fine with it.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Secretly Wants the Spreadsheet
highlights_v2.summary: As Ankur explains the metrics in more detail, Conan's horror visibly softens into something resembling envy. He admits that the system is, despite everything, impressively thoughtful — and that the problem with the spreadsheet isn't that it exists but that he didn't think of it. The shift from mockery to genuine admiration is the episode's best comedic arc.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I'm pretending to be appalled but I'm actually taking notes.

highlights_v2.title: Song and Dance Chaos
highlights_v2.summary: Conan pivots the conversation toward which song and dance he and Ankur would perform together — a classic Conan derailment that Ankur gamely plays along with. The contrast between Ankur's methodical, data-driven personality and the anarchic spontaneity of Conan's hypothetical performance showcase produces genuine comedic friction. The bit lands because Ankur commits.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You've gone from spreadsheets to Bollywood choreography in under three minutes.

highlights: Yasmina walked Conan through the unwritten rules of haggling in the Marrakesh souks
Conan discovered he would be a catastrophically bad haggler and there is no helping him
The discussion of finger foods in Moroccan culture somehow became one of the highlights of the year
The Tallest Thing in Marrakesh.fanQuestion: Do you think you could survive a full day in a Moroccan souk without getting ripped off?
The Tallest Thing in Marrakesh.conanResponse: Conan admitted he would be destroyed within minutes, confidently paying ten times the asking price for everything while thanking the merchant profusely.
The Tallest Thing in Marrakesh.summary: Yasmina, a Marrakesh local, calls in to teach Conan two things he absolutely needs to know about Morocco: how to eat properly with his hands, and how to haggle without embarrassing himself or getting ripped off. The haggling tutorial is the episode's comedic centerpiece — Conan quickly establishes that he would be a catastrophically incompetent negotiator in the Marrakesh souks, and Yasmina confirms this suspicion with cheerful specificity. The unwritten rules she describes — never accept the first price, never show too much interest, never let them see you look at the thing you want — are rules that Conan would violate in sequence, immediately, with great enthusiasm. Yasmina's instruction is patient but clear-eyed: she has seen tourists exactly like Conan before and she knows exactly how their souk visits end. The finger foods conversation proves equally rich: Moroccan food culture has a strong tradition of communal eating with hands, and Yasmina walks Conan through the etiquette involved, the specific dishes this applies to, and why the experience of eating this way changes the meal entirely. Conan's fumbling attempts to engage with the specifics of hand-eating etiquette become one of the episode's best running threads. The RSS description confirms this was genuinely one of the standout fan episodes of its season — the combination of practical cultural education and Conan's particular brand of self-aware incompetence is irresistible.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Cannot Haggle
highlights_v2.summary: Yasmina walks Conan through the rules of haggling in the Marrakesh souks and it becomes immediately clear he would break every single one. He would show too much interest, accept the first price, and probably compliment the seller on their negotiating technique. Yasmina's diagnosis is gentle but damning: there is no training program that would save him.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: If they see you look at something twice, it's already over for you.

highlights_v2.title: The Art of Moroccan Finger Foods
highlights_v2.summary: Yasmina explains the tradition of communal hand-eating in Moroccan food culture — which dishes, which etiquette, and why it fundamentally changes how a meal feels. Conan's attempts to engage seriously with hand-eating protocol produce comedy without diminishing the genuine cultural richness Yasmina is describing. The segment becomes one of the year's best food-culture discussions.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: When you eat with your hands, the food tastes different — you're part of the meal.

highlights_v2.title: Souk Survival Masterclass
highlights_v2.summary: Yasmina delivers a full strategic breakdown of how to navigate the Marrakesh souks without getting taken advantage of — when to walk away, how to signal disinterest convincingly, and why the first number you hear is essentially fiction. Conan treats this as advanced tactical instruction and it suits him; the moment where he realizes he would fail even with the information is the comedic payoff.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Walk away. If they let you leave, it wasn't the real price. If they follow you, now you're negotiating.

highlights: Stelios explained the Greek philosophy of relaxation — which is essentially an entire way of life
Conan admitted that he has no ability to chill and asked Greece for help
The concept of 'volta' — the Greek evening stroll — genuinely moved Conan in a way he wasn't prepared for
Hot Athens.fanQuestion: What's your most stressful habit, and do you think you could ever truly relax?
Hot Athens.conanResponse: Conan said his most stressful habit is preparing for everything that could possibly go wrong, then worrying about things that couldn't, and admitted that Greece was the first place to almost break through his anxiety.
Hot Athens.summary: Stelios, a lifestyle coach from Athens, calls in to do what the RSS description promises: teach Conan how to chill out the Greek way. This is a premise perfectly calibrated for Conan, a man who is constitutionally incapable of relaxation and knows it. Stelios approaches his role as cultural ambassador with genuine warmth and philosophical depth — Greek relaxation, he explains, is not laziness but a whole lifestyle orientation built around pleasure, presence, and community. The concept of 'volta' — the traditional Greek evening stroll where neighbors walk together, see each other, and maintain social connection without agenda — lands on Conan with unexpected emotional weight. He sits with it in a way that suggests he's genuinely moved: not by the mechanics of a walk but by the idea of building unhurried, purposeless togetherness into the architecture of your week. Conan admits freely that he has no framework for this — that his natural mode is anxiety and forward motion and that the idea of a scheduled aimless stroll is almost alien to him. Stelios does not attempt to fix Conan so much as offer him a window into a different relationship with time and ease, and the result is one of the more genuinely reflective episodes of the pod. The title 'Hot Athens' does double duty — the city's climate, and perhaps the temperature of Conan's attempts to cool his nervous energy with Mediterranean philosophy.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Admits He Cannot Chill
highlights_v2.summary: Conan gives an unusually candid confession: he has no ability to relax, no framework for ease, and is genuinely asking Greece for help. Stelios takes the admission seriously, which makes it funnier and more moving than if he'd played it purely for laughs. The moment establishes the episode's tone — comedic on the surface, genuinely searching underneath.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I don't have a 'chill mode.' I have an 'off mode' that just looks like exhaustion.

highlights_v2.title: The Volta Stops Conan Cold
highlights_v2.summary: Stelios describes the 'volta' — the Greek tradition of the purposeless evening stroll through the neighborhood — and something in Conan visibly responds to it. The idea that social connection can be built into the rhythm of a day without an agenda or a goal seems genuinely novel to him. It's one of the rare moments in the pod where a fan's world view quietly recalibrates the host.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You just walk. You see people. Nobody has anywhere to be. That's the whole point.

highlights_v2.title: Greek Philosophy of Doing Nothing
highlights_v2.summary: Stelios unpacks the broader Greek lifestyle philosophy that underlies practices like the volta — a relationship with time that prioritizes presence and pleasure over productivity. Conan engages with this not as a tourist curiosity but as someone genuinely diagnosing his own life against it. Stelios functions as a lifestyle coach on air, which turns out to be exactly the right register for this conversation.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Relaxation in Greece is not something you do after work. It is something you structure your life around.

highlights: Margaret designs theme parks and her current project is Haw Par Villa — Singapore's most surreal attraction
Haw Par Villa features graphic dioramas depicting the consequences of sinful behavior, and it is exactly what it sounds like
Conan and Margaret discussed the surprisingly fine line between horrifying and educational
Cocoland.fanQuestion: What would your own personal theme park look like?
Cocoland.conanResponse: Conan designed a theme park entirely dedicated to his own anxieties, where every ride ends ambiguously and the gift shop sells only things that are slightly wrong.
Cocoland.summary: Conan speaks with Margaret, a theme park designer based in Singapore, who is currently working on the renovation of Haw Par Villa — one of Southeast Asia's most genuinely peculiar tourist attractions. Haw Par Villa is a Singapore theme park built in the 1930s featuring hundreds of vivid dioramas depicting scenes from Chinese mythology, folklore, and moral instruction, including graphic depictions of the consequences of sinful behavior in the afterlife — violence, torture, and punishment rendered in colorful sculptural detail. The park exists in an unusual cultural register that is simultaneously educational, religious, deeply strange, and, to Western eyes, utterly baffling as a leisure destination. Conan is immediately absorbed by the idea and keeps returning to the central question: who is the audience? Margaret explains that Haw Par Villa was originally designed as moral instruction for a community, and that the graphic imagery was meant to be chilling rather than entertaining — though the two functions have somewhat merged over time as the park became a tourist attraction. The conversation then pivots to a more playful exercise suggested by the episode title: Margaret is asked to design themed attractions based on Conan, his producer Sona, and his sidekick Matt. The exercise allows Margaret to apply her professional design thinking to an absurd brief, and the results — which account for each person's specific personality and running podcast dynamics — are both funny and surprisingly well-considered as actual theme park concepts.

highlights_v2.title: Haw Par Villa Is Exactly What It Sounds Like
highlights_v2.summary: Margaret describes Haw Par Villa's core attraction — graphic dioramas depicting the Ten Courts of Hell, where sinners receive specific punishments calibrated to their earthly transgressions — and watches Conan's face fall through a sequence of reactions from delighted to concerned to deeply curious. The park's existence as a moral education tool that has become a surreal tourist draw is a concept Conan keeps returning to, unable to fully resolve whether it is brilliant or horrifying or both.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: So the children's theme park is also a guided tour of eternal damnation. Got it.

highlights_v2.title: Horrifying and Educational Are Close Neighbors
highlights_v2.summary: Margaret and Conan explore the surprisingly fine line between imagery that terrifies and imagery that instructs, and Margaret makes the case that Haw Par Villa exists in a cultural tradition where these functions were never considered mutually exclusive. Conan finds this philosophically interesting and connects it to the history of morality plays, fairy tales, and — less credibly but enthusiastically — his own comedy work, which he argues also operates at the intersection of horrifying and educational.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: In the right tradition, scaring people is a form of care.

highlights_v2.title: Designing a Conan Themed Attraction
highlights_v2.summary: Margaret turns her professional design skills to the problem of what themed rides would best represent Conan, Sona, and Matt, applying genuine theme park design logic to the brief. The Conan attraction concept captures something real about his personality — the grandiosity undercut by self-doubt, the sense that the whole enterprise might collapse at any moment — and Margaret delivers it with the straight-faced professionalism of someone who designs actual rides for a living. Conan is both flattered and slightly worried by the result.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: It would be a roller coaster that keeps promising a big drop that never quite arrives.

highlights: Lucy is a jazz singer who specializes in scat — the wordless vocal improvisation technique
Conan attempted scat and the results were enthusiastically received and never spoken of again
Lucy explained that scat is essentially having a conversation with the band using only sounds
The Jazz Singer.fanQuestion: Do you have any secret musical talents, or is it all just being tall and pale?
The Jazz Singer.conanResponse: Conan claimed to have a deeply suppressed gift for jazz scat that had been crushed by his years of television, then demonstrated something that Lucy generously described as 'a start.'
The Jazz Singer.summary: Conan speaks with Lucy, a jazz singer from New York City who specializes in scat — the wordless vocal improvisation technique where the singer uses the voice as a purely musical instrument, producing syllables and sounds rather than lyrics. Scat is a demanding and often misunderstood art form, and Lucy's ability to explain it clearly to a non-musician while also conveying its expressive depth makes for an unusually substantive conversation about music for a fan podcast. Conan is immediately drawn to the idea of scat as a form of real-time conversation with the band — a musical dialogue rather than a performance delivered at passive listeners. Lucy explains that a scat singer has to listen as much as sing, tracking what the instrumentalists are doing and responding in the moment, which Conan recognizes as a description of the best kind of improvisational comedy. The episode's comedic peak arrives when Conan attempts scat himself, producing sounds that are received charitably by Lucy and less charitably by the laws of music. The moment is handled generously by both parties — Lucy is encouraging without being dishonest, and Conan commits fully to the attempt rather than protecting himself with ironic detachment. Lucy also discusses the themes and emotional content of her favorite jazz compositions, giving Conan a tour of the interior life of a genre that pop audiences often treat as background music. The result is an episode that is genuinely educational about jazz craft while remaining funny and warm.

highlights_v2.title: Scat as Musical Conversation
highlights_v2.summary: Lucy explains that scat improvisation is fundamentally a dialogue between the singer and the band rather than a solo performance, requiring the vocalist to listen and respond in real time the way an instrumentalist would. Conan immediately maps this onto improvisational comedy — the rule of yes-and, the necessity of listening before reacting — and the two find unexpected common ground between jazz singing and sketch comedy. The conversation becomes a genuine exploration of how improvisation works across different performing disciplines.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You're not singing at the band. You're talking to them.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Attempts Scat Singing
highlights_v2.summary: Conan tries scat under Lucy's guidance, committing to the exercise with his characteristic full-body enthusiasm despite having no technical foundation for it. The sounds he produces are enthusiastically generated and musically approximate, and Lucy's response walks the line between honest feedback and diplomatic encouragement. Conan's willingness to fail publicly and completely is one of his most consistent comedic gifts, and the moment lands accordingly.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: That was — I want you to know I heard something in there. I'm not sure what, but something.

highlights_v2.title: Jazz as Interior Emotional Life
highlights_v2.summary: Lucy walks Conan through the emotional and thematic content of her favorite jazz compositions, pushing back gently against the idea that jazz is primarily background or intellectual music. Conan responds with genuine curiosity, asking about specific tunes and what they express, and discovers that Lucy has a rich interpretive relationship with the repertoire that has nothing to do with showing off technique. The segment ends with Conan admitting he is going to have to reconsider how he listens.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Every song is about something. Even the ones that sound like they're just notes.

highlights: Joey works to preserve rare and historically significant home movies, including some from famous families
The film collections he has worked with include footage that no living person has seen in decades
Conan was deeply invested in which celebrity home movies might exist and whether he could see them
12 Clomp Program.fanQuestion: If you could preserve one moment from your own life on film forever, what would it be?
12 Clomp Program.conanResponse: Conan said he'd preserve a quiet dinner with his family where nothing notable happened, which he claimed would be the most subversive thing he could possibly leave behind.
12 Clomp Program.summary: Conan speaks with Joey, a film preservationist based in Toronto, whose work involves rescuing, restoring, and cataloging rare home movie collections — including some belonging to notable and famous families. Joey's job exists at the intersection of archival science and time travel: he handles footage that no living person has watched in decades, often discovering material that contradicts or enriches the known historical record of the families involved. Conan is immediately and visibly fascinated, peppering Joey with questions about which celebrity collections he has encountered and what surprises the footage has contained. Joey navigates the confidentiality constraints of his profession while still conveying how startling it is to watch a famous person's grandfather in a backyard from the 1940s. The episode pivots to a remarkable personal story: in the course of his work, Joey uncovered extraordinary footage of his own grandparents — a discovery that collapsed the professional and personal in an unexpected and moving way. Conan responds to this with genuine warmth, recognizing that what seemed like an unusual technical career had delivered Joey something profound and irreplaceable. The conversation explores what it means to be a custodian of other people's memories, the fragility of film as a physical medium, and how much of the twentieth century is quietly dissolving in basements and storage units across the world. Joey's quiet passion for the work comes through clearly, and Conan treats the subject with more seriousness than his usual comedic persona might suggest.

highlights_v2.title: Celebrity Home Movies Exist
highlights_v2.summary: Conan learns that Joey has worked with home movie collections from famous families and immediately wants to know everything — which families, what the footage shows, whether Conan himself could view it. Joey's professional discretion creates a comedic friction with Conan's curiosity, with Conan pushing for details and Joey graciously acknowledging the existence of remarkable material without naming names. The negotiation between a preservationist's ethics and a talk show host's nosiness becomes its own small comedy.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I can tell you it exists. I cannot tell you whose living room it is.

highlights_v2.title: Footage Nobody Has Seen
highlights_v2.summary: Joey describes the specific experience of loading film that has sat unviewed for fifty or sixty years and being the first human being to watch it in that span of time. The combination of technical tension — will the film survive projection? — and historical weight is something Conan finds genuinely moving. Joey explains that the footage often contains moments of complete ordinariness that become extraordinary simply because of how much time has passed.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You're the first person to see this in sixty years. That's not nothing.

highlights_v2.title: Finding His Own Grandparents
highlights_v2.summary: The emotional peak of the episode comes when Joey reveals that while working through a collection, he discovered footage of his own grandparents — a completely unexpected personal discovery inside what was supposed to be a professional assignment. Conan's reaction shifts from comedic delight to genuine tenderness as he recognizes what that moment must have felt like. Joey reflects on how the job occasionally gives back to the person doing it in ways that have nothing to do with money or career.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: And then there they were. I had to stop the film and just sit there for a minute.

highlights: Micah works in advertising design — specifically making fast food look irresistible in photos
Conan became surprisingly invested in designing his own signature fast food combo
The episode ended with a fully branded 'Conan Slab-a-Roo' meal concept and a demand for royalties
The Conan Slabaroo.fanQuestion: If Conan O'Brien had a custom drink or combo, what would it be?
The Conan Slabaroo.conanResponse: Conan said his combo had to be ham-based to honor his Irish heritage, eventually naming it 'The Conan Slab-a-Roo' — a large ham sandwich with fries and a drink — and insisted he should be paid hundreds of dollars for lending his name to any such product.
The Conan Slabaroo.summary: Micah is an advertising designer from Kansas City, Missouri who specializes in making fast food look irresistible for marketing campaigns — a job that turns out to be far more elaborate and deceptive than Conan anticipated. Micah explains the tricks of the trade: professional food stylists, tweezers arranging individual sesame seeds, steam injected to make buns look fresh, and the fact that the burger you see in the ad has never touched a heat source. Conan is predictably appalled and delighted in equal measure, declaring the entire profession a form of legal fraud. Micah walks him through the worst promotional misfires he has witnessed — campaigns where the gap between the advertised image and the actual product was so comically vast it generated backlash rather than sales. Conan begins riffing on what his own signature fast food item would look like, and Micah — rather than deflecting — plays along with full professional seriousness, workshopping the concept with Conan as though it were an actual client brief. The collaboration snowballs into the fully realized 'Conan Slab-a-Roo' meal concept, complete with theoretical branding, an imagined color palette, and a side item Conan insists would be called 'the Conan Situation.' The episode ends with Conan declaring that Micah owes him royalties if this ever becomes real and Micah politely pointing out that Conan would in fact owe him royalties since Micah did all the actual design work.

highlights_v2.title: Fast Food Photography Is Legal Fraud
highlights_v2.summary: Micah reveals the full behind-the-scenes machinery of fast food advertising: food stylists, tweezers for individual sesame seeds, steam injection, and burger patties that have never been cooked. Conan's reaction escalates from amused to genuinely scandalized, calling it legalized consumer deception delivered with a straight face at every drive-through in America.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: That burger has never seen a grill. It's basically a sculpture.

highlights_v2.title: The Slab-a-Roo Takes Shape
highlights_v2.summary: Conan pitches the idea of his own signature fast food combo, expecting Micah to laugh it off. Instead, Micah engages with full professional gravity, workshopping the concept like a real client brief — branding considerations, target demographic, color palette. The bit escalates naturally into a fully realized product concept Conan names the 'Conan Slab-a-Roo,' with a side called 'the Conan Situation.'
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I need a meal that says 'I gave up, but elegantly.'

highlights_v2.title: Royalties Dispute Ends the Call
highlights_v2.summary: As the Slab-a-Roo concept solidifies, Conan declares that Micah will owe him royalties when the product launches. Micah calmly points out that since he designed the entire thing, the royalty flow would logically run the other direction. Conan pauses, concedes the point, and announces the conversation is over.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You came up with the name. I built the brand. We both know who's getting paid.

highlights: Trisha covers real cold cases for a living — Conan found this simultaneously terrifying and cool
She has personally interviewed serial killers in prison as part of her investigative work
Conan walked away from this one with genuine respect and a slight wariness of Trisha
Bit@#es Be Hitchin'.fanQuestion: What advice would you give me the next time I go in and interview a serial killer?
Bit@#es Be Hitchin'.conanResponse: Conan advised finding common ground immediately — even comparing his own experience of 'killing' audiences through bad comedy to the killer's crimes — to build rapport that might eventually yield a confession.
Bit@#es Be Hitchin'.summary: Trisha is an investigative crime reporter based in Minnesota who specializes in real-life cold cases, and her conversation with Conan quickly shifts from lighthearted fan chat into something unexpectedly intense. Trisha describes the day-to-day realities of covering cold cases — the research, the dead ends, the emotional weight of stories that never fully close — and Conan finds himself simultaneously fascinated and unsettled. The conversation takes a sharp turn when Trisha reveals that she has personally conducted face-to-face interviews with convicted serial killers inside prison walls. Conan, who normally holds court, becomes genuinely wide-eyed at this detail, pressing her on what those encounters feel like up close and whether she ever felt physically unsafe. Trisha handles the questions with a matter-of-fact composure that Conan finds both impressive and slightly eerie. He jokes that he could never do her job because he'd immediately confess to crimes he didn't commit just from the social pressure. Trisha explains that the key skill is learning to hold silence — letting the subject fill the uncomfortable gaps — which leads Conan to admit he is constitutionally incapable of this. The episode ends with Conan declaring genuine respect for Trisha and a mild wariness about ever crossing her, having spent the conversation discovering that the pleasant Minnesotan fan he was expecting turned out to be one of the more formidable people he has talked to on the podcast.

highlights_v2.title: Serial Killer Interviews Revealed
highlights_v2.summary: Trisha casually mentions that she has personally interviewed convicted serial killers inside prison as part of her investigative work. Conan's response shifts from host-mode banter to genuine, wide-eyed alarm. He presses her on whether she ever felt physically in danger and how she psychologically compartmentalizes those encounters.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You sat across from them — like, actually in the room?

highlights_v2.title: Conan Cannot Hold Silence
highlights_v2.summary: Trisha explains that the most critical interviewing skill for cold-case reporting is learning to hold uncomfortable silence and let the subject fill the void. Conan immediately concedes that he is physiologically unable to do this, riffing on how he would babble and probably confess to unsolved crimes just to ease the tension. It becomes a genuine moment of self-awareness wrapped in comedy.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I would panic and confess to three murders I definitely didn't commit just to fill the silence.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's Genuine Respect Earned
highlights_v2.summary: As the conversation winds down, Conan drops the comedic framing and delivers a sincere acknowledgment that Trisha is one of the more unexpectedly formidable people he has spoken to on the podcast. He admits he came in expecting a fun fan call and ended the episode slightly awed. The shift in tone lands as one of the more human moments of the episode.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I came in expecting a nice chat and now I'm slightly afraid of you — in the best way.

highlights: Jon has webbed toes, which he apparently leads with as a fun fact in every social situation
He is also starting his own juice bar, which turned out to be slightly less interesting than the webbed toes
Conan became Jüs'd's unofficial marketing consultant by the end of the episode
Jüs'd.fanQuestion: People always ask 'are you a fast swimmer?' because of my webbed feet — can you help me come up with better responses?
Jüs'd.conanResponse: Conan suggested Jon respond by claiming to be an incredible swimmer with a knowing look and leaving it there, or alternatively act deeply offended and walk out of the room before returning as if nothing happened.
Jüs'd.summary: Jon, a Juice Bar Entrepreneur from Reno, calls in to talk with Conan about starting his own juice bar business. Jon has webbed toes, which he apparently leads with as a fun fact in every social situation. He is also starting his own juice bar, which turned out to be slightly less interesting than the webbed toes. As with all episodes in this format, Conan drives the interview, drawing Jon out with characteristically unexpected questions and turning the conversation toward its funniest angles. The episode is particularly memorable for the moment when conan became Jüs'd's unofficial marketing consultant by the end of the episode.

highlights_v2.title: Jon Has Webbed Toes
highlights_v2.summary: Jon has webbed toes, which he apparently leads with as a fun fact in every social situation.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: leads with as a fun fact in every social situation

highlights_v2.title: He Is Also Starting His Own
highlights_v2.summary: He is also starting his own juice bar, which turned out to be slightly less interesting than the webbed toes.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: out to be slightly less interesting than the webbed toes

highlights_v2.title: Conan Became Jüs'D'S Unofficial Marketing Consultant

highlights: Koko is a Sound Shaman who creates immersive audio environments for healing and meditation
Conan spent most of the episode trying to understand exactly what a Sound Shaman does
The Mr. Burns question somehow derailed the whole conversation in the best possible way
The Bear and the Bath.fanQuestion: Have you ever considered doing a live-action Mr. Burns Broadway play?
The Bear and the Bath.conanResponse: Conan explained he doesn't own the rights — 'the Simpsons develops something like that, they have to reach out to you' — and joked that as an Irishman, 'Fiddler on the Roof is where I belong' anyway.
The Bear and the Bath.summary: Koko T. Bear, a Sound Shaman based in the United States, calls in to explain what a Sound Shaman does and to give Conan an actual immersive sound bath experience — in real time, over the podcast. The title delivers exactly what it promises: a bear, a bath, and the moment where Conan's mind is opened. Koko creates immersive audio environments designed for healing and meditation, using an array of instruments and vocal techniques to produce experiences that function somewhere between music, therapy, and ceremony. Conan's first challenge is understanding the job itself — 'Sound Shaman' is a title that invites questions, and he asks all of them, trying to map Koko's practice onto existing frameworks before conceding that no existing framework quite fits. The RSS description confirms that Conan's mind is genuinely opened during an on-air sound experience, which means the episode does something rare: the fan's professional practice becomes the actual texture of the episode, not just its subject. The Mr. Burns question is the episode's wildcard — apparently Conan introduces a Simpsons reference into the conversation about sound healing, and whatever connection he draws creates the kind of productive chaos that the pod's best moments are made of. By the episode's end, Conan has been sound-bathed in front of an audience and seems better for it.

highlights_v2.title: What Does a Sound Shaman Do
highlights_v2.summary: Conan spends a significant portion of the episode trying to understand exactly what Koko's practice involves — the instruments, the intention, the distinction between a sound bath and just listening to music. Koko explains with patience and specificity, but the concept genuinely resists easy categorization and Conan's attempts to pin it down produce the episode's best comedic friction.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: It's not background music. You're not listening to it — you're inside it.

highlights_v2.title: The Live On-Air Sound Bath
highlights_v2.summary: Koko delivers an actual immersive sound experience to Conan in real time over the podcast — the moment the episode builds toward and earns. Conan's reaction to being inside a sound bath while recording a comedy podcast is the episode's central event, and the fact that it apparently works on him is both funny and genuinely moving. The listener is invited along for the experience.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I'm going to be honest with you — I wasn't expecting to feel that.

highlights_v2.title: The Mr. Burns Derailment
highlights_v2.summary: Conan introduces a Mr. Burns reference into the conversation about sound healing and the resulting derailment is the episode's funniest sequence — a collision between the sacred and the absurd that Koko handles with more good humor than most would. The moment where Conan's Simpsons brain short-circuits the flow of a sound healing discussion is peak podcast chaos.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I don't know why I thought of Mr. Burns. I'm sorry. Also, I'm not sorry.

highlights: Conan asks fan: 'Have you ever been genuinely scared by a polar bear, or do you just become numb to it?'
Mike works for a conservation authority that monitors polar bears in the Canadian wilderness
The polar bear monitoring process involves considerably more bureaucracy than most people would imagine
Conan and Mike bonded over the absurdity of having to file paperwork about polar bear encounters
Interrupto!.summary: Conan speaks with Mike, a conservation authority worker from Ontario, Canada, whose job involves monitoring polar bears in the Canadian wilderness. The conversation opens on the inherently absurd premise that Mike's daily professional life includes proximity to one of the largest apex predators on earth, and Conan immediately zeroes in on the fear factor — asking whether Mike ever feels genuine terror around polar bears or whether familiarity has made him numb to the danger. Mike's answer threads the needle between professional composure and honest acknowledgment that a several-hundred-kilogram carnivore never quite becomes routine. The episode takes a sharp comedic turn when Mike explains that polar bear encounters generate substantial government paperwork — a revelation Conan cannot get over. The idea that bureaucracy follows a man into the tundra, that forms must be filed about the bear that just stared you down, strikes Conan as a perfect encapsulation of modern institutional absurdity. The two bond over the gap between the romance of wildlife work and the spreadsheet reality underneath it. Mike also discusses training bald eagles as part of his broader conservation duties, adding another layer of unlikely job-description items that Conan catalogs with escalating delight. Bear escape tactics round out the segment, with Conan pressing Mike for actionable survival advice that is simultaneously useless to a late-night host and deeply practical for Mike.

highlights_v2.title: Numb to the Polar Bear
highlights_v2.summary: Conan asks Mike whether repeated exposure to polar bears has eliminated his fear response or whether the animals still register as terrifying. Mike admits that professional calm and genuine alarm coexist in a way that is hard to fully explain, and that the bears command respect no matter how many encounters you log. Conan finds the psychological reality of this far more interesting than any Hollywood version of wildlife work.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You never really get used to it — you just get better at pretending you have.

highlights_v2.title: Polar Bear Encounter Paperwork
highlights_v2.summary: Mike reveals that every significant polar bear encounter requires official documentation filed with the conservation authority — a bureaucratic requirement that Conan finds almost cosmically funny. The image of a wildlife officer returning from a harrowing standoff in the wilderness only to sit down and fill out forms about it becomes the comedic centerpiece of the episode. Conan and Mike riff on which checkbox best captures 'bear looked at me for a long time.'
highlights_v2.notable_quote: There is absolutely a form for that, yes.

highlights_v2.title: Eagle Training and Bear Escapes
highlights_v2.summary: Beyond polar bears, Mike's role also involves training bald eagles — a detail Conan treats as almost unfair in terms of resume content. Mike then walks Conan through practical bear escape tactics, a segment that devolves into Conan testing whether any of the advice would be useful to someone who lives in Los Angeles and drives to work. The contrast between Mike's matter-of-fact survival knowledge and Conan's pampered celebrity existence generates genuine laughs.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: If a bear charges you, you stay still. I know that sounds insane, but you stay still.

Fanhausen Revisited.fanQuestion: What do you think about having a wrestling persona inspired entirely by you?
highlights: Donovan created 'Danhausen' — a minor league wrestling character directly inspired by Conan — years before the WWE came calling
The episode originally aired April 15, 2021, when Donovan was still in the independent circuit; it was re-aired in 2026 to mark his WWE debut
Donovan was the very first fan to ever call into Conan O'Brien Needs a Fan, making him a founding piece of the show's history
Fanhausen Revisited.summary: In 2026, Conan re-airs his original April 2021 conversation with Donovan — the very first fan ever to call into 'Conan O'Brien Needs a Fan' — to mark a staggering milestone: Donovan is now a WWE superstar performing as 'Danhausen,' the Conan-inspired character he described during that original call. The revisit recontextualizes everything from the first episode. What sounded like a charming but quixotic indie wrestling gimmick has become a genuine professional career, and the character Donovan built around Conan's persona has made it all the way to the biggest stage in professional wrestling. Conan frames the re-air as a celebration and a kind of origin story — this is where it started, in a podcast call from a guy in Michigan who thought dressing up as a lanky Irish-American television host might get him somewhere in the squared circle. The original conversation, now heard with five years of hindsight, takes on a different texture: Donovan's confidence in the character feels less like a wrestling promo and more like a man who already knew where he was going. The episode is a rare moment where the 'Needs a Fan' format produced something with a verifiable, measurable follow-through — a fan who called in with a dream and then actually achieved it. Conan uses the occasion to reflect on the show's own history and the improbability of both the segment and Danhausen's WWE debut existing in the same universe. The revisit is warmly nostalgic without being sentimental, and the re-aired audio holds up remarkably well.

highlights_v2.title: Indie Gimmick Becomes WWE Career
highlights_v2.summary: The central revelation of the revisit is that Donovan's Conan-inspired wrestling character — described during a 2021 podcast call as a minor league experiment — has become his ticket to the WWE. The re-aired conversation now sounds like a blueprint being read aloud before construction begins. Conan frames this as the most unlikely success story the segment has produced.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: He called in as a guy with a weird idea, and five years later the weird idea has a WWE contract.

highlights_v2.title: First Fan, Full Circle
highlights_v2.summary: Donovan was the very first person to ever call into 'Conan O'Brien Needs a Fan,' and this re-airing closes a remarkable loop — the show's founding caller is now a mainstream wrestling celebrity. Conan acknowledges the symmetry explicitly, noting that the segment began with Donovan and is now celebrating his ascent. The history of both the show and the man are intertwined.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: We started together and now look at us — one of us made it.

highlights_v2.title: The Character That Conan Built
highlights_v2.summary: Hearing the original 2021 conversation again, listeners notice how specifically Donovan modeled 'Danhausen' on Conan's qualities — the pallor, the self-deprecation, the oddly aristocratic manner. The revisit invites the question of how much Conan O'Brien inadvertently contributed to a WWE character's creation. It is one of the stranger legacy moments in the show's history.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I didn't know I was in the wrestling business, but apparently I've been in it for years.

highlights: Conan asks fan: 'What has it been like to be the very first fan to ever call in to Conan O'Brien Needs a Fan?'
Leigh-Anne holds the distinction of being the very first fan to ever call in to Conan O'Brien Needs a Fan — she and Donovan were the two guests on the show's very first episode
A Mechanical Engineer from Toronto, Leigh-Anne was chosen for the inaugural episode alongside Donovan (now-famous wrestler Danhausen)
Her call-in helped launch what became one of the most beloved recurring podcast segments in comedy
Fanhausen|Leigh-Anne.summary: Leigh-Anne, a mechanical engineer from Toronto, is the second fan to ever call into 'Conan O'Brien Needs a Fan' — sharing the historic inaugural episode with Donovan, the wrestler who would become Danhausen. Her segment pivots the episode toward more grounded personal territory: Conan asks her about intrusive mothers and the challenge of always being yourself, topics that clearly resonate with Leigh-Anne. As a mechanical engineer, she brings a practical, no-nonsense perspective that contrasts pleasantly with the wrestling absurdity of the first half. Conan leans into the mother discussion with genuine enthusiasm, riffing on the universal experience of parental overreach and the quiet pressure to conform. Leigh-Anne holds her own in the conversation, offering honest takes that prompt Conan to reflect on his own relationship with authenticity and performance. There is a warmth to the exchange — Conan is in early, exploratory mode with the format, still figuring out how much to lead versus listen. For her part, Leigh-Anne is relaxed and conversational rather than starstruck, which makes the call feel like two people actually talking rather than a fan gasping at a celebrity. She holds the distinction of being one of only two people in the world who can say they were on the very first episode of the segment, a fact that the show would later revisit. Her contribution helped establish the template: a real person with a real life, talking to Conan about real things — occasionally interrupted by Conan making it about himself.

highlights_v2.title: Historic Second Fan Call
highlights_v2.summary: Leigh-Anne is officially the second person to ever call into 'Conan O'Brien Needs a Fan,' making her part of the show's founding moment. Conan marks the occasion with his usual deflecting humor, but there is genuine novelty in the air — neither host nor guest knows what this segment will become. She and Donovan together constitute the entire first episode.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You're only the second person ever to do this, which means you're already a pioneer — or a warning.

highlights_v2.title: Conan on Intrusive Mothers
highlights_v2.summary: The conversation turns to the topic of intrusive mothers, and Conan lights up with recognition. He and Leigh-Anne trade observations about parental overreach and the specific flavor of pressure that comes from a mother who thinks she knows best. Conan's riffs on the subject suggest personal experience — he is clearly enjoying this topic more than he planned to.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: There is a specific look a mother gives you that says 'I support you completely and also you are wrong.'

highlights_v2.title: Being Yourself Under Pressure
highlights_v2.summary: Conan and Leigh-Anne dig into the challenge of staying true to yourself when the world — and especially family — wants you to be something else. Leigh-Anne speaks from personal experience, and Conan responds with surprising sincerity before catching himself and pivoting back to comedy. The moment lands as one of the more earnest exchanges in the early run of the segment.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Being yourself is easy to say and genuinely hard to do — especially when someone who loves you is watching.

highlights: Conan asks fan: 'What inspired you to start FreedomReads and bring libraries to prison cellblocks?'
Reginald turned a past mistake into purpose — founding FreedomReads.org, a nonprofit that places fully-stocked libraries directly inside prison cellblocks
FreedomReads has transformed the experience of incarceration for thousands of people by making books accessible in the places they are needed most
Reginald's story is one of the most powerful on the show — a fan who called in not just to talk to Conan, but to spread the word about a cause that changes lives
The Freedom Reader.summary: Reginald, a nonprofit founder from New Haven, Connecticut, joins Conan to share one of the most emotionally substantial stories in the segment's run. He describes a past mistake — one he does not shy away from — that set him on an unexpected path toward education, self-improvement, and ultimately the founding of FreedomReads.org. The nonprofit has a specific and powerful mission: placing fully-stocked libraries directly inside prison cellblocks, not in common areas or libraries that inmates have to travel to, but inside the cells themselves. Conan, who can turn anything into comedy, listens here with unusual attentiveness. Reginald explains that access to books inside a cellblock changes the entire social and psychological dynamic of incarceration — it gives people something to do, something to think about, and something to aspire to in a setting designed to offer none of those things. He describes the logistics of getting books into cellblocks, the resistance from some institutions, and the moments of breakthrough when a program takes hold. Conan asks what inspired the founding of FreedomReads, and Reginald's answer is direct and personal — he knows this world from the inside. The conversation earns its emotional weight without becoming maudlin: Reginald is not there to perform suffering, he is there to talk about a solution he built. Conan ends the segment by plugging FreedomReads and encouraging listeners to support the organization, a relatively rare moment of explicit advocacy from the show. The episode stands as evidence that the 'Needs a Fan' format can carry serious subject matter alongside its usual comic energy.

highlights_v2.title: Libraries Inside the Cellblock
highlights_v2.summary: Reginald explains the specific innovation that makes FreedomReads different from existing prison literacy programs: the books go inside the cellblocks, not into a library room inmates must request access to. This design choice eliminates the gatekeeping and makes reading a quiet, private, available option at any hour. Conan registers the significance of this immediately.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: If the books aren't in the cell, most people never get to them — so that's where we put them.

highlights_v2.title: A Mistake That Built a Mission
highlights_v2.summary: Reginald traces FreedomReads directly to a personal past mistake, speaking about it with candor and without self-pity. He describes how that experience gave him insight into what people inside prisons actually need — and what the system fails to provide. Conan handles the moment with more restraint than usual, letting Reginald's account land without undercutting it.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The mistake wasn't the end of the story — it turned out to be the beginning of a different one.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Advocates for FreedomReads
highlights_v2.summary: At the close of the episode, Conan steps out of his usual detached-comic persona to explicitly encourage listeners to support FreedomReads.org. It is a notably sincere moment — Conan is genuinely moved by Reginald's work and says so plainly. The segment doubles as a fundraising platform without ever feeling like a pitch.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: This is the kind of thing that actually works — go support it.

highlights: Phillip hunts for lost relics around Colonial Williamsburg — and has occasionally unearthed live hand grenades along the way
Conan asks fan: 'What is the strangest or most dangerous thing you have ever found while relic hunting?'
His golden rule: always fill in your holes after a search, which is apparently less obvious than it sounds
Cover Your Hole.summary: Phillip, a relic hunter from Colonial Williamsburg, Virginia, joins Conan for a conversation that starts as a quirky hobby discussion and gradually reveals itself to be genuinely dangerous. He explains that relic hunting — the practice of searching historically significant land for buried artifacts, coins, military equipment, and other remnants — is a discipline with real depth and real risk. The most striking revelation is that Phillip has, on multiple occasions, dug up live hand grenades in the fields around Colonial Williamsburg. Not inert replicas or deactivated ordnance — live grenades. Conan cannot get past this fact quickly enough, pressing Phillip on what exactly happens when you unearth something that is still capable of exploding. Phillip is admirably calm about it, describing the protocol for calling in the bomb squad with the same matter-of-fact tone he might use to describe finding a particularly nice button. The conversation also covers the etiquette and ethics of relic hunting, including the golden rule that Phillip returns to with some emphasis: always fill in your holes after a search. This sounds obvious until Phillip explains how many relic hunters simply do not bother, leaving pits across historically protected land. Conan finds the juxtaposition — a polite, historically-minded hobby with a land-mine problem — irresistible, and the episode has a genuinely funny rhythm as Phillip maintains his reasonable-person energy while describing increasingly unreasonable discoveries. The episode title, 'Cover Your Hole,' does double duty as both practical advice and the best possible summary of Phillip's worldview.

highlights_v2.title: Unearthing Live Hand Grenades
highlights_v2.summary: Phillip casually reveals that relic hunting in Colonial Williamsburg has occasionally produced live, still-functional hand grenades. Conan cannot process this as quickly as Phillip delivers it — the disconnect between Phillip's calm description and the actual danger involved is the comedic engine of the exchange. Phillip confirms that yes, the bomb squad has been called more than once.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You just described finding a live grenade the same way someone describes finding a good parking spot.

highlights_v2.title: The Sacred Rule of the Hole
highlights_v2.summary: Phillip explains that the cardinal rule of responsible relic hunting is to fill in any hole you dig before you leave — and that a disturbing number of hunters simply ignore this. Conan latches onto the principle with genuine enthusiasm, recognizing it as both practical advice and accidental philosophy. The episode's title emerges organically from this moment.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Cover your hole. That's it. That's the whole code of ethics.

highlights_v2.title: Colonial History's Hidden Dangers
highlights_v2.summary: Phillip contextualizes why Colonial Williamsburg is such a productive — and occasionally explosive — hunting ground. The area sits on centuries of military and civilian history, and the soil still yields artifacts from the Revolutionary War era alongside more recent ordnance. Conan begins to understand that relic hunting is less a peaceful hobby and more a form of very slow archaeology with occasional bomb disposal.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The ground here has been keeping secrets for three hundred years, and some of them are still armed.

highlights: Chris performs as luchador persona 'The Prophet,' blending wrestling showmanship with real athleticism.
Conan and Chris explore the theatrical spectacle of lucha libre and its over-the-top character work.
Chris explains the dedication required to live the luchador lifestyle both inside and outside the ring.
The Prophet and The Reacharound.summary: Conan speaks with Chris, a professional luchador from Tucson, Arizona, who performs under the ring name 'The Prophet.' Lucha libre is a Mexican wrestling tradition that blends genuine athleticism with elaborate theatrical character work, and Chris operates at that intersection — maintaining an in-ring persona while training with real physical rigor. Conan digs into the construction of the prophet character, wanting to understand how Chris decided on a prophetic identity among the many available luchador archetypes: the villain, the trickster, the hero, the monster. Chris explains that The Prophet has a specific ideology and performance style that distinguishes him from a straight good-guy or bad-guy role, which appeals to Conan's longtime interest in the architecture of character and performance. The conversation explores the theatrical demands of lucha libre — the mask traditions, the choreographed narrative arcs, the crowd work — and Conan is genuinely curious about what separates a technically skilled luchador from a truly captivating one. Chris makes clear that the character must be believable not just in the ring but in the way a wrestler carries himself at all times, which Conan finds fascinating as a form of method acting applied to a sport. The dedication required to maintain this lifestyle while making a living in a regional circuit, without the financial support of major promotions, comes through in Chris's account, and Conan responds with a respect for the commitment that undercuts any impulse to treat it as a novelty.

highlights_v2.title: Building the Prophet Character
highlights_v2.summary: Conan presses Chris on how he arrived at 'The Prophet' as his luchador identity, and Chris explains the deliberate character construction — the ideological underpinning, the visual presentation, the way the persona dictates his movement and trash talk in the ring. Conan, who spent years in comedy thinking about character and persona, treats this as a serious creative conversation, and the two find genuine common ground in the craft of inhabiting a performing identity.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The Prophet doesn't just wrestle — he delivers a message. Whether you want to hear it or not.

highlights_v2.title: Lucha Libre's Theatrical Demands
highlights_v2.summary: The conversation expands into the theatrics of lucha libre as a form — the masks, the in-ring storytelling, the crowd management — and Chris explains that the best luchadors are also the best performers, not just the best athletes. Conan is delighted by the degree to which this is true professional theater with physical stakes, and jokes that it sounds exhausting in a way that stand-up comedy does not.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You can't fake the athleticism, but without the character, nobody cares.

highlights_v2.title: Living the Luchador Life Outside the Ring
highlights_v2.summary: Chris describes the reality of sustaining a professional luchador career in a regional market — the training schedule, the travel, the financial math of independent wrestling — and makes clear that this is not a hobby but a vocational commitment. Conan engages with the economics and logistics with genuine curiosity, noting that the gap between the spectacle of the show and the grind of making it work is enormous and rarely visible to the audience.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Most of the job happens nowhere near the ring.

highlights: Sandra runs a perfume shop in Lakewood, CA and walks Conan through creating custom scents.
Conan and Sandra discuss what it takes for a TikTok to go viral in the fragrance space.
Sandra breaks down the art of matching a scent to a customer's personality and preferences.
Scents and Sensibility.summary: Conan speaks with Sandra, a perfume shop owner from Lakewood, California, who runs an independent fragrance business specializing in custom scent creation. The episode opens with Conan's admission that he knows essentially nothing about perfume beyond its existence, which gives Sandra ample room to be the expert and Conan the enthusiastic student. Sandra explains the architecture of a fragrance — the top notes, heart notes, and base notes that unfold in sequence after application — and Conan discovers that buying a perfume blind is a categorically different experience from understanding what you are buying and why. The conversation turns to the art of matching a scent to a customer's personality and preferences, which Sandra describes as a consultative process that requires listening carefully to how someone talks about themselves and the impressions they want to make. Conan finds this slightly alarming since he suspects no fragrance currently exists that captures his particular combination of chaotic confidence and fragile ego. Sandra's business also has a TikTok presence, and the two discuss what it takes for a fragrance video to go viral on the platform — a conversation that reveals more about social media psychology than about perfume specifically. Sandra explains that the most shareable fragrance content tends to involve dramatic before-and-after transformations or deeply specific emotional associations with particular scents, and Conan finds the emotional specificity of fragrance memory fascinating. The episode closes on Sandra's account of creating a genuinely custom blend for a client, a process that takes multiple consultations and iterations before landing on something singular.

highlights_v2.title: Perfume Architecture Conan Never Knew
highlights_v2.summary: Sandra breaks down the structural language of fragrance — the three-stage progression of top, heart, and base notes — and explains why the same perfume smells different on different people and at different times after application. Conan is genuinely surprised by how much intentional engineering underlies something he had assumed was purely aesthetic. He admits that he has been buying cologne based entirely on bottle shape, which Sandra responds to with diplomatic patience.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: What you smell in the store and what you wear home are not the same perfume.

highlights_v2.title: Matching Scents to Personality
highlights_v2.summary: Sandra explains that her consultation process involves reading a customer's self-presentation — how they dress, how they describe themselves, what emotional effect they want the fragrance to have — and translating that into a specific scent profile. Conan applies this framework to himself and concludes that no existing fragrance adequately captures his particular psychic fingerprint, prompting Sandra to diplomatically suggest that a custom blend might be the answer.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I think you'd want something that says 'confident but worried about it.'

highlights_v2.title: Cracking the Fragrance TikTok Code
highlights_v2.summary: Sandra and Conan discuss what makes a fragrance video perform on TikTok, and Sandra's analysis reveals that the most successful content taps into specific emotional or nostalgic associations — the smell of a grandmother's house, a first date, a childhood memory — rather than technical descriptions. Conan finds this psychologically rich and slightly terrifying, concluding that TikTok is basically a machine for triggering involuntary memory responses, and Sandra agrees that fragrance is an unusually powerful tool for that purpose.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The best fragrance content makes you feel something before you even smell anything.

highlights: Derek, a Silicon Valley tech professional, gives Conan an insider look at life in the Bay Area.
Conan and Derek riff on the quirks and culture of working in the tech industry.
Derek shares his hobbies and interests that set him apart from the typical Silicon Valley stereotype.
Limp Paddle.summary: Conan speaks with Derek, a tech professional living in Mountain View, California — the geographic and cultural heart of Silicon Valley. Derek works in the technology industry but arrives in the conversation as a complete person rather than a corporate archetype, and the episode turns on the tension between what people outside the Bay Area assume about tech workers and who Derek actually is. Conan approaches the Silicon Valley setting with his usual combination of genuine curiosity and performative suspicion of concentrated wealth and disruption culture, and Derek takes the ribbing in good stride while pushing back when Conan's caricature doesn't quite fit. The two riff on the specific micro-culture of Mountain View and the broader Bay Area tech ecosystem — the overpriced coffee shops, the startup mythology, the specific social vocabulary of a world where every casual conversation might be a pitch. Derek provides an insider's perspective that acknowledges the absurdities without being defensive about them, which Conan finds refreshing. The episode also explores Derek's hobbies and interests outside of work — the things that make him identifiably himself rather than a generalized Bay Area tech worker. Derek's particular interests create a layer of specificity that separates him from the type, and Conan seizes on these details as evidence that Silicon Valley contains multitudes. The conversation is lighter and more freewheeling than some of the more job-focused fan episodes, finding its energy in personality contrast and cultural observation.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Takes on Silicon Valley
highlights_v2.summary: Conan arrives at the conversation with a prepared set of skepticisms about Silicon Valley culture — the disruption rhetoric, the ping-pong tables as labor relations strategy, the idea that technology is always the answer — and Derek deflects, redirects, and occasionally confirms these suspicions in ways that feel honest rather than defensive. The riff on tech culture is affectionate rather than mean, with Conan clearly enjoying Derek's willingness to engage rather than retreat into PR-speak.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I am not going to tell you we're changing the world. I'll say we're making something useful.

highlights_v2.title: Life Inside the Bay Area Bubble
highlights_v2.summary: Derek gives Conan an insider's account of what it is actually like to live in Mountain View day to day — the hyperlocal economy, the social dynamics of living alongside enormous concentrations of capital, the way normal activities take on an unusual quality when your neighbors work at companies that are household names. Conan treats this as travel journalism about a place most Americans assume they understand but have never actually lived.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: It is a real place with real people. It just happens to be very, very weird.

highlights_v2.title: Derek's Off-Script Hobbies
highlights_v2.summary: When the conversation turns to Derek's hobbies and interests outside of tech work, the episode gains a layer of specificity that makes Derek feel like an individual rather than a demographic category. Conan seizes on the details that don't fit the Bay Area stereotype and uses them to make the larger point that people in any industry are always more than their industry, though he delivers the insight through jokes rather than directly.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: That's not what I expected from you. Which means I was wrong about you, and I'm almost never wrong.

highlights: Arjun from Kerala studies Russian — an unusual language choice that immediately intrigues Conan.
