Conan and Arjun discuss Kerala's rich history as a spice trading hub and cultural crossroads.
Arjun considers whether Conan could actually serve as his wingman in his search for a partner.
Classic Colonizer.summary: Conan speaks with Arjun from Kerala in southern India — a young man who is studying Russian, looking for a romantic partner, and whose home region has a history as one of the most fought-over spice trading hubs in the ancient and colonial world. The Russian language choice immediately intrigues Conan, who wants to know what drives someone in Kerala to pursue a language with essentially no local practical application — and Arjun's answer navigates the space between intellectual curiosity and vague notions of what the language represents. The episode is warm and playful throughout, with Conan adopting the persona of a potential wingman as a running comedic conceit. Arjun is searching for a romantic partner, and Conan appoints himself advisor on this project, offering dating counsel with the full confidence of a man who has never needed to sell himself cold to a stranger. The negotiation over whether Conan would be an effective wingman generates the best comedic energy of the episode — with Arjun considering the proposition seriously before identifying several ways in which Conan's very specific presence might complicate rather than assist the endeavor. Kerala's history as a spice hub provides the episode's most substantive thread: Arjun explains how the region's location at the intersection of ancient trade routes made it a target for successive colonial powers, and how that history has left a layered cultural residue visible in the local architecture, cuisine, and social life. Conan connects this history to the episode title in a moment of self-aware humor about who typically arrives in a new place and immediately starts directing things.

highlights_v2.title: Why Russian in Kerala
highlights_v2.summary: Conan cannot get past the logic of studying Russian in Kerala — a choice that seems deliberately counterintuitive — and presses Arjun to explain the chain of reasoning that led there. Arjun's explanation is both personal and philosophically interesting, touching on what it means to choose a language for its own sake rather than for instrumental reasons. Conan, who has always admired people who pursue things that make no obvious sense, finds this deeply relatable.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Nobody in Kerala needs Russian. That's sort of the point.

highlights_v2.title: Conan as Potential Wingman
highlights_v2.summary: Conan proposes himself as a wingman to assist Arjun's search for a romantic partner, and Arjun considers the offer with a thoughtful seriousness that is itself funny. The negotiation over terms — what Conan would contribute, what risks he might introduce, whether his presence would help or simply dominate the social space — plays as a mock business meeting with genuine comedic stakes. Arjun's polite skepticism is more effective comedy than outright rejection would have been.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You would be an asset. You would also be the only thing anyone is looking at.

highlights_v2.title: Kerala's Spice Route History
highlights_v2.summary: Arjun explains how Kerala's geography made it the destination for successive waves of traders and colonizers drawn by the region's spice exports, and how that history created a layered cultural landscape visible in everyday life. Conan engages seriously with the history before making the self-aware joke embedded in the episode title — acknowledging that arriving somewhere with full confidence and an agenda is a very particular kind of behavior with a very particular historical name.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: So everyone kept showing up and deciding they should be in charge. Classic behavior.

highlights: Yassir from Casablanca schools Conan on the unique menu items at Moroccan McDonald's locations.
Conan and Yassir debate what makes Casablanca's local food culture distinct and worth celebrating.
Yassir shares what life in Morocco's biggest city is actually like for a young local expert.
Play It Again Yassir.summary: Yassir, a local cultural expert calling from Casablanca, Morocco, joins Conan for a conversation that begins with fast food and expands into a genuine portrait of life in one of the world's most storied cities. The episode's most immediately memorable thread is Yassir's rundown of what Moroccan McDonald's serves that no American location would recognize — a culturally specific menu that reflects local tastes and ingredients and that Conan cannot stop interrogating. Conan approaches this with the energy of someone who is both a fast food appreciator and a man who genuinely did not expect Morocco to have interesting McDonald's options. Yassir, playing the role of patient and enthusiastic local guide, navigates Conan's questions with wit and specificity. The conversation then expands into what makes Casablanca's broader food culture distinct: the street food, the family cooking traditions, the way local restaurants function differently from Western equivalents. Yassir is clearly proud of his city and communicates that pride without turning the episode into a travel brochure — the specificity keeps it grounded. The episode title riffs on the classic 'Play It Again, Sam' Casablanca connection, a joke Conan presumably could not resist. By the end, Conan has been given a Casablanca education he did not know he needed, and the listener has too.

highlights_v2.title: Moroccan McDonald's Menu Breakdown
highlights_v2.summary: Yassir walks Conan through the specific menu items available at Moroccan McDonald's that do not exist at American locations — a conversation that starts as a curiosity and becomes a genuine cultural deep-dive. Conan's fast-food knowledge turns out to be both extensive and geographically limited, which Yassir corrects with relish. The specificity of the menu items is what makes the bit work.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You can't get that in America — and once you know about it, you're going to be upset about that.

highlights_v2.title: Defending Casablanca's Food Culture
highlights_v2.summary: Yassir pushes back on any suggestion that Moroccan food is simply an exotic novelty, instead making a detailed case for why Casablanca's local food scene is worth celebrating on its own terms. Conan, who came in with some preconceptions, finds himself persuaded by the specificity and confidence of Yassir's argument. The debate is affectionate and genuinely illuminating.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Casablanca isn't a backdrop — it's a living food city.

highlights_v2.title: Young Local Life in Morocco
highlights_v2.summary: Yassir describes what daily life in Casablanca actually looks like for a young person today — beyond the tourist landmarks and the Humphrey Bogart mythology. Conan's curiosity here is genuine and the questions are good, pulling out details about social life, neighborhoods, and the texture of urban Moroccan existence that don't show up in travel guides.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The city in the movie and the city I live in — they're not really the same place.

highlights: Sid, a cheese innovation expert in Budapest, explains the cutting edge of modern cheese development.
Conan probes Sid on what 'cheese innovation' actually means and which experiments have gone wrong.
The episode features Matt Gourley stepping in as host while Conan takes a back seat.
Matt Gourley Needs A Fan.summary: This episode features a format twist: Matt Gourley, longtime Conan collaborator and podcast producer, steps in as host while Conan cedes the wheel — a reversal that the title announces explicitly and that gives the conversation a meta-comedic layer from the first moment. The fan is Sid, a cheese innovation expert calling from Budapest, Hungary, whose job title alone generates considerable confusion and delight. Gourley, now in the hosting seat, must navigate Sid's world of cutting-edge dairy development with the same exploratory curiosity Conan typically deploys. The central question — what does 'cheese innovation' actually mean — turns out to have a genuinely fascinating answer involving texture experiments, flavor hybridization, alternative milk sources, and the ways food science is pushing fermented dairy into new territory. Gourley probes for the experiments that have gone wrong, which Sid answers with the candor of someone who has learned from real failures. Conan's presence is felt even in his absence; the format inversion works because listeners know what Conan would have said at each turn, and Gourley's different comedic sensibility creates productive contrast. The Budapest setting adds texture — Sid is doing European food innovation work, and the city's own rich culinary heritage makes it an interesting base for someone at the cutting edge of cheese development. The RSS description confirms the cheese development framing as the episode's spine.

highlights_v2.title: What Is Cheese Innovation Exactly
highlights_v2.summary: Sid is asked to define 'cheese innovation' from the ground up and the answer turns out to be genuinely surprising — not just new flavors but texture science, alternative fermentation methods, and dairy-adjacent products that challenge the boundaries of what cheese even means. Gourley asks with the right mix of genuine curiosity and deadpan skepticism to draw out the best specifics.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The category is bigger than you think — and some of it will upset traditionalists.

highlights_v2.title: The Experiments That Went Wrong
highlights_v2.summary: Gourley pushes Sid to describe the cheese innovations that failed — the textures that didn't work, the flavor combinations that were abandoned, the products that made it to tasting and then mercifully disappeared. Sid's willingness to share these failures makes him instantly likeable and the specifics of what 'failed cheese' means in a food science context are unexpectedly compelling.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Some of them we knew immediately. Some of them we knew after the third bite, which was two bites too many.

highlights_v2.title: Gourley Takes the Hosting Wheel
highlights_v2.summary: The episode's structural joke — Matt Gourley hosting instead of Conan — pays off throughout but reaches its peak as Gourley finds his comedic footing and starts driving the conversation with his own style. The comparison to Conan's hosting approach is implicit and productive, revealing how much of the show's personality comes from the host's specific curiosity patterns.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I'm going to ask this the way Conan would ask it, except I'm going to ask it differently.

highlights: Asya recounts the chicken sandwich experience that triggered an unexpected culinary revelation.
Conan and Asya dig into how a single meal can completely reshape someone's relationship with food.
Asya's story takes surprising turns as she unpacks what the sandwich actually led her to discover.
Don't Look A Gift Horse Meat In the Mout.summary: Asya, a culinary enthusiast calling from Kazakhstan, tells Conan about a seemingly ordinary chicken sandwich that detonated something unexpected in her relationship with food. What begins as a food anecdote reveals itself as a story about how a single meal — the right meal, at the right moment — can fundamentally reorganize how you think about eating, flavor, culture, and your own palate. Conan, who approaches food stories with genuine appetite, follows Asya's narrative closely as she unpacks the specific details of the sandwich, the context she was in when she ate it, and what exactly shifted afterward. The 'revelation' turns out to be multifaceted: part discovery of a new flavor dimension, part awakening to how much of Kazakhstani food culture she had been taking for granted, part recognition that food is a lens for understanding the world around her. The episode title is a knowing pun on the phrase 'don't look a gift horse in the mouth' — but in Kazakhstan, where horse meat is a genuine culinary tradition, the wordplay has teeth. The conversation takes surprising turns as Asya unpacks the full scope of what the sandwich unlocked, and Conan's questions push her deeper into the story each time. The topic field describes this as 'how a chicken sandwich led to a revelation' — and the episode earns that framing.

highlights_v2.title: One Sandwich Changes Everything
highlights_v2.summary: Asya describes the specific chicken sandwich experience that cracked open her relationship with food — the context, the taste, the moment where something shifted. Conan is a willing listener for food stories and his questions here are good, pulling the narrative forward without overriding it. The setup earns the reveal.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: It wasn't even a remarkable sandwich. That's the part that stayed with me.

highlights_v2.title: The Revelation Behind the Revelation
highlights_v2.summary: As Asya unpacks what the sandwich actually led her to discover, the conversation expands into Kazakhstani food culture, what she had been overlooking about her own culinary heritage, and how the experience reshaped her relationship with eating more broadly. Conan's curiosity about the specifics of Kazakhstani cuisine gives Asya space to contextualize her story within a larger cultural frame.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I realized I'd been eating the food but not actually understanding it.

highlights_v2.title: A Meal That Reshapes a Person
highlights_v2.summary: Conan and Asya land on a broader conversation about how food functions as a vehicle for identity, memory, and self-understanding — and how a single unexpected meal can reorganize all three. The conversation achieves a surprising philosophical depth without losing the conversational warmth that makes the fan pod work best. The chicken sandwich, by the end, has become a genuinely resonant metaphor.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: A meal can teach you something about yourself you didn't know you needed to learn.

highlights: Tasha, a psychotherapist in Zagreb, gives Conan an inside look at mental health culture in Croatia.
Conan and Tasha discuss what brings people to therapy in Zagreb and how attitudes differ from the US.
Tasha explains what the episode title's 'Zagrebian attitude' actually means to locals.
That Zagrebian 'Tude.summary: Tasha, a psychotherapist practicing in Zagreb, Croatia, joins Conan for a conversation that moves between professional insight and local cultural identity. She begins by giving Conan a genuine window into what the mental health landscape looks like in Croatia — who comes to therapy, what they come for, and how attitudes toward psychological treatment differ from what an American listener would expect. In Croatia, Tasha explains, the stigma around therapy is different in character from American stigma: not necessarily worse, but differently shaped by cultural history, by the legacy of the Yugoslav era, and by a particular kind of emotional self-sufficiency that Croatians cultivate as a source of pride. This is the 'Zagrebian attitude' of the episode title — a local toughness, a certain flavor of urban stoicism that Tasha both diagnoses as a therapist and personally embodies as a Zagrepčanka. Conan is fascinated by the comparative angle — how does what brings people to therapy in Zagreb differ from what brings people to therapy in Los Angeles? — and Tasha's answers are specific enough to be genuinely illuminating. The episode has the texture of the best fan calls: a person with real expertise who is also a vivid personality, talking to Conan about something she knows and loves, in a conversation that leaves the listener feeling like they understand a corner of the world they didn't before.

highlights_v2.title: Therapy Culture in Croatia
highlights_v2.summary: Tasha explains what the mental health landscape in Croatia actually looks like — who seeks therapy, what they come for, and how the cultural legacy of Yugoslavia shapes attitudes toward psychological treatment. The picture is nuanced and specific, neither a simple version of American therapy culture nor its opposite. Conan's comparative questions draw out the most revealing contrasts.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: It's not that people don't struggle — it's that they've been taught that struggling is private.

highlights_v2.title: What Zagreb Brings to the Couch
highlights_v2.summary: Tasha describes the specific emotional themes that show up in Zagreb therapy offices versus what she understands American patients to present with — a comparison Conan drives with genuine curiosity. The cultural specificities Tasha names are both illuminating and funny in their particularity, grounding an abstract subject in lived urban experience.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: In Zagreb, people often come in because something external broke. The idea that you'd come in just to know yourself better — that's still newer here.

highlights_v2.title: The Zagrebian Attitude Decoded
highlights_v2.summary: Tasha unpacks what the episode title actually refers to — the distinctive Zagreb urban personality, characterized by a dry wit, a certain emotional self-sufficiency, and a local pride that borders on gentle chauvinism. As a therapist, she can describe this cultural tendency from the outside; as a Zagrepčanka, she admits she fully embodies it. Conan appreciates the self-awareness.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: We have an attitude, yes. We're aware of it. We consider it a feature.

highlights: Nurzhamal from Kazakhstan quizzes Conan on how much he actually knows about her home country.
Conan stumbles through his limited Kazakhstan knowledge, leading to good-natured corrections from Nurzhamal.
Nurzhamal shares what Kazakhstani cuisine — including horse meat — is really like for locals.
Don't Look A Gift Horse Meat.summary: Nurzhamal, a culinary expert calling from Almaty, Kazakhstan, arrives with a mission: find out how much Conan actually knows about her country. The quiz format is an inspired choice because Conan is simultaneously very good at generating confident-sounding answers and very bad at being correct, a combination that produces the ideal comedic energy for this kind of cultural reckoning. Nurzhamal brings both the questions and the corrective commentary, playing the role of patient educator who is also genuinely amused by the depth of Conan's ignorance. The conversation covers Kazakhstani geography, culture, and — most pointedly — cuisine, which is where the episode title lives. Horse meat is a genuine cornerstone of traditional Kazakhstani food, not an exotic novelty or a provocateur's choice, and Nurzhamal explains this with the matter-of-fact confidence of someone who grew up eating it and considers it utterly ordinary. Conan navigates his own cultural preconceptions about horse meat with more grace than expected, asking genuine questions about preparation, tradition, and what the meat actually tastes like and when people eat it. Nurzhamal's expertise as a culinary professional gives the food discussion a depth that goes beyond shock value. By the end, Conan has had his Kazakhstan knowledge significantly upgraded, and Nurzhamal has successfully represented her country on one of the world's most listened-to comedy podcasts.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's Kazakhstan Knowledge Quiz
highlights_v2.summary: Nurzhamal quizzes Conan on what he actually knows about Kazakhstan and the results are exactly as entertaining as expected — a mix of confident wrongness, lucky guesses, and occasional genuine ignorance that Conan leans into rather than hides. Nurzhamal's corrections are warmly delivered but thorough, and the dynamic of the knowing local correcting the enthusiastic but uninformed host is the episode's best ongoing bit.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: That's almost right. Which means it's also quite wrong.

highlights_v2.title: Horse Meat Is Just Dinner
highlights_v2.summary: Nurzhamal explains that horse meat is not a culinary provocation in Kazakhstan — it is a traditional, everyday food with a history rooted in nomadic culture, specific preparation methods, and ceremonial significance. Conan asks about what it tastes like, when it's eaten, and how it's prepared with genuine curiosity, and Nurzhamal's culinary expertise makes the answers specific and satisfying.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: It's not something we eat to shock you. We eat it because it's good and it's ours.

highlights_v2.title: Almaty Through a Culinary Lens
highlights_v2.summary: Nurzhamal uses food as the frame through which she introduces Conan to Almaty and Kazakhstani culture more broadly — the markets, the family food traditions, the dishes that define the city's culinary identity beyond horse meat. Conan's engagement is genuine and the picture Nurzhamal draws is vivid enough that the listener finishes the episode with a real sense of place.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Almaty has a food culture that most of the world has never heard of. That's something I'd like to change.

highlights: Lucas plays professional basketball in the Netherlands and explains how European leagues differ from the NBA.
Conan and Lucas discuss the reality of being an American pro athlete living and competing abroad.
Lucas breaks down the Dutch basketball scene and what it takes to stand out on a European roster.
Drainin' Treys and Drivin' Hoops.summary: Lucas is an American professional basketball player living and competing in Amsterdam, giving Conan a ground-level view of what it actually means to pursue an NBA dream that lands you in the Netherlands instead. The conversation opens with Conan trying to wrap his head around European professional basketball as a concept — he imagines something between a rec league and a gym class, and Lucas good-naturedly corrects him, explaining that the Dutch professional league is a legitimate competitive circuit with serious athletes, real contracts, and fan culture that takes the sport earnestly. Lucas walks Conan through the structural differences between European and American basketball: shorter shot clocks in some formats, different physicality expectations, and the fact that being American in a European league comes with a particular set of assumptions from coaches and opponents alike — you're presumed to be the star, which creates pressure even before tip-off. Conan zeroes in on the daily life aspect, asking what it's like to be a young American athlete building a life in Amsterdam, a city that is beautiful and culturally rich but also completely different from anywhere most American kids grew up. Lucas describes navigating a new language, a new food culture, and the loneliness that can come with chasing a professional sports career far from home. Conan brings genuine curiosity rather than condescension, and the conversation finds its warmth in the gap between what Lucas imagined his basketball career would look like and what it actually became — something different, but still real.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Imagines Dutch Basketball Wrong
highlights_v2.summary: Conan's opening assumption is that professional basketball in the Netherlands is something like an enthusiastic gym class. Lucas systematically dismantles this image, explaining the structure, competitiveness, and legitimacy of the European professional circuit. Conan's gradual recalibration — from bemused to genuinely impressed — drives the first section of the conversation.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I pictured, like, very tall men politely passing the ball in wooden shoes.

highlights_v2.title: American Player Presumed Automatic Star
highlights_v2.summary: Lucas explains that being an American in a European basketball league comes loaded with built-in expectations: coaches, opponents, and fans all assume you're the best player on the floor before you've touched the ball. He describes how this creates both opportunity and a unique kind of pressure — you have to either live up to the presumption or quietly correct it every game.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Everyone assumes you're the guy. You have to prove it or prove it differently every night.

highlights_v2.title: Building a Life Far From Home
highlights_v2.summary: The conversation turns to daily life in Amsterdam — a new language, unfamiliar food, and the particular loneliness of being a young American athlete abroad. Lucas is candid about the gap between what he imagined his career would look like and what it actually became, and Conan listens without deflecting into comedy, letting the emotional honesty sit.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: It's not what I pictured, but it's still mine — and that counts for something.

highlights: Mike co-founded The Forest Farmers in Lake Placid and walks Conan through the many uses of birch sap.
The episode includes a live Chill Chums syrup taste test that puts Conan's palate to the test.
Mike explains why birch sap is a surprisingly versatile and underappreciated ingredient.
Conan's Own.summary: Mike is the co-founder of The Forest Farmers, a Lake Placid, New York business built around harvesting and selling birch sap — an ingredient most of Conan's audience has almost certainly never thought about and Conan himself had no framework for at all. The conversation begins with Conan genuinely confused about what birch sap is, what it tastes like, and why anyone would want it, which gives Mike a natural opening to evangelize. He walks Conan through the harvesting process — tapping trees in early spring when temperatures swing between freezing nights and above-freezing days, a narrow seasonal window that makes birch sap both precious and time-sensitive. Mike then pivots to the versatility of the ingredient: birch sap can be drunk raw as a lightly sweet, slightly mineral water-like beverage, reduced into syrup, fermented into wine or beer, or used as a cooking liquid. Conan's skepticism melts incrementally as Mike describes each application with genuine enthusiasm. The episode's centerpiece is a live Chill Chums syrup taste test, in which Conan tries birch syrup on the air and is forced to give an honest reaction. The tasting becomes a comedic set piece because Conan clearly cannot decide whether he loves it or merely tolerates it, and his hedging — elaborate, over-explained qualifications for what is ultimately a simple flavor reaction — becomes funnier the longer it goes. Mike handles Conan's ambivalence with good humor. The episode ends with Conan declaring that a co-branded 'Conan's Own' birch syrup product is now inevitable.

highlights_v2.title: Birch Sap Explained From Scratch
highlights_v2.summary: Conan comes in knowing nothing about birch sap and does not pretend otherwise. Mike walks him through the harvesting window — the narrow early-spring temperature swing required — and the range of products it yields. Conan's genuine curiosity turns the educational segment into an entertaining back-and-forth as he asks exactly the questions a total novice would ask.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: So you're telling me trees just... have juice? And we've been ignoring it this whole time?

highlights_v2.title: The Chill Chums Syrup Taste Test
highlights_v2.summary: Mike sends Conan birch syrup ahead of the recording, and the episode includes a live on-air tasting. Conan's reaction is the comedic engine of the moment: he clearly cannot locate a definitive opinion about the flavor and spends an escalating amount of time constructing hedged qualifications about what he is tasting — a masterclass in avoiding commitment while technically answering the question.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: It's like if maple syrup went to college and became more interesting but less fun at parties.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's Own Birch Syrup Line
highlights_v2.summary: As the conversation wraps, Conan declares that a co-branded 'Conan's Own' birch syrup product is now an inevitability and begins assigning himself creative control over the label design. Mike, who has an actual business to run, navigates Conan's co-branding enthusiasm with diplomatic amusement, neither fully endorsing nor dismissing the idea.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I need my face on the bottle. That's a non-negotiable for me.

highlights: Phil coordinates adventure-based team building, and Conan tries to understand why anyone would enjoy it.
Phil explains how activities like rock climbing and rope courses actually foster workplace collaboration.
Conan and Phil debate whether corporate team building ever genuinely works or is just awkward.
We're gettin' Belayed!.summary: Phil coordinates adventure-based team building activities in Vermont — ropes courses, rock climbing, rappelling, orienteering — and his conversation with Conan quickly becomes a philosophical debate about whether corporate team building has ever, in the history of corporate team building, actually worked. Conan arrives at this episode with a full arsenal of skepticism. He presents team building as a socially enforced humiliation ritual that produces only resentment and bad stories, and he wants Phil to either defend it or admit the whole industry is a con. Phil, to his credit, does not take the defensive bait. He acknowledges that badly designed team building absolutely produces the outcomes Conan describes, but argues that adventure-based activities specifically work differently because they require genuine interdependence — you cannot fake a belay. When one person is holding the rope for another, the trust is functional, not performative. Conan finds this argument more compelling than he expected, and his resistance softens from wholesale rejection to targeted skepticism: he accepts that the activity itself might create real bonds but questions whether those bonds survive the Monday morning return to the office. Phil concedes that sustainability is the harder problem. The conversation finds an unexpectedly honest groove in that gap — acknowledging that the conditions for human connection can be artificially created but that what people do with the connection afterward is outside anyone's control. Conan ends the episode with grudging respect for Phil's work while maintaining plausible deniability about team building as a concept.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Indicts All Team Building
highlights_v2.summary: Conan opens with a sweeping dismissal of corporate team building as a genre — structured humiliation dressed up as bonding, producing nothing except shared discomfort and a mutual agreement never to speak of it again. He challenges Phil to either defend the industry or confess it's a racket. The directness of the challenge sets an entertainingly confrontational tone.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Name one team that became better because they fell off a log together. I'll wait.

highlights_v2.title: You Cannot Fake a Belay
highlights_v2.summary: Phil's strongest argument lands when he explains that adventure-based activities create functional trust rather than performative trust — when someone is physically holding your rope, the relationship is real whether you like each other or not. Conan pauses at this, visibly recalibrating, and admits it's a better argument than he was prepared for.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You can't half-ass holding someone's rope. That's the whole point.

highlights_v2.title: Does the Bond Survive Monday Morning
highlights_v2.summary: Conan's residual skepticism focuses on a single question: even if adventure activities create genuine connection, does that connection survive the return to fluorescent-lit cubicles and org-chart politics? Phil concedes that post-activity sustainability is the genuinely hard problem. The honest acknowledgment from both sides gives the conversation unexpected depth.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The rappelling was great. Then we got back and Dave was still Dave.

highlights: Maaike stands 6 feet tall and shares what the international high fashion modeling world is really like.
Conan and Maaike discuss the physical and logistical realities of modeling runways in Amsterdam and beyond.
Maaike gives Conan an unfiltered look at the challenges and perks of being tall in the fashion industry.
Long In The Leg.summary: Maaike is a six-foot-tall international high fashion model based in Amsterdam, and her conversation with Conan is an extended, affectionate examination of what it actually means to inhabit a body that the fashion industry simultaneously fetishizes and makes logistically impossible to navigate. Maaike describes the runway world with a combination of dry wit and factual precision — the casting processes, the way height creates opportunity in one room and makes you invisible in another, and the specific absurdity of being told you are simultaneously too tall for a job while being too short for a different one. Conan, who is famously tall himself, seizes on this shared experience and begins workshopping the parallels between modeling and late night television through the lens of height as both asset and liability. The conversation digs into the physical realities of the job: finding clothes that actually fit when your proportions fall outside the sample-size curve, navigating airports and hotel beds designed for someone eight inches shorter, and the particular challenge of sharing a runway with models who are significantly shorter while trying to make the lineup look cohesive. Maaike describes the international circuit — Amsterdam, Paris, Milan — with a grounded practicality that strips glamour from the mythology while leaving genuine appeal intact. She is neither complaining nor performing gratitude, which Conan clearly appreciates. The episode has a warm, easy rhythm, with Conan's own tallness giving him genuine footholds in a world he otherwise knows nothing about.

highlights_v2.title: Too Tall and Too Short Simultaneously
highlights_v2.summary: Maaike explains the paradox at the center of fashion modeling as a tall woman: she is told she's too tall for certain jobs and too short for others, sometimes in the same season. Conan is delighted by the logical impossibility of this and pushes her to explain how she navigates an industry where the goalposts are physically unattainable by design.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You're too tall, you're too short — they just mean 'not exactly what we pictured, which we never told you.'

highlights_v2.title: Conan and Maaike Bond Over Height
highlights_v2.summary: Conan finds genuine common ground with Maaike in their shared experience of being tall in spaces designed for people who are not — hotel beds, airplane seats, doorframes, clothing. He stops interviewing and starts commiserating, and the conversation briefly becomes two tall people exchanging grievances with the built environment.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Every hotel bed in the world was built for someone five-foot-eight. It's a conspiracy.

highlights_v2.title: Glamour Stripped from the Circuit
highlights_v2.summary: Maaike describes the international modeling circuit — Amsterdam, Paris, Milan — without the mythology. She is neither performing glamour nor performing martyrdom; she describes a job with specific physical demands, logistical complications, and rewards that are real but different from what outsiders imagine. Conan appreciates the directness and lets her account stand without embellishment.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: It's beautiful work in beautiful cities and also exhausting in completely unglamorous ways.

highlights: Geography professor Stephanie explains why Richmond, VA is not her favorite place — and doesn't hold back.
Conan and Stephanie discuss what people consistently get wrong about the field of geography.
Stephanie connects her climate change research to why geographic literacy actually matters.
I Hate Richmond.summary: Stephanie is a geography professor at a university in Richmond, Virginia who studies climate change — and she opens the conversation by expressing, without particular hesitation, that Richmond is not her favorite place. This immediately delights Conan, who had presumably expected a standard fan call and instead gets a professor who has opinions about her own city that she is willing to air on a national podcast. Stephanie's Richmond critique is not a rant but a measured assessment: it's a city with real history and some good qualities that nonetheless doesn't quite work for her personally, and she delivers this with the even-handed precision of someone who grades student papers for a living. Conan extracts as much city-shade as he can before pivoting to what Stephanie actually studies. She explains climate change research through the lens of geography — which turns out to be a genuinely different entry point than the atmospheric science framing most people have encountered. Geography asks where climate change is happening, who it's happening to, how land use shapes vulnerability, and why some communities are exposed to risk that others are insulated from. Conan admits he has spent most of his life thinking geography was memorizing state capitals, and Stephanie sets the record straight about what the field actually encompasses. The episode moves fluidly between Stephanie's dry humor about Richmond, her genuine passion for her research, and Conan's increasingly earnest questions about climate geography — a combination that gives the call an unusual density for the format.

highlights_v2.title: Professor Roasts Her Own City
highlights_v2.summary: Stephanie opens with a candid, measured critique of Richmond, Virginia — not a rant, but a thoughtful assessment delivered with professional detachment. Conan immediately seizes on this, encouraging her to go further, delighted that a fan called in not to express love for Conan but to express ambivalence about their own city.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: It's not that I hate it. I just wouldn't have chosen it and I'm still processing that.

highlights_v2.title: Geography Is Not State Capitals
highlights_v2.summary: Conan confesses that his mental model of geography is entirely built around memorizing state capitals and country outlines on a quiz. Stephanie patiently but directly dismantles this, explaining that geography as a field is about spatial relationships, power, land use, and vulnerability — a frame Conan finds genuinely revelatory.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Geography is about why things happen where they happen to whom — not which city is the capital of Ohio.

highlights_v2.title: Climate Change Has a Where Problem
highlights_v2.summary: Stephanie reframes climate change through a geographic lens, explaining that the discipline's contribution is answering where the impacts land, whose communities absorb the greatest risk, and how land use decisions compound or reduce exposure. Conan asks genuinely curious follow-up questions, and the conversation reaches a level of substance uncommon for the fan segment format.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The question isn't just what's warming — it's who gets flooded first and why that was already decided decades ago.

highlights: Max works as a meteorologist in Fargo, ND, where the weather is both his job and his daily nemesis.
Conan and Max explore what it's like to forecast weather in one of America's most extreme climates.
Max explains the particular challenges and absurdities of telling Fargo residents what they already know.
This Is My Deal Here Wade.summary: Max is a meteorologist based in Fargo, North Dakota — a posting that puts him at the intersection of two extreme realities: genuinely severe, frequently historic weather, and an audience that already knows exactly how bad it is because they live there. Conan opens the conversation by marveling at the premise: you are a weather professional in one of the harshest climate environments in the contiguous United States, telling people information they can directly verify by looking out a window. Max acknowledges the absurdity cheerfully. He explains what Fargo meteorology actually requires: modeling the behavior of Arctic air masses, tracking blizzard systems that can materialize and escalate faster than forecast models can update, and calibrating public communication for an audience that is simultaneously well-informed and highly skeptical of forecasters. Conan zeroes in on the psychological dimension — what is it like to go on air and say 'it's going to be negative thirty tonight' to a population that will not be surprised but still needs to hear it from you? Max describes the particular professional pressure of being the person who has to deliver, with authority and precision, information that is both critical and unavoidable. The conversation moves into the technical — what Fargo winters actually look like from a data perspective, the difference between cold and dangerous cold, and the specific kinds of storms that make Max's job genuinely difficult rather than merely repetitive. Conan ends the episode with the observation that Max may have the most honest job in America: a forecaster whose audience already knows the answer.

highlights_v2.title: Telling Fargo What It Already Knows
highlights_v2.summary: Conan focuses immediately on the existential comedy of Max's position: he is a trained meteorologist delivering weather forecasts to people who can see the blizzard from their kitchen. Max leans into the absurdity, describing how Fargo viewers engage with forecasts — not to learn new information but to confirm what they already fear.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You're basically going on TV to say 'yes, it's exactly as bad as you thought' — every single day.

highlights_v2.title: Dangerous Cold vs. Just Cold
highlights_v2.summary: Max explains that Fargo meteorology requires distinguishing between cold and operationally dangerous cold — a line that moves based on wind, moisture, and how long the temperatures will hold. He walks Conan through the specific thresholds that change how public warnings are issued, and Conan's questions reveal a genuine curiosity about the technical craft underneath the broadcast surface.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Negative ten is cold. Negative thirty with a thirty-mile-per-hour wind is a different category of problem.

highlights_v2.title: The Most Honest Job in America
highlights_v2.summary: Conan wraps the conversation by observing that Max may hold the most transparently honest professional position in the country — a forecaster whose audience can immediately fact-check his output by stepping outside. He frames this as a kind of occupational integrity that most jobs lack, and Max accepts the compliment with dry Midwestern humor.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You are the only person in America whose audience can instantly verify everything you say. That's either noble or terrifying.

highlights: Glassblower Dan from Vacaville, CA describes his most intricate and elaborate glass creations.
Conan and Dan discuss how Conan allegedly pulls his weight around the household — a claim Conan defends.
Dan explains the skill and danger involved in working with molten glass as an art form.
Get Over Myself!.summary: Dan is a glassblower from Vacaville, California who spends his days working with molten glass at temperatures exceeding two thousand degrees Fahrenheit — a profession that Conan approaches with a mix of aesthetic curiosity and practical alarm. Dan describes the craft of glassblowing with obvious love: the way hot glass behaves like a living material, the narrow working window between liquid and solid, and the physical vocabulary of the craft that takes years to develop. Conan is immediately drawn to the more elaborate and impractical end of Dan's portfolio, pressing him to describe his most complex creations — the pieces that pushed the boundaries of what glass can structurally do while also looking like they should be in a museum. Dan walks him through several ambitious projects, describing the technical challenges with the enthusiasm of someone who still finds the material surprising after years of practice. The conversation then pivots to the episode's secondary comedic thread: how Conan allegedly contributes to domestic life at home. This subject comes up organically from something in the prior conversation and gives Conan the opportunity to mount a defense of his household contributions that is elaborate, unverifiable, and extremely funny. Dan serves as a reasonable skeptic throughout, asking follow-up questions that force Conan to get increasingly specific about tasks he claims to perform. The rss description notes that this includes how 'Conan pulls his own weight around the household,' and Conan's case for himself — passionate, self-aggrandizing, probably fictional — becomes the comedic counterweight to Dan's actual, physical, dangerous craft.

highlights_v2.title: Molten Glass as a Living Material
highlights_v2.summary: Dan describes the physical properties of hot glass — the narrow window in which it can be worked, the way it behaves almost organically as it cools, and the years required to develop the muscular intuition for navigating that window. Conan listens with genuine aesthetic engagement, asking about the most elaborate things Dan has built and how they stay structurally intact.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: For about thirty seconds it'll do anything you want. After that it's making its own decisions.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Defends His Household Contributions
highlights_v2.summary: The conversation pivots to Conan's domestic contributions, which he defends with mounting specificity and increasing implausibility. Dan functions as the straight man, asking follow-up questions that force Conan to get ever more granular about tasks he claims to perform — each answer slightly more elaborate than the last, none particularly convincing.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I do things around the house. Many things. Specific things that I will describe to you right now.

highlights_v2.title: The Danger Is the Point
highlights_v2.summary: Dan explains that glassblowing's physical danger — the heat, the molten material, the risk of structural failure in a finished piece — is inseparable from what makes the craft meaningful. Objects that couldn't shatter don't earn the same relationship between maker and material. Conan takes this seriously and draws a brief, genuine parallel to performance work that can fail in real time.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: If it couldn't break, making it wouldn't mean anything.

highlights: Aadithya (Aadu) works at the University of Washington School of Oceanography and describes his research.
Aadu assigns specific birds to Conan, Matt, and Sona — and explains his ornithological reasoning.
Conan and Aadu discuss Aadu's upcoming wedding and how his life straddles science and celebration.
The Cock of the Rock.summary: Aadithya — who goes by Aadu — is a research engineer at the University of Washington School of Oceanography, and his episode with Conan covers three distinct but equally entertaining territories: his scientific work, his upcoming wedding, and a bit involving him assigning birds to Conan, Matt Gourley, and Sona Movsesian. The RSS description confirms that the episode's title is a reference to this bird assignment segment, which becomes the comedic spine of the call. Aadu walks Conan through his oceanography research with genuine enthusiasm, explaining what it means to be a research engineer at that level — the instrumentation, the data collection, the specific questions his lab is trying to answer about ocean systems. Conan is curious and asks reasonably substantive follow-up questions before eventually asking Aadu which bird he thinks Conan most resembles. Aadu has clearly thought about this — possibly prepared for it — and delivers his ornithological reasoning for each of the three show figures with deadpan confidence. The reasoning for Conan's bird involves something about presence, coloring, and a personality trait Aadu identifies with characteristic specificity. The responses for Matt and Sona are presumably equally considered and generate additional comedic mileage. The episode's third thread is Aadu's upcoming wedding, which Conan uses as an opportunity to offer unsolicited advice about marriage and celebration that is probably less useful than Aadu's bird analysis. The conversation has a warm, chaotic energy driven by the range of topics — from ocean data to nuptial planning to ornithology — and Aadu navigates it with relaxed fluency.

highlights_v2.title: Aadu Assigns Everyone a Bird
highlights_v2.summary: The episode's comedic centerpiece has Aadu assigning birds to Conan, Matt, and Sona, each with fully articulated ornithological reasoning. The bit works because Aadu delivers his analysis with genuine specificity and no ironic winking — he has clearly thought about this, and the earnestness makes the humor land harder than if it were played as a joke.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The Cock of the Rock is bright, attention-commanding, and not entirely sure what it's doing — but it owns the space anyway.

highlights_v2.title: Oceanography Research Explained
highlights_v2.summary: Aadu describes his work as a research engineer at the University of Washington School of Oceanography — the instrumentation his lab uses, what ocean system data they're collecting, and what questions drive the research. Conan engages with genuine curiosity, and the segment gives the episode an unexpectedly substantive scientific backbone before pivoting to birds.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: We're basically building instruments to listen to the ocean at scales most people don't think about.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Offers Wedding Advice
highlights_v2.summary: Learning that Aadu is planning a wedding, Conan pivots into unsolicited marriage and celebration advice — confident, self-referential, and of debatable value. The contrast between Aadu's careful, evidence-based scientific sensibility and Conan's freewheeling opinion delivery becomes the joke, with Aadu accepting the advice politely and unconvincingly.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The only thing I know about weddings is that everyone is wrong about everything except the food.

highlights: Civil War historian Cooper explains how to weave Civil War discourse naturally into a first date conversation.
Conan and Cooper debate how Conan would have actually fared as a soldier on the Civil War battlefield.
Cooper uses a PowerPoint-style breakdown to make Civil War history accessible and surprisingly funny.
Power Point Night.summary: Conan speaks with Cooper, a Civil War historian based in Washington, D.C., about the peculiar challenge of being someone who loves the Civil War in all social settings. The episode's premise — how do you naturally work Civil War discourse into a first date — is absurd on its face, but Cooper handles it with the earnest expertise of someone who has clearly thought about this more than is healthy. Cooper walks Conan through a kind of informal PowerPoint-style breakdown of how to introduce the topic without clearing the room, framing historical anecdotes as conversation-starters rather than lectures. Conan is an enthusiastic sparring partner here, treating the advice with mock seriousness while genuinely seeming to enjoy the historical tangents. The conversation then turns to how Conan himself would have fared as a Civil War soldier — a rich vein of comedy given Conan's lanky frame and admitted aversion to physical hardship. Cooper provides an honest assessment that is not flattering but is delivered with affection. The two debate which regiment Conan would realistically have been assigned to, with Cooper applying actual historical criteria that make the joke land harder. The episode has the feeling of two people who are very different on paper discovering they share a fondness for absurdist historical tangents. Cooper's enthusiasm is infectious and his willingness to meet Conan at the level of comedy rather than pedantry makes him an exceptional guest. The title references the PowerPoint-style approach Cooper uses to make history accessible at social events, which became an organizing metaphor for the episode.

highlights_v2.title: Civil War Opener for First Dates
highlights_v2.summary: Cooper lays out a genuine strategy for introducing Civil War history into first-date conversation without derailing the evening. He presents this with the seriousness of someone who has developed the method through experience, which Conan finds both alarming and admirable. The bit works because Cooper's advice is actually sound, making the absurdity more effective.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You lead with a story, not a date. Nobody wants to hear about Antietam if you start with a body count.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's Civil War Fitness Assessment
highlights_v2.summary: Cooper applies actual historical criteria to evaluate how Conan would have performed as a Civil War soldier, and the prognosis is not encouraging. Conan enthusiastically accepts each unflattering conclusion, building a portrait of himself as a catastrophically ill-suited combatant. The exchange has a mock-serious quality that makes it one of the episode's funniest stretches.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You would have been reassigned within the first week, and not to somewhere better.

highlights_v2.title: The PowerPoint Approach to History
highlights_v2.summary: Cooper explains his method of breaking down Civil War history into digestible, visually structured segments — essentially a PowerPoint presentation delivered conversationally. Conan sees the practical genius of this and the two riff on applying the technique to increasingly inappropriate social situations. It becomes a meditation on how passion for niche subjects can be made contagious with the right framing.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: If you organize it right, people actually want to know what happened at Gettysburg.

highlights: Rianne from Taguig, Philippines is vertically challenged and describes the daily struggles that come with it.
Conan and Rianne bond over the indignities of navigating a world not built for shorter people.
Rianne's blunt, unfiltered takes on crowd navigation give the episode its memorable title.
Move Motherf@#$er!.summary: Conan connects with Rianne, a content creator from Taguig in the Philippines, whose episode centers on the very specific and very relatable indignities of being a shorter person navigating a world designed for people taller than you. Rianne is direct, funny, and utterly unapologetic about her frustrations — which include being invisible in crowds, being unable to see at concerts, being talked over in conversations, and having to navigate through public spaces where taller people simply do not register that she exists. Conan, coming from the opposite extreme as a very tall person, finds himself genuinely curious about what her experience is like and asks questions that reveal how little he has had to think about spatial navigation in his life. The conversation finds its best energy when Rianne describes her strategy for getting through crowds — an aggressive assertion of presence that is deeply at odds with her physical stature and entirely consistent with her personality. Conan is delighted by the gap between the approach and the person deploying it. The episode's title comes directly from Rianne's blunt characterization of what she wishes she could say to the people who block her path without noticing her. The candor of it — stated plainly without hedging — made it an obvious choice for the title and the episode's comedic anchor. Rianne's work as a content creator gives her a storyteller's instinct for shaping anecdotes, and her timing is sharp throughout. The exchange has the warmth of two people who find the same things funny for entirely different reasons.

highlights_v2.title: Crowd Navigation as Contact Sport
highlights_v2.summary: Rianne describes her approach to moving through crowded public spaces — assertive, direct, and with full knowledge that she will not be seen until she makes herself impossible to ignore. Conan is fascinated and slightly alarmed by the tactical specificity. The portrait of someone who has developed a whole philosophy around a problem most people have never considered is what makes the moment work.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: If you wait for people to notice you, you will be waiting forever.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Discovers Tall Privilege Exists
highlights_v2.summary: Rianne's descriptions prompt Conan to realize he has spent his entire life never having to think about whether he will be seen in a crowd. His dawning recognition that his height gives him an effortless social presence Rianne has to actively construct is played both for laughs and with genuine surprise. The bit lands because Conan's reaction is honest rather than performative.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I have never once wondered if someone would see me. I assumed everyone always saw me.

highlights_v2.title: The Title Phrase Arrives Naturally
highlights_v2.summary: The moment when Rianne delivers the phrase that became the episode title is remarkable for how unforced it is — she says it the way someone says something they have thought many times but rarely stated aloud. Conan's response is immediate laughter followed by the quiet acknowledgment that this is the most honest summary of the experience he has heard. It crystallizes the episode's energy in a single line.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Sometimes you just want to say: move. Just move. That is the whole thought.

highlights: Yousra from Rabat makes the case that Moroccan street food is among the highest quality in the world.
Conan and Yousra explore Rabat's club scene and best spa options, with Yousra as expert guide.
Yousra explains the title — why asking directions in Rabat is a uniquely unhelpful experience.
Never Ask Directions In Rabat.summary: Conan talks with Yousra, a food enthusiast and cultural guide from Rabat, Morocco, in an episode structured as an informal tourism consultation with a sharp comedic edge. Yousra is a confident advocate for her city — she makes the case for Moroccan street food with the authority of someone who knows exactly what she is talking about and does not feel the need to oversell it. The food discussion is one of the episode's strongest stretches, with Yousra describing specific dishes and the street culture around them in a way that makes Conan visibly hungry and immediately regretful he is not there in person. The conversation expands to cover Rabat's club scene — which Yousra navigates with insider knowledge — and the city's spa options, which Conan pursues with the enthusiasm of a man who genuinely enjoys self-care and is not embarrassed about it. The episode's comedic peak is the discussion that gives it its title: Yousra explains why asking a local for directions in Rabat is a uniquely unhelpful experience. The explanation involves a combination of cultural hospitality — no one wants to admit they do not know — and Rabat's street logic, which resists the kind of linear navigation that directions assume. Conan treats this as confirmation of something he has suspected about every city he has visited. The exchange between Yousra's proud localism and Conan's gleeful willingness to accept every Rabat imperfection as evidence of the city's character gives the episode a warm, almost travelogue-like quality.

highlights_v2.title: Morocco's Street Food Gets Its Defense
highlights_v2.summary: Yousra makes a passionate, specific case for Moroccan street food as among the finest in the world — not as a general cultural boast but with the precision of someone citing particular dishes and vendors. Conan responds as a genuine enthusiast rather than a polite host, pushing for details and expressing real regret that he is not eating any of it right now. The exchange has the energy of two food-obsessed people who have found each other.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: It is not cheap food. It is food that happens to be on the street.

highlights_v2.title: Why Rabat Directions Are Useless
highlights_v2.summary: Yousra explains the phenomenon that inspired the episode title: asking for directions in Rabat produces confident, enthusiastic, and largely incorrect guidance, because local hospitality demands an answer even when the answer is unknown. Conan finds this philosophically interesting and practically terrifying. The bit works as both a genuine cultural observation and an absurdist comedic premise.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: They will send you somewhere. It may not be where you asked to go, but they will send you somewhere.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Plans His Rabat Spa Day
highlights_v2.summary: Conan's enthusiasm for Yousra's spa recommendations escalates into a detailed personal itinerary — which treatments, in which order, and for how long. Yousra provides expert guidance with the patience of a professional while Conan's questions grow increasingly specific. The segment is funny because Conan's investment is entirely genuine and Yousra is a perfect guide who clearly also loves this topic.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I need you to build me a full day. Start with the hammam. Tell me everything.

highlights: Catalonian twins Alba and Berta teach Conan how to be more chill and spontaneous when invited to events.
Conan admits he struggles with impromptu social situations, and the twins attempt to coach him.
Alba and Berta explain the relaxed Catalonian approach to social life and why Conan needs it.
Conan and the Chill Amigas.summary: Conan speaks with twins Alba and Berta from Catalonia, Spain, in one of the few fan episodes to feature two guests simultaneously. The dynamic created by having twins on the call is immediately interesting — they finish each other's thoughts, correct each other's framing, and present a unified front that Conan has to navigate differently than a single conversation partner. The episode's premise is that Conan needs coaching: he is someone who struggles with spontaneity and impromptu social situations, and the twins have offered to help. Their Catalonian social philosophy emphasizes ease, presence, and a willingness to say yes to things without extensive prior deliberation — an approach that is entirely foreign to Conan's anxious, over-prepared way of moving through the world. The coaching is delivered warmly but with real directness, which Conan finds both useful and slightly destabilizing. The episode's best sequences involve Conan presenting specific scenarios — an unexpected invitation, a last-minute plan change, a social situation he did not anticipate — and the twins analyzing where his instinct goes wrong and what a more chill version of him would do instead. Their explanations of the Catalonian approach to social life feel genuinely cultural rather than just personal preference, which gives the conversation texture beyond the comedy of Conan's uptightness. Alba and Berta are funny together, comfortable on the podcast, and clearly not intimidated by Conan — which is exactly what this kind of episode needs. The title frames Conan as an honorary amigas in training, which is both a joke and a fair description of where the conversation leaves him.

highlights_v2.title: Twins Diagnose Conan's Social Anxiety
highlights_v2.summary: Alba and Berta walk through the specific ways Conan's approach to impromptu social situations goes wrong — the over-deliberation, the need for advance planning, the discomfort when events deviate from expectation. They are direct without being unkind, and Conan accepts each diagnosis with the rueful recognition of someone hearing an accurate description of themselves. The twin dynamic makes the coaching feel more authoritative than a single perspective would.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You are treating a spontaneous invitation like a problem to be solved. It is not a problem. It is an invitation.

highlights_v2.title: The Catalonian Philosophy of Just Showing Up
highlights_v2.summary: The twins explain that the Catalonian approach to social life centers on the willingness to arrive without a plan and trust that the situation will provide what it needs. Conan finds this philosophically interesting and practically terrifying. The cultural specificity of the framing — this is not just personal preference, it is regional identity — makes the lesson feel like more than self-help advice.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: We do not plan to have a good time. We go, and then it becomes a good time.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Attempts to Practice Being Chill
highlights_v2.summary: At some point in the episode Conan attempts to embody the twins' coaching in real time, responding to hypothetical scenarios with more spontaneous answers than he would naturally give. His efforts are imperfect in ways that are revealing and funny — he can say the chill thing, but the anxiety comes through in the delivery. The twins assess his attempts with the patient seriousness of coaches who know progress takes time.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: That was better. You said yes immediately. But you looked like it cost you something.

highlights: Javier works as a tourism information agent near Madrid and shares the strangest requests tourists make.
Conan and Javier compare notes on the most absurd things visitors ask about Spanish culture and history.
Javier explains how his job as a handyman and tourist guide puts him at the intersection of two worlds.
Claro Que Si.summary: Conan talks with Javier, who works as a tourism information agent near Madrid while also doing handyman work — a combination that puts him at the intersection of two very different relationships to other people's problems. The episode centers on the stranger requests Javier receives in his tourism role, which turns out to be an extremely rich seam of material: tourists want to know things that no information agent could reasonably be expected to know, ask for help with things that are not information-related, and occasionally treat the tourism office as a general consultation service for living in Spain. Javier's collection of absurd tourist requests is both specific and clearly curated from real experience, which gives the stories credibility — these are not invented for the sake of comedy, they are things that actually happened. Conan is the ideal audience for this material: he has enough travel experience to recognize the type and enough creative instinct to push each story toward its most extreme version. The conversation also explores what it means to occupy both the handyman and tourism agent roles simultaneously — two jobs that both require patience with confused or demanding people, but which manage confusion very differently. Javier navigates the comparison with good humor and some obvious pride in his adaptability. The episode title, 'Claro Que Si' (Of course, yes), captures the tourism agent's essential disposition — the professional commitment to finding a solution or at least a convincingly positive response regardless of what is being asked. Conan and Javier explore how far that commitment can stretch before it breaks, which turns out to be quite far.

highlights_v2.title: The Strangest Tourist Requests in Madrid
highlights_v2.summary: Javier shares his collection of the most absurd requests he has received at the tourism information desk — questions and demands that reveal how tourists think about a foreign city and how optimistically they view the scope of an information agent's authority. Conan treats each story with the enthusiasm of someone taking notes for future travel. The material is funny because it is specific and because Javier's patience with the chaos is clearly well-practiced.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: They do not want information. They want someone to fix whatever has gone wrong. That is a different job.

highlights_v2.title: The Professional Art of Saying Yes
highlights_v2.summary: Conan and Javier explore the tourism agent's core skill — maintaining a 'claro que si' posture regardless of what is being requested — and how far that commitment can realistically extend. Javier is honest about where the limits are while also being clearly proud of how wide the zone of 'yes' has become through experience. The conversation becomes a meditation on professional hospitality and what it costs to sustain it.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You learn to say yes first and figure out what that means afterward.

highlights_v2.title: Handyman and Tour Guide as One Role
highlights_v2.summary: Javier explains how his handyman work and tourism job require similar instincts — patience with people who do not know what they need, and the ability to improvise a solution when the standard one does not apply. Conan finds the combination unusual and admirable in equal measure, pushing for examples where the two roles overlapped in unexpected ways. The portrait of someone who is useful in multiple registers has an appealing practicality.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Sometimes the problem is the pipe. Sometimes the problem is they do not know where they are. I can help with both.

highlights: Vasco from Portugal walks Conan through the region's rich history and why its borders have stayed stable.
Conan and Vasco debate Portuguese delicacies, with Vasco steering Conan toward the best options.
Vasco floats the idea of Conan as a surfing wingman, a role Conan seems entirely unqualified for.
Longest Fixed Borders In History.summary: Vasco calls in from Portugal and brings with him an encyclopedic enthusiasm for his country's extraordinary history. He explains that Portugal has one of the longest continuously stable national borders in the world — a geopolitical feat that Conan greets with genuine curiosity, pressing Vasco on why Portugal managed to hold its shape when so much of Europe was constantly redrawing its maps. Vasco attributes this to a combination of geography, early consolidation of the kingdom, and frankly just getting the borders sorted out before everyone else thought to fight about them. The conversation shifts to food, where Vasco becomes an evangelist for Portuguese cuisine. He steers Conan toward specific dishes and delicacies, navigating around the things tourists order versus what locals actually eat, and seems mildly scandalized by what Conan might have previously assumed about Portuguese food. The most memorable tangent involves surfing: Vasco floats the idea of Conan accompanying him as a surfing wingman, a proposal that Conan receives with a mix of flattery and self-awareness about his athletic limitations. Portugal's Atlantic coast is world-class surf territory, and Vasco's pitch involves less actual surfing from Conan and more moral support and possibly holding towels. Conan leans into his own inadequacy here, imagining himself in a wetsuit looking pale and confused while Vasco rides waves. The episode has the feel of a genuinely warm international friendship forming over shared appreciation for history, food, and the comedy of mismatched skill levels.

highlights_v2.title: Portugal's Remarkably Stable Border Explained
highlights_v2.summary: Vasco walks Conan through the surprising fact that Portugal's borders have been among the most unchanged in all of European history. He explains the historical and geographic reasons with genuine pride, and Conan engages seriously before the conversation turns absurd.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: We sorted our borders out very early, and everyone else was too busy fighting each other to bother us.

highlights_v2.title: Vasco Corrects Conan's Portuguese Food Assumptions
highlights_v2.summary: Vasco takes on the role of culinary guide, steering Conan away from tourist traps and toward authentic Portuguese delicacies. His passion for the subject and mild exasperation at common misconceptions makes him a compelling and funny advocate for his country's cuisine.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Please do not just eat pastel de nata and think you understand us.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Recruited as Surfing Moral Support
highlights_v2.summary: Vasco proposes that Conan join him as a surfing wingman on Portugal's Atlantic coast, then immediately revises what this would entail — mostly Conan standing on the beach looking encouraging. Conan's self-deprecating acceptance of this diminished role generates the episode's best comedic beat.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You don't have to surf. You just have to be there. Looking tall.

highlights: Jules and wife Maria join from Miami; Jules has ALS and has used AI technology to preserve his voice.
Conan and Jules discuss the daily challenges and unexpected inspirations of living with ALS.
Jules and Maria share how their podcast The Couple Shift tells their story and connects with others.
Jules is Always Right.summary: Jules and his wife Maria join Conan from Miami in one of the podcast's most emotionally substantive episodes. Jules has been diagnosed with ALS, and the call opens with the remarkable detail that he has used AI voice-cloning technology to preserve his voice — banking recordings before his disease progressed so that his voice could be maintained artificially as his ability to speak naturally diminishes. Conan engages with this not as a gimmick but as a genuine and moving act of identity preservation, asking Jules what it meant to him to be able to hold onto his voice. Maria is present throughout, and the dynamic between Jules and Maria gives the episode its emotional texture — they have clearly navigated a profound challenge as a team, and their manner of talking about it is frank rather than grief-stricken. The conversation moves through the daily realities of living with ALS: the logistical challenges, the moments of absurdity that illness sometimes produces, and the unexpected sources of inspiration Jules has found. The couple's podcast, The Couple Shift, gets discussed as the vehicle through which they tell their story and connect with others facing similar circumstances. Conan handles the emotional weight of the conversation with characteristic sincerity — he does not deflect into comedy, instead letting Jules and Maria's story breathe. The episode title, 'Jules is Always Right,' likely refers to a running dynamic between Jules and Maria that emerges organically during the call, a small domestic joke that grounds a heavy conversation in genuine warmth.

highlights_v2.title: AI Voice Preservation Before ALS Progresses
highlights_v2.summary: Jules explains how he used AI voice-cloning technology to record and preserve his natural speaking voice before his ALS progressed further. Conan responds with quiet admiration, recognizing this as both a technical marvel and a profound act of self-preservation for Jules's identity and legacy.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: My voice is still mine, even if the technology is doing some of the work now.

highlights_v2.title: Daily Life Realities of Living with ALS
highlights_v2.summary: Jules and Maria describe the logistical and emotional challenges of day-to-day life with ALS, including unexpected moments of dark humor that have helped them cope. Conan follows their lead, allowing the conversation to be honest without becoming heavy-handed.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You find out very quickly what actually matters and what was just noise.

highlights_v2.title: The Couple Shift Podcast Origin Story
highlights_v2.summary: Jules and Maria describe how their podcast The Couple Shift grew out of a need to process their experience publicly and connect with others navigating similar crises. The conversation reveals how storytelling became a survival mechanism and a way to find community through isolation.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: We realized we weren't the only people going through something like this, and talking about it helped.

highlights: Etta works as an LGBTQ+ sober living counselor and describes what her clients navigate day to day.
Conan and Etta explore how sober living intersects specifically with LGBTQ+ community challenges.
Etta explains what drew her to this work and what keeps her committed to it.
Couldn't Have Done It Without You.summary: Etta calls in from Los Angeles, where she works as a counselor in LGBTQ+ focused sober living facilities. The episode opens with Conan working to fully understand what the job entails — sober living houses occupy a specific space in recovery, sitting between inpatient treatment and fully independent living, and Etta walks him through the daily rhythm of supporting residents through early sobriety. What makes her work specifically LGBTQ+ focused is the subject of extended discussion: Etta explains that queer people face particular vulnerabilities in standard recovery environments, where assumptions about family support, social networks, and identity can make generic programs feel alienating or even hostile. The LGBTQ+ sober living context creates space for residents to address sobriety and identity simultaneously rather than compartmentalizing them. Conan asks what drew Etta to this specific intersection of work, and she speaks with obvious commitment about the people she serves and the gaps she saw in existing resources. The conversation avoids being heavy-handed because Etta is matter-of-fact rather than preachy — she describes her clients' daily lives with warmth and specificity, and moments of humor emerge from the human texture of the work rather than at anyone's expense. The episode title 'Couldn't Have Done It Without You' likely reflects the community-centered philosophy Etta brings to her counseling, emphasizing the role of peer support in recovery. Conan engages genuinely, asking follow-up questions that reveal actual curiosity about a world he clearly knows little about.

highlights_v2.title: Why LGBTQ-Specific Sober Living Matters
highlights_v2.summary: Etta explains the specific gaps that standard recovery programs leave for LGBTQ+ individuals — from assumptions about family support structures to hostile social dynamics in mixed settings. Conan listens and probes, clearly learning about a corner of social services he had no prior framework for.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Recovery is already hard. You shouldn't also have to fight to be seen as a full person while you're doing it.

highlights_v2.title: What Clients Navigate Every Single Day
highlights_v2.summary: Etta describes the daily texture of her clients' lives in sober living — the structure, the peer dynamics, the small milestones that matter enormously. Her specificity makes the work feel real rather than abstract, and Conan responds to the human detail rather than the policy framework.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Some days the victory is just that everyone got through breakfast together without a crisis.

highlights_v2.title: What Keeps Etta Committed to This Work
highlights_v2.summary: Etta explains what originally drew her to counseling in the LGBTQ+ sober living space and what sustains her commitment through difficult cases. Her answer is personal and specific rather than generically inspirational, grounding the conversation in genuine conviction.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I've seen what happens when people don't have this, and I couldn't unsee it.

highlights: Julia explains how looms work and name-drops big-name celebrity weavers Conan has never heard of.
Conan learns Julia engineers biomedical textiles — actual human-body-grade fabrics — from her Ithaca studio.
The conversation spirals into whether weaving is a competitive sport and who the LeBron James of looms is.
