The Looming Loom.summary: Julia calls in from Ithaca, New York with a dual identity that confounds Conan immediately: she is both a traditional textile artist who weaves on a loom and a biomedical textile engineer who designs fabrics for use inside the human body. The conversation opens with Julia explaining the basics of loom weaving, which Conan receives with the enthusiasm of someone who has never thought about looms and now cannot stop. Julia name-drops celebrity weavers — apparently a real community of famous people who weave as a serious hobby — and Conan is delighted by the existence of this niche because he has not heard of a single one of them. The episode's comedic spine grows from Conan's competitive instincts: he becomes fixated on whether weaving is a competitive sport, who would be considered its LeBron James, and what a weaving championship would look like as a spectator event. Julia, who takes her craft seriously, navigates this with amusement rather than irritation. The biomedical engineering angle is genuinely surprising — Julia explains that textiles engineered to interface with human tissue, blood vessels, or internal devices require extraordinary precision and biocompatibility standards. The gap between 'traditional folk art' and 'things doctors put inside people' is funny on its face, and Julia handles the cognitive dissonance with patience. Conan cannot stop returning to the loom as a physical object — the size of it, the sound it makes, the implied grandeur of something called 'the looming loom' — and this fixation on nomenclature runs pleasantly throughout the episode.

highlights_v2.title: Celebrity Weavers Conan Has Never Heard Of
highlights_v2.summary: Julia drops the names of notable celebrity weavers as if they are household names, and Conan's total blankness at each one — delivered with increasing conviction that these people are famous — becomes a running bit about the invisible parallel universe of serious weaving culture.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I'm sure they're very important in the weaving world, which I now understand is a world that exists.

highlights_v2.title: Fabrics Engineered for Inside Human Bodies
highlights_v2.summary: Julia explains that her biomedical textile engineering involves designing fabrics that are implanted or used within the human body — blood vessel grafts, surgical mesh, medical device coatings. Conan's realization that the same person who weaves decorative textiles also engineers things that go inside people produces a genuine moment of amazed confusion.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I work with fabrics that need to coexist peacefully with your bloodstream.

highlights_v2.title: The LeBron James of Loom Weaving
highlights_v2.summary: Conan, unable to let the competitive angle go, demands that Julia identify the single greatest weaver alive — the LeBron James of looms. Julia takes the question more seriously than expected, which makes Conan's escalating investment in the answer increasingly funny.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: There has to be someone who is just humiliating everyone else at the loom. Who is it?

highlights: Pam explains the wildest calls she gets at Poison Control, including panicked parents and accidental ingestions.
Conan and Pam discuss how to handle being too high at Disneyland — a surprisingly common Poison Control inquiry.
The episode title is explained: certain foods and substances produce a colorful result that alarms callers.
Why Is Your Poop a Rainbow?.summary: Pam calls in from Farmington, Connecticut, where she works for Poison Control — a job that, she makes clear almost immediately, involves fielding a much broader range of calls than most people imagine. Conan opens by trying to picture the Poison Control hotline as a place of constant crisis, and Pam gently revises this image: while genuine emergencies do come in, a significant portion of calls involve panicked parents whose toddlers have eaten something alarming that turns out to be harmless, or adults who have consumed something unusual and need reassurance before they spiral into full catastrophe. The episode title is explained early: certain foods, dyes, and non-toxic substances can produce dramatically colorful results in a person's digestive output, and the resulting alarm leads people to call Poison Control convinced they are dying. Pam has clearly fielded this exact call many times and delivers the explanation with the measured patience of someone who has become an accidental expert in calming people down about the color of their stool. The episode's comedic centerpiece is an extended discussion of what to do if you find yourself too high at Disneyland — a situation that Pam confirms is a genuinely common category of Poison Control inquiry, generally involving edibles and underestimated dosing. Conan and Pam work through the protocol with increasing specificity, discussing the optimal strategy for managing an overwhelming experience in one of the world's most aggressively cheerful environments. Pam's professional composure against Conan's gleeful enthusiasm is the episode's essential comedic engine.

highlights_v2.title: The Colorful Digestive Alarm Explained
highlights_v2.summary: Pam explains the phenomenon behind the episode title: certain foods and substances produce startlingly colorful results in the digestive system, and the resulting panic produces a reliable stream of calls to Poison Control from people convinced they are facing a medical emergency. She has clearly explained this many times and does so with weary expertise.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Beets will do this. Certain food dyes will do this. You are not dying. You ate something colorful.

highlights_v2.title: How to Handle Being Too High at Disneyland
highlights_v2.summary: Conan and Pam develop a detailed protocol for managing an overwhelming cannabis experience in a theme park environment. Pam confirms this is a genuinely common Poison Control inquiry category and approaches the tactical discussion with professional seriousness, which only makes Conan's investment in the topic funnier.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The goal is to find a quiet corner, sit down, drink some water, and remember that it will end. The rides will still be there.

highlights_v2.title: Wildest Calls Poison Control Actually Receives
highlights_v2.summary: Pam walks Conan through the range of calls that come into Poison Control — from genuine chemical emergencies to accidental ingestions of household items by toddlers to adults who have consumed things they absolutely should not have but are generally going to be fine. The breadth of human error she has witnessed is both alarming and somehow comforting.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: People will eat almost anything and then be surprised by what happens.

highlights: Ben from Seattle builds and plays massive pipe organs, including the instrument at Kraken NHL games.
Conan marvels at how a single pipe organ can fill an entire arena with sound during hockey games.
Ben details the engineering complexity of pipe organ construction — thousands of pipes, each hand-tuned.
Unleash the Kraken.summary: Ben calls in from Seattle, where he builds and plays pipe organs — including the instrument used during Seattle Kraken NHL games. The episode opens with Conan working to calibrate the scale of the enterprise: pipe organs, Ben explains, are not simply large instruments but engineering projects involving thousands of individual pipes, each hand-tuned to precise specifications, housed inside structures that can take years to build. Conan's response to this information escalates from impressed to genuinely reverential, as the pipe organ begins to seem less like a musical instrument and more like a civil engineering achievement. The Kraken hockey connection is the episode's most immediately engaging hook — the image of a massive, centuries-old instrument being played during NHL games at a modern sports arena is inherently funny, and Ben explains that the organ's ability to fill the arena with sound without amplification is part of what makes it so effective as a crowd-energy tool. Conan asks increasingly specific questions about the mechanics: how many pipes, how are they tuned, what happens when one goes wrong mid-game, and whether there is a specific pipe organ sound that reliably ignites a crowd. Ben is an enthusiastic and knowledgeable guide who seems genuinely pleased that Conan is taking the instrument seriously. The engineering complexity section — thousands of pipes, hand-tuned individually, organized by pitch and timbre across multiple manuals — is delivered with enough specificity that Conan's growing wonder feels earned. The episode title 'Unleash the Kraken' connects the sports context with the mythological sea monster, a pun that works on multiple levels given the instrument's oceanic scale.

highlights_v2.title: Pipe Organ Fills an Entire Hockey Arena
highlights_v2.summary: Ben explains that the pipe organ at Kraken games projects sound across the entire arena without electronic amplification — a feat that impresses Conan once he grasps the physics involved. The combination of ancient instrument technology and modern sports spectacle strikes Conan as genuinely surreal.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: It was designed to fill spaces much larger than a living room, which turns out to be useful at hockey games.

highlights_v2.title: Thousands of Hand-Tuned Pipes
highlights_v2.summary: Ben breaks down the engineering complexity of pipe organ construction: individual pipes numbered in the thousands, each requiring manual tuning, organized into ranks by pitch and character, housed in a structure that took years to build. Conan's genuine amazement at this scale reads as earned rather than performed.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Every single pipe is a slightly different length, and every single one has to be right.

highlights_v2.title: What Pipe Organ Sound Ignites a Crowd
highlights_v2.summary: Conan presses Ben on the practical sports-entertainment dimension: what specific sounds or musical choices work best for energizing a hockey crowd? Ben answers with the specificity of someone who has tested this empirically, and the discussion of crowd-response optimization through pipe organ selection is unexpectedly riveting.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You want something percussive in the bass pipes. That's what moves people physically.

highlights: Lani explains what a tour manager actually does — logistics, babysitting artists, and preventing disasters.
Conan asks Lani how he would calm a frightened child who found him terrifying, leading to a creative answer.
The episode title references Lani's name featuring a macron — a long vowel marker unfamiliar to most Americans.
Lani with an Ā.summary: Lani calls in from Nashville, where he works as a tour manager — a profession that, as he explains to Conan, involves being equal parts logistics coordinator, crisis manager, and professional babysitter for touring musicians. The episode opens with the title joke: Lani's name includes a macron over the 'a' (Ā), a long-vowel marker common in Pacific Island languages, which most Americans have never encountered or pronounced correctly. Conan leans into this immediately, exploring the comedy of a spelling distinction that is phonetically meaningful in Lani's cultural context but invisible to most of his American interlocutors. Lani walks Conan through what tour management actually involves on a daily basis: routing logistics, rider negotiations, venue relationships, keeping artists on schedule, and managing the inevitable personality crises that arise when creative people spend weeks on a tour bus together. Conan, who has some experience with production logistics from his television career, engages as a semi-informed audience member who can appreciate the scale of the problems Lani describes. The episode's most memorable segment involves Conan asking Lani how he would go about calming a frightened child who found Lani's appearance terrifying — a question that implies Lani has a physically imposing or unconventional appearance. Lani takes this seriously, developing a creative, specific approach to winning over a scared child through humor and disarming behavior. Conan is impressed by both the thoughtfulness of the answer and the fact that Lani clearly has a prepared philosophy for this scenario, suggesting it may have come up before.

highlights_v2.title: The Macron Nobody Knows How to Say
highlights_v2.summary: Lani explains that his name features a macron — a diacritical mark indicating a long vowel — that most Americans simply ignore or fail to notice. Conan's attempt to fully grasp the phonetic significance and the cultural weight of consistently having your name mispronounced by everyone around you generates both comedy and a moment of genuine cultural exchange.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: It's just a line over a letter, but it's also kind of the whole thing.

highlights_v2.title: Tour Manager as Professional Artist Wrangler
highlights_v2.summary: Lani explains that tour management is fundamentally about preventing disasters rather than creating experiences — routing logistics, managing personalities on a bus, negotiating with venues, and keeping artists from destroying their own schedules. Conan recognizes several of these dynamics from his television production experience and engages as a fellow professional in organized chaos.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: My job is to make sure everything goes wrong on my end so the artist never has to know about it.

highlights_v2.title: The Strategy for Calming a Frightened Child
highlights_v2.summary: Conan asks Lani how he would handle a frightened child who found him scary — an oddly specific hypothetical that Lani takes seriously. Lani develops a thoughtful, specific approach involving humor and deliberate disarming behavior. The fact that he has clearly considered this before makes the answer both funnier and more touching.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You get down to their level, not just physically, and you give them something to laugh at instead of fear.

highlights: Michael Jay is a retired clown from Kitty Hawk who now works as a death doula — two very different callings.
Conan and Michael discuss what kind of classic clown Conan would be, with Michael offering a detailed diagnosis.
Michael explains 'benevolent clowning' — spreading joy rather than terror — and how clown culture has evolved.
Weekend at Conan's.summary: Michael Jay calls in from Kitty Hawk, North Carolina carrying two careers that at first seem incompatible: he is a retired clown and a practicing death doula. The juxtaposition is the episode's comic and emotional foundation — someone who spent decades professionally manufacturing joy is now spending his days guiding people through the final passage of their lives, and Michael Jay sees deep continuity between these callings rather than contradiction. Conan opens by trying to understand the retirement angle — clown careers apparently do end, with specific physical demands and performance circuits that have natural conclusions — before the death doula revelation arrives as a second tonal pivot. Michael explains that death doulas provide non-medical emotional and logistical support to the dying and their families, helping people approach death with intention rather than panic. Conan engages thoughtfully, noting the genuine service this provides while also acknowledging the emotional weight of the work. The episode's comedic engine is the clown analysis: Michael, using his professional expertise, assesses what kind of classic clown Conan would be. This involves Michael asking probing questions about Conan's social instincts, his relationship to physical comedy, and his capacity for genuine silliness versus performed silliness. The diagnosis he arrives at is specific and clearly considered. Michael also explains the philosophy of 'benevolent clowning' — an approach that prioritizes joy over shock or discomfort — and how clown culture has evolved away from the horror-movie associations that dominated public perception for a period. The episode title riffs on the film 'Weekend at Bernie's,' likely connecting to the death-adjacent themes.

highlights_v2.title: From Clown to Death Doula Career Pivot
highlights_v2.summary: Michael Jay introduces himself as both a retired clown and an active death doula, and Conan works to understand what he sees as the logical connection between manufacturing joy professionally and guiding people through dying. Michael explains the continuity with conviction — both roles involve being fully present with people at intense moments.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You are showing up for people at moments that matter. Whether that's a birthday party or someone's last week, the skill is the same.

highlights_v2.title: Michael Diagnoses Conan's Clown Type
highlights_v2.summary: Michael Jay applies his professional expertise to assess what category of classic clown Conan would belong to — a question that requires probing Conan about his physical instincts, his relationship to audience approval, and the nature of his comedic impulses. The resulting diagnosis is specific and clearly pleases Conan.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You're not a slapstick clown. You're a status clown — someone who plays with the hierarchy of who's in charge.

highlights_v2.title: Benevolent Clowning as a Philosophy
highlights_v2.summary: Michael explains the principle of benevolent clowning — an approach that orients the performance entirely toward the joy and comfort of the audience rather than the clown's own theatrical agenda. He describes how this philosophy separates meaningful clowning from the creepy associations that haunt the profession, and Conan finds the ethical framework unexpectedly compelling.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The moment you make it about your performance instead of their experience, you've lost the whole point.

highlights: Brad, a real miner from Manitoulin Island, breaks down efficient mine design and underground safety systems.
Conan asks Brad how deep he could bury himself — Brad provides a disturbingly precise and practical answer.
Brad assesses which specific mining job Conan would be best suited for, concluding it's a supervisory role.
Minecraft.summary: Brad calls in from Manitoulin Island, Canada — itself a geographic curiosity as the world's largest freshwater island — where he works as a miner. The episode title is a pun that Conan deploys with obvious satisfaction, since Brad is literally a person who mines, making him the real-world version of the game's central character. Brad explains what professional mining actually involves: the design of extraction systems, the engineering logic behind efficient mine layouts, the safety infrastructure that makes underground work survivable, and the specific roles that exist within a mining operation. Conan treats this as a genuine education, asking follow-up questions that reveal curiosity about a world of industrial labor he has clearly not spent time near. The comedic centerpiece of the episode comes when Conan asks how deep Brad could bury him — a question that sounds absurd but which Brad takes with professional seriousness, providing a disturbingly precise and practical answer based on soil conditions, equipment capabilities, and what a realistic burial scenario would actually involve. Brad's lack of alarm at this question and willingness to calculate the answer on the fly is itself funny. The episode's other major bit involves Brad assessing what specific mining job Conan would be best suited for. Brad conducts this evaluation with the rigor of a placement professional, working through Conan's apparent skill set and physical capabilities before concluding that Conan belongs in a supervisory role — a conclusion that is both diplomatically delivered and obviously correct. Manitoulin Island's remote geography also gets discussed as background texture for Brad's life and career.

highlights_v2.title: Brad Calculates How Deep to Bury Conan
highlights_v2.summary: Conan asks Brad how deep underground he could realistically be buried, framed as a casual hypothetical. Brad treats this as a legitimate engineering question, factoring in soil composition, equipment specs, and practical constraints before arriving at a specific and unsettling answer. His total professional composure makes the exchange funnier.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Practically speaking, you could go quite deep. The question is whether you'd want to.

highlights_v2.title: Brad Assigns Conan a Mining Position
highlights_v2.summary: Brad evaluates Conan for specific mining roles, working through the job categories with a placement professional's diligence. After assessing Conan's apparent strengths and liabilities, Brad concludes he belongs in mine supervision — diplomatically framed but clearly meaning 'nowhere near the actual excavation.'
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You'd be most valuable directing people and making sure things stay organized above ground.

highlights_v2.title: Efficient Mine Design as Actual Science
highlights_v2.summary: Brad explains the engineering logic behind mine layout — how extraction paths are designed for efficiency, safety, and ventilation, and how experienced miners think in three-dimensional networks underground. Conan's fascination with the spatial complexity is genuine and infectious.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You're always thinking about where the air is and where you can get out if something goes wrong.

highlights: Anthony works in cybersecurity and walks Conan through how hackers exploit everyday digital vulnerabilities.
The episode covers the hidden threats lurking in common online behavior most people never think about.
Conan and Anthony explore what a cyberattack on a podcast would actually look like and who the target would be.
In Cyberspace No One Can Hear You Scream.summary: Anthony calls in from Windsor, Ontario, where he works in cybersecurity, and the episode functions partly as a practical education in digital vulnerability and partly as Conan reckoning with everything he has probably done wrong online. Anthony walks Conan through the mechanisms by which hackers exploit ordinary digital behavior — the kinds of everyday online habits that most people engage in without awareness of the attack surface they create. The conversation covers the hidden threats embedded in common actions: clicking links in emails, using public Wi-Fi, reusing passwords, failing to enable two-factor authentication, and the variety of social engineering tactics that make humans the weakest point in any security chain. Conan receives this information as someone who is simultaneously curious and slightly terrified, occasionally recognizing specific behaviors Anthony describes as things Conan definitely does. The episode's most memorable conceptual bit involves Anthony and Conan working through what a cyberattack on a podcast would actually look like — who would bother to attack a comedy podcast, what they could plausibly steal or disrupt, and who the actual target would be. Anthony takes this exercise seriously, mapping out plausible attack vectors against podcast infrastructure, which has the surreal effect of making the Conan podcast seem both more important and more vulnerable than it did at the start of the conversation. Anthony is a knowledgeable and accessible explainer who avoids jargon without dumbing the content down, making him an effective guide for both Conan and a general audience who probably shares Conan's casual relationship with their own digital security.

highlights_v2.title: Everyday Habits That Hackers Love
highlights_v2.summary: Anthony walks Conan through the ordinary digital behaviors that create the largest attack surfaces — email link-clicking, public Wi-Fi use, password reuse — and Conan's growing recognition that he does most of these things gives the segment its comedic energy alongside its genuine educational value.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The hacker doesn't need to be sophisticated. They just need you to do one normal thing at the wrong moment.

highlights_v2.title: Mapping a Cyberattack on a Comedy Podcast
highlights_v2.summary: Anthony and Conan work through what a realistic cyberattack on the Conan podcast would involve — what an attacker could steal, disrupt, or exploit, and who the actual intended target would be. Anthony's willingness to conduct this analysis seriously makes the exercise both funnier and more educational than expected.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The podcast itself isn't the target. You're the target. The podcast is just how they get to you.

highlights_v2.title: Humans as the Weakest Security Link
highlights_v2.summary: Anthony explains that most successful cyberattacks exploit human psychology rather than technical vulnerabilities — social engineering, urgency, trust manipulation — and that no amount of technical security infrastructure protects against a person who is willing to be fooled. Conan processes this with both alarm and a kind of philosophical acceptance.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You can have perfect systems. But if someone calls you and says it's urgent, the system doesn't matter anymore.

highlights: Brie, a Utah mother of six, describes her post-Mormon journey discovering coffee, alcohol, and freedom.
Conan learns Brie owns a bridal shop — and proceeds to design his own bridal gown in enthusiastic detail.
Brie explains raising six sons in Utah while quietly drinking rum and Cokes in the bathroom during church events.
Rum and Cokes in the Bathroom.summary: Brie is a Utah mother of six boys who has left the Mormon church and is navigating a life full of small, hard-won freedoms — including coffee, alcohol, and owning her own bridal shop. Conan opens the conversation by probing what post-Mormon life actually looks like day-to-day, and Brie explains that the changes feel both liberating and socially complicated in a community where the church shapes nearly every social interaction. She describes the episode's title moment: sneaking rum and Cokes in the bathroom during church-adjacent family events, which she frames not as rebellion but as quiet survival. Conan is delighted by the specificity and asks follow-up questions about whether she worries about being caught, leading to a genuinely funny riff on the logistics of stealth drinking in a bathroom while surrounded by relatives. The conversation shifts to her bridal shop, and Conan — who cannot resist any invitation to become an active participant — launches into designing his own bridal gown with total commitment, specifying silhouette, fabric choices, and what he imagines the veil situation would be. Brie plays along as the reluctant professional, offering notes. The episode has a warm undertone: Brie is clearly someone who has done real work to define herself outside a very structured identity, and Conan treats her story with genuine curiosity rather than mockery.

highlights_v2.title: Bathroom Rum and Coke Survival
highlights_v2.summary: Brie reveals that her method of coping with church-adjacent family events is to sneak rum and Cokes in the bathroom. Conan presses for operational details — timing, concealment, exit strategy — and Brie explains it with the calm efficiency of someone who has done it many times. The specificity of the confession makes it both funnier and oddly moving.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: It's not rebellion, it's just how I get through the afternoon.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Designs His Gown
highlights_v2.summary: Upon learning Brie owns a bridal shop, Conan immediately pivots to designing his own wedding gown in elaborate detail — discussing silhouette, fabric weight, and veil options with full sincerity. Brie, as the reluctant bridal professional, offers feedback. The bit escalates as Conan becomes increasingly invested in the vision.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I want something that says 'I've arrived, but I'm also mysterious.'

highlights_v2.title: Post-Mormon Coffee Discovery
highlights_v2.summary: Brie describes the experience of drinking coffee for the first time as an adult after leaving the church — a small moment that carries real weight. Conan asks her to walk him through it, and the resulting exchange balances humor with genuine curiosity about what it means to discover ordinary things at an unusual age.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Everyone else had just always had coffee. For me it was this whole thing.

highlights: Brandon investigates paranormal occurrences in Georgetown, Ontario and shares his scariest destinations.
Conan and Brandon analyze what Conan's unique haunting style would look like if he were a ghost.
Brandon describes the strangest evidence he's collected and how skeptics respond when confronted with footage.
My Ghoulish Figure.summary: Brandon is a paranormal investigator from Georgetown, Ontario who takes his work with uncommon seriousness — cataloguing evidence, visiting haunted locations, and engaging with skeptics who challenge his findings. Conan starts by asking what the job actually entails and Brandon lays it out methodically: site visits, equipment, documentation, and a careful distinction between what can and cannot be explained. Conan is intrigued rather than dismissive, which unlocks Brandon's more specific stories. The scariest destinations come up and Brandon names locations with the quiet authority of someone who has genuinely been frightened in a professional context. The episode finds its comedic peak when Conan turns the analysis on himself — asking Brandon what his haunting style would be if he were a ghost. Brandon, clearly a thoughtful person, plays along seriously and the result is an extended, absurd character study of Ghost Conan: a tall, pale presence who probably talks too much and makes it weird for everyone. The conversation also addresses the evidence problem — Brandon has footage and recordings that he finds compelling, but explains the frustrating dynamic of presenting this to skeptics who require impossible standards of proof. Conan engages with genuine empathy here, noting that this is a specific kind of loneliness. The episode is funnier than expected and warmer than the subject matter implies.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's Ghost Haunting Style
highlights_v2.summary: Conan asks Brandon to analyze what kind of ghost he would be, and Brandon approaches the question with paranormal investigator seriousness. The resulting portrait — a very tall, very pale entity who cannot stop talking and makes every haunting about himself — lands as both a precise character study and a genuinely funny bit. Conan enthusiastically agrees with the assessment.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You'd be the ghost that other ghosts find exhausting.

highlights_v2.title: Skeptics Confronted With Evidence
highlights_v2.summary: Brandon explains the thankless dynamic of presenting paranormal footage to skeptics who dismiss anything short of a ghost shaking hands with a notary. Conan zeroes in on the specific frustration: the evidence is never enough, and the goalposts always move. Brandon describes one particular incident where even people in the room remained unconvinced, which Conan finds genuinely baffling.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: They were there. They saw it. And they still found a reason.

highlights_v2.title: Scariest Paranormal Destinations Named
highlights_v2.summary: Brandon lists the locations that have genuinely disturbed him during investigations, and the specificity — real places, real encounters — gives the segment an atmospheric weight. Conan asks follow-up questions that reveal genuine curiosity rather than showmanship, and Brandon responds by deepening the stories rather than playing them for effect.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: There are places I would go back to and places I absolutely would not.

highlights: Janis, a Miami sex therapist, shares professional tips for bringing more fun and connection into the bedroom.
Conan's team is assigned roles in a hypothetical sex therapy class, with Conan choosing his own subject matter.
Janis explains what the most common issues her clients bring to therapy are, surprising Conan with the answers.
Indecent Proposal: Czechoslovakia.summary: Janis is a sex therapist based in Miami, and this episode is a masterclass in Conan's ability to be simultaneously fascinated and deeply, performatively uncomfortable. Janis brings genuine clinical expertise — she explains her practice with clarity, names the most common issues clients bring to therapy, and offers concrete advice about communication and novelty in long-term relationships. Conan's reactions to each piece of advice range from theatrical shock to reluctant acknowledgment that the advice is actually sensible. The conversation covers what specifically brings most couples into therapy, and Janis's answers surprise Conan — not the salacious topics he might have braced for, but more fundamental issues around communication, expectation, and emotional disconnection. This leads to a broader discussion about how people avoid direct conversations about intimacy even in otherwise functional relationships. The episode's standout segment assigns Conan's team roles in a hypothetical sex therapy class, with Conan naturally claiming the most prominent position for himself and designating specific topics to colleagues based on what he imagines their expertise might be. The bit is chaotic and funny and Janis gamely evaluates the proposed curriculum. Throughout, Janis maintains professional composure while clearly enjoying the absurdity of her interlocutor, which gives the episode a nice dynamic tension.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's Team Teaches Sex Therapy
highlights_v2.summary: Conan assigns his production team roles in a hypothetical sex therapy curriculum, choosing subjects for each based on his own highly idiosyncratic logic. Janis evaluates the proposed curriculum with professional seriousness, which makes the bit funnier. Conan reserves the most important topic for himself without specifying what it is.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I feel like Matt brings something to the table that I can't name but everyone knows what it is.

highlights_v2.title: Most Common Issues Surprise Conan
highlights_v2.summary: When Janis reveals what clients most commonly bring to sex therapy, Conan's expectation — something titillating — gives way to the more mundane and relatable reality: communication failures, mismatched expectations, emotional distance. Conan's visible recalibration in the moment is genuinely funny, and the conversation briefly becomes substantive before he steers it back toward absurdity.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I thought it would be something scandalous. It was somehow more uncomfortable.

highlights_v2.title: Professional Tips for the Bedroom
highlights_v2.summary: Janis shares clinical advice about bringing more fun and connection into long-term relationships, and the advice is actually good — practical, non-embarrassing, grounded in what she sees work with real clients. Conan oscillates between performing discomfort and genuinely engaging with the ideas, which creates a funny and oddly useful segment.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The couples who talk about it are the couples who are still having it.

highlights: Trey runs escape rooms in Los Angeles and explains how Game Masters watch and subtly guide trapped players.
Conan pitches his plan to transition from podcasting into serious dramatic acting, with Trey's reluctant feedback.
Trey describes the most chaotic escape room group he's ever monitored and how close they came to destroying everything.
The Game Master.summary: Trey works as a Game Master at an escape room company in Los Angeles — the person behind the scenes who watches players struggle through puzzles via camera, feeds them hints when they're stuck, and occasionally has to intervene when groups begin to break apart under pressure. Conan immediately wants to understand the surveillance angle: what it is like to watch strangers argue, panic, and occasionally cheat, from a hidden observation room. Trey explains that Game Masters develop a kind of clinical read on group dynamics within the first few minutes — you can usually tell which teams will communicate well and which will fracture under a five-minute countdown. The most chaotic group Trey has ever monitored becomes a detailed story: a group that came very close to actually damaging the set, required multiple interventions, and still managed to fail the room. Conan's reaction to this story involves deep admiration for the chaos and pointed questions about whether Trey ever wanted to just let it happen. The episode's major comedic arc involves Conan pitching his transition from podcasting into serious dramatic acting, complete with a description of the types of roles he envisions and why he feels the industry is finally ready for him. Trey's feedback is measured, professional, and only slightly encouraging — which is not what Conan wanted but is funnier.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Pitches His Acting Career
highlights_v2.summary: Conan explains to Trey that he intends to transition from podcasting into serious dramatic acting, describing the kinds of roles he has in mind with total conviction. Trey offers feedback that is more measured than enthusiastic, noting specific concerns while trying to be supportive. The gap between Conan's certainty and Trey's gentle skepticism drives the bit.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The industry has been waiting for someone who looks exactly like me to be in dramatic roles. I'm just finally answering that call.

highlights_v2.title: Most Chaotic Escape Room Group
highlights_v2.summary: Trey recounts the most disruptive group he has ever monitored — a team that nearly caused property damage, required multiple hint interventions, completely ignored the narrative, and still failed. Conan asks at what point Trey considered simply letting the chaos play out, and Trey's answer reveals that this thought occurs to every Game Master eventually.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: There's a moment where you think: what if I just don't press the hint button?

highlights_v2.title: Reading Group Dynamics in Minutes
highlights_v2.summary: Trey explains that experienced Game Masters can assess a group's communication style and likelihood of success within the first few minutes of observation. He describes the tells — who takes charge, who goes silent, how they handle the first failed attempt — and Conan finds this unexpectedly fascinating, likening it to a social laboratory.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You can usually tell in the first puzzle whether they're going to make it.

highlights: Liz, a Providence children's librarian, explains the increasingly chaotic demands of modern library programming.
Conan is assigned a role in a fairy tale world by Liz — and the result is not a heroic one.
Liz discusses how children's libraries have changed and why her job is equal parts storytelling and crowd control.
Questions 3.summary: Liz is a children's librarian in Providence, Rhode Island, and she brings a wry, exhausted humor to the conversation that immediately clicks with Conan. The job description sounds gentle — books, story time, children — but Liz quickly dismantles that image. Modern children's library programming has expanded well beyond reading: she now coordinates craft projects, technology sessions, sensory play, and interactive events while simultaneously managing the crowd-control realities of working with large groups of small children who have not necessarily chosen to be there. Conan is genuinely interested in the logistics and asks pointed questions about what happens when a program goes sideways, which Liz answers with the specificity of someone who has many such stories. The evolution of public library services comes up — how children's departments have had to expand their mandate as community resources in ways that go far beyond books — and this briefly grounds the conversation in something substantive before Conan pulls it back. The episode's signature bit involves Liz assigning Conan a role in a fairy tale world. She approaches this with evident thought, and the role she selects for him is not heroic — it is something more specific, more comedically accurate, and more unflattering than Conan was hoping for. His reaction — oscillating between protest and reluctant recognition — is the episode's funniest sustained exchange.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's Fairy Tale Role Revealed
highlights_v2.summary: Liz assigns Conan his role in a fairy tale world, and it is not the hero, the wise mentor, or even the comic sidekick — it is something more specific and less flattering. Conan protests the assignment before gradually accepting that it is, in fact, accurate. Liz defends her choice with gentle precision, which makes it funnier.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You're not the villain. You're the character who makes everything more complicated before anyone can fix it.

highlights_v2.title: Library Programming Is Crowd Control
highlights_v2.summary: Liz explains that children's library work has evolved into something closer to event production and crowd management than quiet book-curation. She describes specific programs — sensory play, interactive story time, technology sessions — and the chaos that ensues when thirty children under seven have different expectations. Conan finds this vision of gentle mayhem deeply funny.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Story time is wonderful until the moment it isn't, and then it isn't very quickly.

highlights_v2.title: Libraries Became Community Anchors
highlights_v2.summary: Liz explains how children's library departments have taken on a community resource role that extends far beyond books — functioning as safe spaces, social programming hubs, and early-childhood support systems. Conan engages with this seriously for a beat before noting the gap between the public perception of librarians and the actual scope of the work.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Most people think we're just shushing people. We haven't shushed anyone in fifteen years.

highlights: Steven from Silver City is both an astrologer and a jeweler — and explains how the two disciplines intersect.
Conan is told which stone he should wear based on his birth chart, and he's skeptical but oddly invested.
Steven and Conan discuss whether Conan's belief in the power of objects is secretly a form of astrology.
Goofy Goo.summary: Steven is from Silver City, New Mexico, and he operates at the intersection of two disciplines that most people would not think to combine: astrology and jewelry-making. He explains that the connection is not as unusual as it sounds — specific stones have astrological associations, and many of his clients come to him wanting jewelry that aligns with their birth chart, their rising sign, or a particular planetary moment they want to honor. Conan finds this genuinely interesting and asks about the practical side: do people actually buy more jewelry when Mercury is in retrograde? Do they return it? Steven handles these questions with the equanimity of someone who has fielded them many times and answers them with more specificity than Conan expected. The conversation turns to what stone Conan should wear based on his birth chart, and Steven delivers a recommendation. Conan is skeptical but clearly paying attention — he asks clarifying questions about why that stone specifically and what it is supposed to do for him, which signals investment even as he maintains performative doubt. The episode's most interesting exchange arrives when Conan and Steven examine whether Conan's admitted belief in the power of objects — lucky charms, sentimental items, totems — is itself a form of informal astrology. Steven argues yes; Conan resists but cannot quite find the counterargument. The conversation ends with Conan in a position he rarely occupies: not fully convinced, but not fully dismissive either.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's Skeptical Stone Consultation
highlights_v2.summary: Steven reads Conan's birth chart and recommends a specific stone, explaining the astrological reasoning. Conan is overtly skeptical but asks a string of increasingly specific follow-up questions — what does it do, how does he wear it, what happens if he doesn't — that reveal genuine engagement under the skepticism. Steven answers each question seriously without rising to the bait.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I don't believe in any of this, and yet I very much want to know which stone I should be wearing.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's Object Belief Is Astrology
highlights_v2.summary: Steven argues that Conan's admitted habit of investing meaning in objects — lucky items, sentimental totems, things he keeps for irrational reasons — is functionally the same as astrology: assigning metaphysical significance to the material world. Conan resists the comparison but cannot articulate a clear distinction. The exchange is more philosophically interesting than expected.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You believe in the power of objects. I believe in the power of objects. We're not that different.

highlights_v2.title: Astrology and Jewelry Intersect
highlights_v2.summary: Steven explains how his two practices feed each other — clients come wanting jewelry that aligns with their astrological chart, and the stones he selects carry specific planetary associations. Conan asks the practical question of whether astrological timing actually drives purchasing behavior, and Steven's answer is more grounded and specific than Conan anticipates.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: People come in knowing exactly what they want. They just need someone who knows which stone that is.

highlights: Jonah sells used cars in Tupelo, MS and shares the psychological tricks that make a reluctant buyer say yes.
Conan develops his own universal sales pitch on the fly, which Jonah critiques with surprising professionalism.
The conversation pivots to Jonah's tattoos and how they affect customer trust on the used car lot.
Car Talk.summary: Jonah sells used cars in Tupelo, Mississippi, and he brings the low-key charm of someone who is very good at a job that most people underestimate. Conan's opening questions probe the psychology of the sale: what does a skilled used car salesman actually do that an unskilled one doesn't? Jonah's answers are surprisingly nuanced — he talks about reading hesitation, managing the moment of silence after a price is quoted, and understanding that buyers often need permission to say yes rather than persuasion. Conan finds this fascinating and immediately wants to apply the framework to his own work. The episode's comedic centerpiece is Conan developing his own universal sales pitch in real time, riffing on what he imagines would work across any product category and pitching it to Jonah for evaluation. Jonah, to his credit, critiques it seriously — identifying what works, what would actually backfire, and what category of buyer each line would hit. His professionalism in the face of Conan's creative chaos is consistently funny. The conversation pivots unexpectedly to Jonah's tattoos and the specific challenge of managing customer trust when you look a certain way on a used car lot. Jonah describes the calculus he does before each customer interaction — what he leads with, how he dresses, when he lets the tattoos come up naturally — and this segment has an honest, textured quality that elevates the episode beyond its comic premise.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's Universal Sales Pitch
highlights_v2.summary: Conan constructs a sales pitch in real time that he claims would work for any product. He delivers it with full commitment and then waits for Jonah's professional assessment. Jonah provides a genuine critique — what lands, what would alienate buyers, what is technically a threat — and Conan absorbs the feedback with performative dignity.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Some of that was good. The middle part might be illegal in certain states.

highlights_v2.title: Tattoos and Customer Trust Management
highlights_v2.summary: Jonah describes the active image management he does on the lot — how he adjusts his approach depending on who he's selling to, and how his visible tattoos affect the early moments of a customer interaction. He explains not defensively but practically, as someone who has worked out a system. Conan listens without making it a joke, which makes the segment work.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You learn pretty quickly who's going to relax about it and who isn't. And you adjust.

highlights_v2.title: Psychology Behind Saying Yes
highlights_v2.summary: Jonah explains that effective car sales is less about persuasion and more about creating the conditions for a buyer to give themselves permission to commit. He describes the silence after a price quote as a make-or-break moment and explains how amateur salespeople always fill it too quickly. Conan finds this framework genuinely interesting and applies it to interviewing.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Most people already know they want it. You just have to not talk them out of it.

highlights: Tom from Dublin makes the case that Bram Stoker — born in Dublin — should be reclaimed as an Irish icon.
Conan pitches wild ideas for the annual Bram Stoker festival, including events that horrify Tom slightly.
Tom explains how Stoker's Irish identity has been overshadowed by Dracula's Transylvanian setting.
Muahaha.summary: Tom is from Dublin and has a mission: he wants the world to recognize Bram Stoker — born and raised in Dublin — as an Irish cultural icon rather than a figure subsumed entirely by his Transylvanian creation. Tom makes the case with real conviction, explaining that Stoker's Irish identity, his upbringing in the shadow of Dublin Bay, and the specific Victorian Irish anxieties embedded in Dracula are almost completely absent from public conversation about the novel and its author. Conan, who enjoys this kind of contrarian cultural argument, is immediately on board as an ally. The conversation covers how the Transylvanian setting of Dracula has so thoroughly dominated the cultural imagination that it has effectively erased the author's actual biography — a kind of literary identity theft. Tom explains that Dublin does host an annual Bram Stoker Festival, which is a genuine effort to reclaim this connection, but that it remains under-resourced and under-known. This is the opening Conan needs. He begins pitching additions to the festival — events, activities, and programming choices — and the suggestions escalate from the plausible to the deeply impractical. Tom reacts to each pitch with the strained enthusiasm of a true believer who desperately wants an ally but is not sure about this particular ally's suggestions. The episode has a genuinely funny tension between Tom's earnest cultural project and Conan's creative chaos.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Pitches Festival Chaos
highlights_v2.summary: After learning about the annual Bram Stoker Festival in Dublin, Conan begins suggesting programming additions with increasing ambition and decreasing practicality. Tom evaluates each suggestion with the strained optimism of someone who genuinely needs the festival to succeed and cannot afford to alienate potential supporters. The escalation structure makes the bit very funny.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Some of these ideas are inspired. I'm not sure they're for our festival specifically, but inspired.

highlights_v2.title: Dracula Erased Stoker's Irish Identity
highlights_v2.summary: Tom argues that Dracula's Transylvanian setting has so completely colonized public perception of Bram Stoker that most people assume a biographical connection to Eastern Europe that does not exist. He explains specific ways that Stoker's Dublin upbringing shaped the novel that are consistently overlooked. Conan finds this argument genuinely compelling and unusual.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: He invented the most famous character in gothic fiction and people forgot he was Irish doing it.

highlights_v2.title: Irish Anxieties Hidden in Dracula
highlights_v2.summary: Tom makes the more subtle case that Dracula encodes specifically Victorian Irish anxieties — about colonialism, identity, the foreign other — that become visible when you read the novel knowing Stoker's background. Conan engages with this seriously before noting that he never expected a phone call with a Bram Stoker enthusiast to be this interesting.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Once you know he was Irish, the whole novel reads differently.

highlights: Sean, a Halifax naturalist, recounts the strangest and most alarming things he's found while diving.
Sean describes his long-running rivalry with a local snapping turtle that refuses to yield territory.
Conan and Sean bond over the idea that nature is both magnificent and deeply, personally inconvenient.
Sean and the Snapping Turtle.summary: Sean is a naturalist based in Halifax, Nova Scotia, and he approaches wildlife with the practiced enthusiasm of someone who has spent years making nature accessible and interesting to people who would otherwise dismiss it. Conan begins by asking what a naturalist actually does — a practical question that Sean answers with the slightly weary efficiency of someone who answers it often — before they get into the genuinely strange material. Sean's diving stories are the entry point: underwater, he has encountered things that do not fit neatly into any category of expected finds, and he describes them with the calm specificity of a scientist who has processed the weirdness and is now simply reporting it. Conan's follow-up questions push toward the extremes and Sean obliges. The episode's headline story is Sean's long-running territorial dispute with a particular snapping turtle — a specific animal with a specific personality who has, over multiple encounters, consistently refused to yield ground or adjust its behavior despite Sean's professional presence. Conan becomes completely invested in this rivalry, asking about its history, whether Sean respects the turtle, and whether he has considered simply accepting defeat. The episode closes on a note of genuine warmth: Sean and Conan converge on the idea that nature is not majestic and accommodating but frequently specific, inconvenient, and deeply personal — and that this is actually what makes it worth paying attention to.

highlights_v2.title: The Snapping Turtle Rivalry
highlights_v2.summary: Sean describes his ongoing territorial conflict with a specific snapping turtle that has, over multiple professional encounters, refused to yield or adjust its behavior. Conan becomes deeply invested — asking about the turtle's personality, Sean's emotional response, and whether there has been any resolution. Sean admits he respects the turtle more than he probably should.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: He's not aggressive. He's just completely certain that I'm wrong and he's right.

highlights_v2.title: Strangest Diving Discoveries Revealed
highlights_v2.summary: Sean recounts the most unexpected and disorienting things he has found while diving — objects, creatures, and scenes that fall outside any ordinary category of natural expectation. He describes them without dramatizing, which paradoxically makes them stranger. Conan pushes for the detail that most disturbed Sean personally, and Sean's answer is surprising.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You think you know what's down there and then you're very much wrong.

highlights_v2.title: Nature Is Personally Inconvenient
highlights_v2.summary: Conan and Sean arrive at a shared thesis: that the appeal of nature is not its majesty but its specific, irreducible inconvenience — the snapping turtle that won't move, the dive that yields something confusing, the animal that has no interest in human narrative. Both find this more interesting than the aspirational version of nature programming. The exchange has genuine warmth.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The animals don't know they're supposed to be awe-inspiring. That's the whole thing.

highlights: Isabel from West Virginia is building a fully self-sustaining homestead and explains what that actually requires.
Conan is asked how he would fill his time if forced entirely off the grid — his answer is panicked and detailed.
Isabel breaks down the hardest skills to learn on a homestead: food preservation, animal husbandry, and water.
Conan Gourley.summary: Isabel is building a self-sustaining homestead in southern West Virginia — not as a romantic back-to-the-land experiment but as a genuine, long-term infrastructure project that requires mastering a specific set of skills most people have never needed. Conan's initial questions probe the practical: what does it actually take to be fully off the grid, and what are the hardest things to learn? Isabel answers with the direct competence of someone who has already done the hard parts — food preservation, water management, animal husbandry each get specific treatment, and she explains not just what they involve but what goes wrong when you don't know what you're doing. Conan is genuinely impressed by the scope of what Isabel is managing and asks follow-up questions that reveal real curiosity. The episode's pivotal moment arrives when Isabel turns the premise back on Conan: how would he fill his days if forced entirely off the grid, with no internet, no staff, no familiar infrastructure? Conan's answer is panicked and specific — he catalogs what he would attempt, acknowledges how quickly he would fail, and then develops an increasingly detailed survival strategy that rests almost entirely on charm and negotiation rather than practical skill. Isabel evaluates his proposed strategy with the patience of someone who has learned that charm does not ferment vegetables. Matt Gourley's imagined off-grid behavior is apparently addressed at some point, which is likely funnier and more self-sufficient than Conan's. The episode captures the quiet appeal of a genuinely different kind of life without romanticizing it.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Panics About Going Off-Grid
highlights_v2.summary: Asked how he would survive without technology, staff, or infrastructure, Conan delivers a detailed and increasingly alarmed account of his likely trajectory — attempting various skills, failing quickly, and pivoting toward a strategy based entirely on social capital and negotiated dependence on more competent neighbors. Isabel's quiet evaluation of this plan is devastating.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: My plan is to be charming enough that someone who actually knows what they're doing lets me stay.

highlights_v2.title: Hardest Homestead Skills Explained
highlights_v2.summary: Isabel identifies the three skills most people underestimate when starting a homestead: food preservation, animal husbandry, and water management. She explains not just what each involves but what specific failures look like — the batch of preserves that goes wrong, the animal that doesn't behave as expected — with the authority of someone who has made and learned from each mistake.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Nobody tells you that water is the hardest part until you don't have it.

highlights_v2.title: Homestead as Long-Term Infrastructure
highlights_v2.summary: Isabel reframes homesteading not as a lifestyle aesthetic but as a multi-year infrastructure project — building soil health, establishing perennial food systems, learning skills in a specific sequence. Conan finds this framing more interesting and demanding than he anticipated, and the conversation briefly becomes a serious discussion of what self-sufficiency actually requires.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You're not living off the land on day one. You're building toward it for years.

highlights: Chris, a Chico paramedic, shares the most surreal calls he's responded to and how he stays calm.
Chris explains why he adamantly refuses to dress up for Halloween despite being surrounded by costumes.
Conan and Chris discuss the mental toll of paramedicine and the dark humor that helps first responders cope.
Medicine Is The Best Medicine.summary: Chris is a paramedic from Chico, California who calls in to discuss the surreal and often harrowing world of emergency medicine. Conan immediately zeroes in on the extremes of the job — what is the strangest call Chris has ever responded to? Chris obliges with vivid stories from the field, describing scenes that are simultaneously tragic and absurdly comic, the kind of situations where the human body meets complete catastrophe in the most unexpected settings. Conan reacts with his signature mix of disbelief and theatrical horror, prodding Chris for escalating details. The conversation then pivots to an unexpected subject: Halloween. Chris, despite being professionally surrounded by costumes, fake blood, and theatrical emergencies on a daily basis, refuses to dress up for Halloween under any circumstances. He is emphatic about this. Conan finds the juxtaposition hilarious and pushes back hard, pointing out that Chris essentially lives a Halloween episode every shift. Chris holds firm. The two then land on the psychological weight of the job — paramedicine demands not just physical skill but a kind of emotional Teflon. Chris explains the dark humor that first responders develop as a survival mechanism, the way gallows jokes become a pressure valve for absorbing trauma. Conan, who has talked about his own anxiety at length on his podcast, engages with genuine curiosity about how people build those mental defenses. The episode is funny and unexpectedly moving, with Chris coming across as sharp, self-aware, and deeply committed to a job that most people could not do for a single shift.

highlights_v2.title: Strangest Emergency Calls Recounted
highlights_v2.summary: Chris walks Conan through the most surreal emergency calls he has responded to as a Chico paramedic. The stories involve ordinary people in extraordinary physical crises, often in mundane settings, which creates a dark comedic contrast. Conan reacts with escalating theatrical disbelief, pushing for more details with each story.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You never really know what you're walking into until the door opens.

highlights_v2.title: Paramedic Refuses All Halloween Costumes
highlights_v2.summary: Despite being surrounded by fake blood, costumes, and theatrical emergencies professionally, Chris flatly refuses to dress up for Halloween. Conan finds this hypocrisy delightful and presses the point repeatedly. Chris is unmoved, insisting the holiday holds no appeal when you live the aesthetic daily.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I see enough fake emergencies that are real emergencies — I don't need to celebrate it.

highlights_v2.title: Dark Humor As First Responder Survival
highlights_v2.summary: Chris explains how paramedics use gallows humor as a psychological pressure valve to process the trauma they absorb on shift. Conan engages seriously, connecting it to his own discussions of anxiety and mental resilience. The exchange is unexpectedly candid, with Chris articulating exactly how dark jokes function as emotional protection.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: If you can't laugh about it later, it stays with you forever.

highlights: Bill's dog Hondo is a German Shepherd approaching retirement age — and the conversation centers on dog careers.
Bill also worked for a funeral home, giving him an unusually philosophical view of endings, human and canine.
Conan and Bill explore what retirement looks like for working dogs and whether Hondo has earned a pension.
Three Days From Retirement.summary: Bill from Winthrop Harbor, Illinois calls in to talk about his German Shepherd Hondo, who is approaching retirement age for a working dog — a milestone that Bill treats with the same gravity one might give a human career ending. Conan is immediately enchanted by the premise. The title riffs on the classic cop-movie trope of the officer who is just days from retirement and thus maximally vulnerable, applied here to a dog who has apparently put in years of loyal service. Bill explains what Hondo's working life has looked like and what the dog's retirement might actually mean in practical terms — what does a working dog do when the work stops? Conan runs with this, speculating wildly about Hondo's pension benefits, his plans for leisure, whether he has considered relocating to Florida. The comedic potential of treating dog retirement with bureaucratic seriousness is fully mined. Then Bill reveals another dimension: he also worked for a funeral home, which gives him an unusually philosophical relationship with endings — human and canine alike. Conan leans into this unexpected combination, noting that Bill seems professionally acquainted with finality from multiple angles. The funeral home background opens a thread about how people and animals are memorialized, and whether the rituals we build around death are more for the living than the departed. Bill handles these heavier moments with the measured calm of someone who has genuinely thought about them. The result is an episode that is consistently funny but carries real warmth, particularly in the affection Bill clearly has for Hondo.

highlights_v2.title: Hondo The Retiring German Shepherd
highlights_v2.summary: Bill introduces his German Shepherd Hondo, who is nearing the end of his working-dog career. Conan seizes on the retirement framing and treats it with complete seriousness, speculating about Hondo's pension, benefits package, and post-career plans. The absurdity of applying human career-retirement logic to a dog drives the comedy.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: He's put in the years. He deserves a good send-off.

highlights_v2.title: Funeral Home Work Shapes Worldview
highlights_v2.summary: Bill reveals he worked for a funeral home, giving him an unusually philosophical relationship with endings. Conan notes the dark symmetry — Bill has professionally processed the conclusions of both human and dog careers. The combination prompts a genuine conversation about how mortality shapes a person's perspective on time and service.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: When you work around death, you stop being afraid of the word.

highlights_v2.title: What Does A Work Dog Retire To
highlights_v2.summary: Conan and Bill explore what retirement genuinely looks like for a working dog whose entire identity is structured around a role. Conan's suggestions grow increasingly elaborate — a lake house, daytime television, perhaps a memoir. Bill provides the grounded counterpoint, describing what the actual transition looks like for dogs trained for purpose.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: He doesn't know what to do with himself when there's nothing to do.

highlights: Amalia is the self-described Local Personality of Thames, NZ — population small enough that everyone knows everything.
Conan fantasizes about running Thames and Amalia explains what the actual politics of a tiny town look like.
The episode explores how gossip functions as community infrastructure in small-town New Zealand.
The Gossip Girl of Thames New Zealand.summary: Amalia calls in from Thames, New Zealand — a small town where, as she cheerfully explains, everyone knows everything about everyone and the role of Local Personality is essentially a civic institution. Conan immediately recognizes a kindred spirit. Amalia's self-bestowed title is delivered with total confidence: she is not claiming to be famous, she is claiming to be essential — the person who knows what is happening, who is fighting with whom, and what the mayor is doing behind the scenes. Conan, who has spent decades as a figure of intense public attention himself, is fascinated by the small-town version of celebrity, where the audience is three thousand people and the gossip travels at exactly the same velocity as in Hollywood. The conversation explores how gossip actually functions in tiny communities — not as malice but as social glue, a way of tracking relationships, loyalties, and shifts in the local power structure. Amalia is candid and self-aware about her role, acknowledging that being the town personality comes with obligations and occasional enemies. Conan then goes full fantasy mode, announcing that he wants to run Thames. He begins laying out a platform — his first acts as leader, what he would change, who would be his cabinet. Amalia patiently explains what actual small-town politics look like: the school board disputes, the zoning arguments, the feuds that outlast generations. Conan's ambitions collide with the unglamorous reality of local governance. The episode is charming, witty, and reveals how much comedy is contained in the serious business of small communities.

highlights_v2.title: Amalia Claims The Local Personality Title
highlights_v2.summary: Amalia introduces herself as the self-described Local Personality of Thames, NZ — a town small enough that the title carries real weight. She explains the role with matter-of-fact confidence: she is the connective tissue of the community, the person everyone talks to and about. Conan is immediately delighted by the specificity of the claim.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: In a town this small, you can't really hide from being known.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Campaigns To Run Thames
highlights_v2.summary: Conan announces he wants to be in charge of Thames and begins constructing an increasingly elaborate platform for his leadership. Amalia listens with amused patience, occasionally pointing out that the actual job involves zoning disputes and school board arguments rather than the dramatic vision Conan is describing. The gap between Conan's ambitions and local political reality is the engine of the comedy.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I feel like I was born to lead a town of three thousand people.

highlights_v2.title: Gossip As Small Town Infrastructure
highlights_v2.summary: Amalia explains that gossip in a tiny community is not malicious — it is the primary mechanism by which relationships, alliances, and local history are tracked and maintained. Conan, who understands media and reputation at a very different scale, finds this framing genuinely interesting and pushes her to elaborate on how news moves and mutates in Thames.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Gossip is just how small towns remember things.

highlights: Joy is a Columbus zookeeper who introduces Conan to hellbenders and snot otters — giant aquatic salamanders.
Conan learns that penguins are far more aggressive than they appear and Joy has the stories to prove it.
The episode title riffs on Joy's zookeeper rank and the surprisingly military structure of zoo staff hierarchies.
Sgt. Microphone Glassian.summary: Joy is a zookeeper from Columbus, Ohio whose call immediately takes a left turn when she mentions her specialty: hellbenders and snot otters. Conan does not know what these are. Joy explains that they are the same animal — a giant aquatic salamander native to North American rivers, one of the largest in the world, named with the kind of unflattering candor that only a field biologist could love. Conan is equal parts appalled and delighted by the name 'snot otter' and asks Joy to justify it. She does, in clinical detail. The episode title itself references the quasi-military staff hierarchy at major zoos, where Joy holds a rank that gets abbreviated in a way Conan finds absurdly formal for an institution built around feeding animals. Joy then pivots to penguins, which most people imagine as adorable and docile. She corrects this at length. Penguins, she explains, are aggressive, territorial, and have no hesitation about attacking handlers who they have decided to dislike. Joy has the scars — metaphorical and possibly literal — to prove it. Conan's horror at discovering penguins are not what he imagined is theatrical and sustained. The conversation ranges across the unexpected realities of zoo life: the hierarchy among keepers, the politics of which animals get the most resources and attention, and what it means to be responsible for the welfare of creatures who have absolutely no interest in your career aspirations. Joy is funny, knowledgeable, and clearly loves her job despite — or because of — all of this.

highlights_v2.title: Hellbenders And Snot Otters Explained
highlights_v2.summary: Joy introduces Conan to hellbenders, also known as snot otters — giant aquatic salamanders with two of the least flattering names in the animal kingdom. She explains the biology in clear terms while Conan fixates on the name 'snot otter,' demanding to know who approved it and whether anyone advocated for a rebrand. Joy defends the nomenclature.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The name fits once you've seen one.

highlights_v2.title: Penguins Are Actually Quite Aggressive
highlights_v2.summary: Joy dismantles Conan's image of penguins as gentle, waddling comedians by describing their actual behavior with zoo handlers they dislike — territorial, combative, and unapologetic. Conan's reaction is a sustained performance of horrified disbelief as each new detail compounds the betrayal. Joy confirms she has dealt with penguin aggression personally.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: They decide they don't like you and that's it — they remember.

highlights_v2.title: Military Rank Structure Among Zookeepers
highlights_v2.summary: Joy explains the surprisingly formal hierarchy among zoo staff, where keepers hold ranks and the episode title references her specific designation in a system that applies military-style structure to the business of animal care. Conan finds the formality of it — rank and title in an institution built on feeding hippos — genuinely funny.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: There's a whole chain of command between you and the giraffe.

highlights: Sarah is an acupuncturist who explains to Conan which body points produce the most dramatic patient reactions.
The episode title comes from Sarah matter-of-factly noting that perineum needling is a standard treatment.
Conan and Sarah discuss the skepticism acupuncture faces and what the evidence actually says about its effects.
I've Needled Many A Perineum.summary: Sarah is an acupuncturist from Portland, Oregon who calls in and immediately establishes her credentials with a phrase that becomes the episode title — a matter-of-fact statement delivered with the breezy professionalism of someone who has said it five hundred times. Conan's reaction is the engine of the first segment. Sarah explains that perineum acupuncture is a standard, evidence-based treatment for certain conditions, and that her delivery of this information is not intended to be provocative — it simply is what it is. Conan cannot get past the title phrase. He returns to it repeatedly, each time generating a fresh reaction. Once the ice is broken, Sarah gets into the genuine mechanics of acupuncture: which body points produce the most dramatic patient reactions, which ones people dread, and which ones — counterintuitively — are painless. Conan is a reluctant and theatrical patient by proxy, flinching at descriptions and asking questions that are half curiosity and half self-preservation. Sarah explains where the needles go that patients find most alarming versus most effective, and the gap between the two is surprisingly wide. The conversation then moves to the skepticism acupuncture faces in Western medicine. Sarah acknowledges the debate directly — she does not dismiss the questions, but she walks through what the research actually supports versus what remains contested. Conan, who is genuinely curious about alternative medicine, engages seriously, asking what evidence would convince him versus what would not. It is an unusually substantive medical conversation wrapped in a very funny episode.

highlights_v2.title: The Episode Title Gets Delivered Casually
highlights_v2.summary: Sarah drops the phrase that becomes the episode title with complete clinical casualness, framing perineum acupuncture as simply part of her standard practice. Conan's inability to process the matter-of-fact delivery is the comedic foundation of the opening segment, and he keeps returning to the phrase as though he cannot quite believe it was said.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: It's just another treatment — patients get used to it quickly.

highlights_v2.title: Most Dramatic Needle Placements Revealed
highlights_v2.summary: Sarah maps out which acupuncture points produce the most intense patient reactions — physical and emotional — versus which ones are counterintuitively painless. Conan plays the role of the squeamish everyman, asking each question with audible trepidation. The contrast between Sarah's calm expertise and Conan's theatrical alarm makes the segment work.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The ones people fear most are rarely the ones that hurt.

highlights_v2.title: What The Research Actually Supports
highlights_v2.summary: Sarah addresses the skepticism acupuncture faces in Western medicine head-on, distinguishing between areas where clinical evidence is robust and areas where it remains genuinely contested. Conan engages with real curiosity rather than mockery, asking what would and would not constitute convincing evidence. The exchange is more substantive than the episode title suggests.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: There's more evidence than people think, and less certainty than practitioners claim.

highlights: Aoife DJs weddings in Connemara and explains the art of reading a room and finding the song that breaks the ice.
Conan and Aoife discuss how she helps shy guests discover their singing voices during receptions.
Aoife shares her philosophy on silencing the inner critic — advice that applies well beyond the dancefloor.
Craic Mechanic.summary: Aoife is a wedding DJ from Connemara, Ireland whose title — Craic Mechanic — is both a joke and an accurate job description. She calls in to talk about the art of reading a room and manufacturing joy on demand at weddings where the guests may or may not want to be there. Conan, who has spent his entire career trying to make strangers laugh and loosen up, immediately recognizes a kindred occupational philosophy. Aoife explains the mechanics of it: the song choice is secondary to the timing, and the timing depends on watching the room for the first twenty minutes and figuring out which table is going to be the ignition point. Once you find the right song for the right moment, the rest follows. She calls this finding the crack in the armor. Conan is fascinated by the craft of it — the idea that a DJ is not playing music but running a social experiment in real time. Aoife then talks about her secondary specialty: helping shy guests find their singing voices. At Irish weddings, this is an expectation rather than a surprise, but there are always guests — often from outside Ireland — who have never been in a room where adults sing spontaneously and seriously. Aoife describes what it looks like to watch someone discover they can do something they believed was not available to them. Conan, who is disarmingly honest about his own performance anxieties, connects with this immediately. The episode closes on Aoife's philosophy about the inner critic — the voice that tells you that you will embarrass yourself — and how silencing it is both the work of a good DJ and a metaphor for every difficult thing.

highlights_v2.title: Reading A Room At Irish Weddings
