highlights_v2.summary: Aoife explains her process for reading a wedding reception in the first twenty minutes — identifying which table will be the ignition point and which song will crack the room open. Conan, who understands the mechanics of audience management intuitively, engages with her as a fellow professional, asking follow-up questions that are precise and knowledgeable.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You're not playing songs — you're looking for the one that breaks the room open.

highlights_v2.title: Shy Guests Discover Their Singing Voices
highlights_v2.summary: Aoife describes her secondary specialty: guiding guests who have never sung in public through the experience of spontaneous communal singing at Irish wedding receptions. She pinpoints the moment when someone realizes they can do something they thought was unavailable to them. Conan connects this to his own experience with performance anxiety.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: There's always that one person who never knew they could, and then they do.

highlights_v2.title: Silencing The Inner Critic Philosophy
highlights_v2.summary: Aoife articulates her philosophy about the inner critic — the voice that predicts public embarrassment — and explains that her job is fundamentally about creating conditions where that voice becomes irrelevant. Conan finds this genuinely resonant and the conversation shifts into a candid exchange about self-consciousness, performance, and what it takes to let go of it.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The inner critic is loudest right before you stop listening to it.

highlights: Gualter is a Portuguese master tailor who explains the dying craft of bespoke suit construction.
Conan asks Gualter what kind of suit he would make for him — Gualter answers diplomatically but with precision.
The episode explores why a truly well-made suit takes weeks and why off-the-rack can never fully replace it.
Yope.summary: Gualter is a master tailor from Portugal who calls in to talk about the craft of bespoke suit construction — a discipline that is simultaneously dying and experiencing a quiet revival among people who can afford to care. Conan, whose on-screen wardrobe has always been a subject of public interest and comedy, is the ideal interlocutor for this conversation. Gualter explains what actually happens in the construction of a truly bespoke suit: the number of fittings, the way a skilled tailor reads a client's body to compensate for asymmetries that the client has never noticed, the weeks of work that go into a garment that most people cannot distinguish from a very good off-the-rack piece. Conan is humbled by this and intrigued. He asks Gualter the central question of the episode: what suit would you make for me? Gualter, who is clearly skilled at managing client expectations without bruising egos, answers with diplomatic precision. He does not say what Conan needs to hear — he says what Conan's body and station require, which is a slightly different thing. The episode then opens into a broader conversation about why bespoke tailoring is irreplaceable. Gualter makes the case that a suit made for your specific body is not a luxury item — it is the difference between wearing a garment and a garment wearing you. Conan, who has worn suits on television for decades and presumably knows which category he has been in, receives this with a mixture of recognition and mild self-deprecation. The episode is warm, specific, and oddly inspiring about craft.

highlights_v2.title: What A Bespoke Suit Actually Involves
highlights_v2.summary: Gualter breaks down the genuine process of building a bespoke suit — multiple fittings, reading the client's body for asymmetries, weeks of handwork on a single garment. Conan listens with the attention of someone who has worn suits on television for decades and is now learning what he has been missing. The detail is specific and the respect for the craft is evident.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: A real suit takes weeks because the body is not symmetrical — and neither is anyone's life.

highlights_v2.title: Gualter Designs Conan's Ideal Suit
highlights_v2.summary: Conan asks Gualter the inevitable question: what suit would you make for him? Gualter responds with careful diplomatic precision — not flattering Conan but assessing him, describing the silhouette and fabric that would suit Conan's specific frame and professional context. Conan receives the assessment with a mixture of curiosity and the mild self-deprecation he brings to all physical self-examination.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You need something that works with your proportions, not against them.

highlights_v2.title: Why Off-The-Rack Can Never Replace Bespoke
highlights_v2.summary: Gualter argues that a suit made for your specific body is not a luxury category but a functional one — the difference between wearing a garment and being worn by it. Conan, who has spent decades in television suits, responds with recognition rather than resistance, acknowledging that he can feel the difference even if he could not previously articulate it.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: When the suit is right, you stop thinking about it — and that's the point.

highlights: Rene from the Netherlands designs custom bicycles and explains what separates an artisan bike from a mass-produced one.
Conan learns about horse-riding underwear — a garment designed for cyclists that becomes a running joke.
Rene asks Conan about his favorite biking trails, revealing a gap between Conan's cycling enthusiasm and experience.
Bike Pervert.summary: Rene is a custom bicycle designer from the Netherlands — a country where bicycles are not a hobby but a fundamental unit of national identity — who calls in to discuss his craft and, inevitably, Conan's relationship with cycling. Rene explains what separates an artisan bicycle from a mass-produced one: the geometry is custom-fitted to the rider's body, the components are selected for purpose rather than price point, and the finished object is built to last decades rather than seasons. Conan, who has referenced his own cycling in various contexts, tries to present himself as a serious cyclist. Rene is patient with this. Then the subject of horse-riding underwear emerges. Rene explains that the padded cycling shorts used by serious riders are often described as horse-riding underwear in the Netherlands, and that the garment — essential for comfort on long rides — is something of a badge of commitment among dedicated cyclists. Conan finds this phrase unreasonably funny and it becomes a recurring motif. Rene asks Conan about his favorite biking trails, which opens a revealing gap between Conan's enthusiastic self-description as a cyclist and the actual specificity of his cycling experience. Conan's trails turn out to be less competitive and more scenic than he had implied. Rene accepts this without judgment. The episode is a gentle and affectionate exploration of what it means to love something — a craft, a sport — at expert level versus enthusiast level, and the comedy emerges from that gap rather than from embarrassing either party.

highlights_v2.title: Artisan Bikes Versus Mass-Produced Bikes
highlights_v2.summary: Rene explains what actually differentiates a custom-built bicycle from one bought off a showroom floor — custom geometry, component selection, and a build designed to last decades. Conan listens attentively, asking follow-up questions that reveal genuine curiosity about craft and the difference between something made and something manufactured.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: A bike made for you fits differently than any other bike you've ridden — you feel it immediately.

highlights_v2.title: Horse-Riding Underwear Becomes A Running Joke
highlights_v2.summary: Rene mentions that padded cycling shorts are colloquially called horse-riding underwear in the Netherlands — a phrase that strikes Conan as so specifically funny that he cannot let it go. It becomes a recurring reference point throughout the episode, each reappearance generating a fresh reaction from Conan who finds the combination of words inexhaustible.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You put them on and you understand immediately why serious cyclists are serious about them.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's Cycling Credentials Under Scrutiny
highlights_v2.summary: Rene asks Conan about his favorite biking trails, prompting Conan to describe his cycling experience with the enthusiasm of a hobbyist rather than the precision of an athlete. The gap between Conan's self-presentation as a cyclist and the actual specificity of his riding history is gentle and affectionate rather than embarrassing. Rene accepts every answer without judgment.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I bike. I bike a lot. I just don't know the names of where I bike.

highlights: Kayla has encountered Conan by chance multiple times in real life, and the episode reconstructs those meetings.
Conan tries to use his influence to expedite Kayla's passport application — with unclear odds of success.
The episode is a rare case where the fan's history with Conan is the subject, not her occupation.
Would You Like A Selfie?.summary: Kayla is a podcast fan from the United States whose call is structured around something unusual: she has encountered Conan by chance multiple times in real life, and the episode is fundamentally about reconstructing those meetings and what they mean. This is a rare format for the show — instead of the fan's occupation driving the conversation, Kayla's accidental relationship with Conan himself is the subject. Each encounter is different: a different city, different circumstances, a different version of both of them. Conan is genuinely curious about what she remembers from each meeting, how he came across, whether he was on, and what the experience of recognizing a familiar figure in an unexpected context actually feels like. Kayla provides specific details, and Conan's reactions range from delighted to mildly self-conscious to outright skeptical about his own behavior in those moments. The running joke — and also the actual plot — is that Kayla needs to renew her passport and Conan, in a burst of good-faith absurdity, offers to use whatever influence he has to help expedite the process. The episode title presumably refers to an offer made during one of their chance encounters. Conan's attempts to brainstorm how a late-night comedian with no government connections could accelerate federal document processing are elaborate and doomed. The charm of the episode is in the role reversal: Kayla knows more about their shared history than Conan does, and he has to reconstruct himself through her memory of him.

highlights_v2.title: Reconstructing Multiple Chance Encounters
highlights_v2.summary: Kayla and Conan work through each of their accidental real-life meetings — different cities, different circumstances — with Kayla providing the specific details and Conan trying to reconstruct who he was in those moments. Conan's self-consciousness about how he came across is genuine, and his reactions to Kayla's recollections range from pleased to mildly alarmed.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I remember every one of those times. I'm not sure you remember any of them.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Pledges To Expedite Her Passport
highlights_v2.summary: Kayla mentions she needs to renew her passport and Conan, in a burst of good-faith absurdity, commits to using whatever influence he has to accelerate the process. His brainstorming about which government levers a late-night comedian might pull is elaborate, inventive, and entirely without foundation. The joke is how seriously he takes a problem he has no tools to solve.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I know people. I don't know anyone in the passport office, but I know people.

highlights_v2.title: Fan Knows Conan Better Than He Knows Himself
highlights_v2.summary: The episode has an unusual dynamic: Kayla holds the complete record of their shared history and Conan has to learn about himself through her account of him. The role reversal — a fan informing a celebrity about his own behavior — is played for warmth rather than awkwardness, and Conan's willingness to be reconstructed through someone else's memory is genuinely charming.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: She's basically the archivist of a chapter of my life I don't fully remember.

highlights: Terri from Atlanta is a fight promoter and former undercover police officer — a combination Conan finds irresistible.
Conan and Terri run through a practice drug sting scenario, with Conan playing an enthusiastic undercover agent.
Terri explains what the psychological training for undercover work looks like and how it changes how you see people.
Off To The Ice Follies.summary: Terri is a boxing instructor and fight promoter from Atlanta who calls in with a résumé that Conan cannot pass up: she also worked as an undercover police officer before transitioning to the fight world. Conan finds this combination almost too good to be true — someone who has lived inside two completely different worlds of controlled violence, institutional and athletic. Terri is sharp, composed, and clearly used to being the most interesting person in any room she enters. She explains what undercover work actually looks like at the operational level: the training, the psychological preparation, the way you learn to inhabit a persona convincingly enough that professionals trained to spot deception do not catch it. Conan's curiosity here is genuine rather than performative — he has spoken at length about performance and persona on his own show, and Terri's professional shape-shifting resonates with him. Then they run a practice drug sting. Conan plays the undercover agent, Terri coaches him through the scenario, and the results are exactly as chaotic as they sound. Conan's instinct is to go big when undercover work requires the opposite — stillness, patience, and the suppression of every comedic impulse he has spent a career developing. Terri critiques his performance with the directness of someone who has run actual stings. The episode closes on the psychological residue of undercover work — how the training changes the way you read people, the habits that stick long after you leave the job. Terri is a genuinely formidable guest and the episode crackles.

highlights_v2.title: Undercover Officer To Fight Promoter
highlights_v2.summary: Terri lays out her career arc — undercover police work to fight promotion — and explains how the psychological skills developed in law enforcement translated directly to the fight business. Conan identifies the common thread — reading people, managing performance, controlling high-stakes environments — and pushes Terri to connect the two worlds herself.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Both jobs are about knowing who's bluffing before they know you know.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Fails A Practice Drug Sting
highlights_v2.summary: Terri coaches Conan through a practice undercover drug sting scenario, and his performance is a sustained disaster. Every instinct Conan has — go big, find the joke, escalate — is the opposite of what effective undercover work requires. Terri's critique is blunt and specific, and Conan's attempts to adjust his performance in real time make things worse.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You're too enthusiastic. Criminals are never that excited about anything.

highlights_v2.title: How Undercover Work Rewires Perception
highlights_v2.summary: Terri explains what the psychological training for undercover work looks like and, more importantly, what it leaves behind — a permanent shift in how you read people, notice inconsistencies, and assess risk in ordinary situations. Conan receives this with genuine attentiveness, connecting it to performance anxiety and the habits of observation that public life creates.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You can turn it off on the surface, but you never really stop watching.

highlights: Greg plays bassoon in a symphony orchestra in Kildare and explains why the bassoon is the comedian of the orchestra.
Conan and Greg discuss the strange social dynamics of orchestra life — hierarchy, rivalries, and rehearsal politics.
Greg describes what it takes to master an instrument that most listeners couldn't identify by sound alone.
Weird Around The Eyes.summary: Greg is a bassoonist in a symphony orchestra based in Kildare, Ireland who calls in to discuss an instrument that most concert-goers could not identify by sight or sound. Conan begins from a position of complete bassoon ignorance, which turns out to be exactly the right starting point. Greg explains that the bassoon is the comedian of the orchestra — its natural timbre is inherently funny, and composers have exploited this for centuries, using it for moments of bumbling grandeur or deflating seriousness. Conan, who spent decades in comedy, is immediately interested in the idea of an instrument whose entire sonic character is oriented toward humor. The episode title references the bassoon's physical appearance — the instrument is large, unwieldy, and visually awkward in a way that its players have apparently accepted as part of the deal. Greg explains what it takes to master an instrument that most audiences cannot identify by sound: the specialized embouchure, the reed construction, the way the bassoon's role in an ensemble is often invisible to the listener even when it is essential to the arrangement. Conan asks what happens in orchestra life when the bassoon does something technically brilliant and nobody in the audience notices. Greg has feelings about this. The conversation then opens into the social dynamics of symphony life: the hierarchy among sections, the unspoken rivalries between instruments, the politics of rehearsal seating, and what it means to spend your career in a world where excellence is expected and recognition is highly unequal. Greg is dry, self-deprecating, and clearly devoted to an instrument that rewards that personality.

highlights_v2.title: The Bassoon Is The Orchestra's Comedian
highlights_v2.summary: Greg explains that the bassoon's natural sonic character is oriented toward humor — composers have used it for centuries to deflate seriousness and produce moments of bumbling grandeur. Conan, whose entire career was built on comedy, is immediately fascinated by the idea of an instrument whose personality is defined by its comic potential. The analogy between the bassoon and Conan's own sensibility is not lost on either of them.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Composers reach for the bassoon when they want something to sound faintly ridiculous — and we oblige.

highlights_v2.title: Bassoon Brilliance Nobody In The Audience Notices
highlights_v2.summary: Conan asks Greg what it feels like to execute a technically demanding bassoon passage brilliantly while the audience has no idea the instrument is present. Greg's answer is dry and resigned — a career's worth of excellence performed in what is effectively acoustic anonymity. Conan's sympathy is genuine and the comedy comes from how seriously they both take the injustice.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The string section gets the applause. We get the satisfaction of knowing we were right.

highlights_v2.title: Symphony Orchestra's Hidden Social Politics
highlights_v2.summary: Greg opens up about the social dynamics inside a professional symphony — the hierarchy between instrument sections, unspoken rivalries, rehearsal seating politics, and the way excellence and recognition are distributed with striking inequality. Conan treats this as a revelation, finding the idea of a world governed by such specific unwritten rules both funny and completely plausible.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Every orchestra has the same hierarchy, and everyone knows it, and no one says it out loud.

highlights: Henry gives tours in County Donegal and shares the strangest questions tourists ask about Irish culture.
Conan and Henry discuss practical tips for being taken seriously in everyday life — a topic Henry has strong views on.
Henry explains what makes Donegal unique among Irish tourist destinations and why visitors keep coming back.
What's The Craic?.summary: Henry is a local tour guide from County Donegal in northwest Ireland — one of the most remote and spectacular parts of the country — who calls in to talk about what it means to represent a place to strangers for a living. Conan, who has traveled extensively and been on the receiving end of many tours, is an interested and occasionally skeptical interlocutor. Henry explains the particular challenge of Donegal tourism: it is not on the standard Irish tourist circuit, which means the visitors who do come are either adventurous or lost, and sometimes both. This produces an unusual range of tourist questions — some deeply ignorant of Irish geography, some surprisingly sophisticated about the local history. Henry has collected the best and worst of these, and Conan extracts them with the enthusiasm of someone who genuinely loves hearing what people do not know. The episode then pivots to the episode's secondary theme: Henry has strong opinions about how to be taken seriously in everyday life. This is not self-help advice — it is specific, observed, and delivered with the confidence of someone who has thought about it more than most people. Henry's tips are practical and occasionally counterintuitive. Conan, who has spent decades navigating the specific challenge of being extremely tall and extremely recognizable, engages with genuine interest. The conversation moves between tourism, local identity, and personal authority with the easy range that characterizes the best episodes of the show. Henry comes across as exactly the kind of guide you would want — knowledgeable, funny, and completely comfortable with the absurdity of showing strangers around a place they cannot find on a map.

highlights_v2.title: Strangest Tourist Questions About Ireland
highlights_v2.summary: Henry shares the most memorable — and most baffling — questions tourists have asked him during Donegal tours. The questions range from complete ignorance of Irish geography to surprisingly specific inquiries about local history. Conan treats each example as a small comedy gem and pushes Henry for progressively more extreme specimens.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Some of them ask where Ireland is relative to England — and they're already in Ireland.

highlights_v2.title: Donegal's Unique Appeal Among Irish Destinations
highlights_v2.summary: Henry explains what makes County Donegal distinct from the more-visited Irish tourist destinations — the landscape, the dialect, the sense that you have arrived somewhere that has not been optimized for visitors. Conan, who has an evident affection for places that feel genuinely themselves rather than performed, responds with real interest and asks what keeps tourists returning.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Donegal doesn't try to be charming — it just is, and people feel the difference.

highlights_v2.title: Henry's Tips For Being Taken Seriously
highlights_v2.summary: Henry pivots to his strong opinions about how to command respect in everyday life — a topic he has clearly thought about more than most people. His advice is specific, practical, and occasionally counterintuitive, delivered with the confidence of someone who has worked it out empirically. Conan engages by testing the tips against his own experience of being six-foot-four and impossible to ignore.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Being taken seriously has more to do with pace than with anything you say.

highlights: Eugenia is an English language learner who speaks with Conan about the quirks and pitfalls of learning English.
The episode title captures a classic English understatement that Eugenia finds baffling compared to her native tongue.
Conan and Eugenia explore which English phrases are most misleading for non-native speakers learning the language.
He's Just Quite Ill.summary: Eugenia, an English language learner from Córdoba, Argentina, joins Conan for a delightful exploration of the absurdities and pitfalls embedded in everyday English. The episode title itself — 'He's Just Quite Ill' — becomes the centerpiece: a classic British understatement that Eugenia finds baffling because in Spanish, severity is expressed directly rather than softened into near-euphemism. Conan, who delights in the strangeness of his own language when viewed from the outside, leans into this dynamic immediately, asking Eugenia to walk him through which phrases sent her to a dictionary only to find the definition made things worse. The conversation becomes a comedic linguistics class as they catalog English idioms that are treacherous for learners — phrases where the literal meaning and the social meaning sit miles apart. Conan confesses that native speakers navigate these landmines unconsciously, which delights Eugenia. She shares specific moments of embarrassment or confusion from her learning journey, and Conan responds with genuine curiosity and exaggerated horror at his own language's contradictions. There's warmth throughout: Eugenia is articulate, funny, and self-deprecating about her English, while Conan finds endless material in the idea that English is somehow broken by design. By the end, both seem to agree that the language rewards learners who embrace its contradictions rather than fight them.

highlights_v2.title: The Understatement That Broke Eugenia
highlights_v2.summary: Eugenia explains to Conan why 'He's just quite ill' threw her completely when she encountered it — in Spanish, you say what you mean. Conan marvels that native English speakers deploy this phrase without a second thought, even when someone is gravely sick. The exchange turns into a back-and-forth about English's strange relationship with honesty.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You say 'quite ill' and I'm thinking, okay, maybe he has a little cold — but no, he is in the hospital!

highlights_v2.title: Idioms That Betray Learners
highlights_v2.summary: Eugenia walks Conan through a catalog of English phrases that made her learning journey treacherous — expressions where the dictionary definition leads you straight into a social disaster. Conan begins trying to explain the logic behind them and keeps failing, eventually admitting that English speakers are just as confused but refuse to show it.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: There is no logic — I just accepted that and things got much better.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Defends The Indefensible Language
highlights_v2.summary: When Eugenia presses Conan on why English speakers mask meaning with softening phrases, Conan attempts a mock defense of English as a language of 'nuance and sophistication' before immediately undercutting himself. The bit escalates as Conan concedes that perhaps the language was designed by people who hated clarity, which Eugenia endorses completely.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: We built a language that actively fights against you understanding it — and we're very proud of that.

highlights: Youssef from Egypt shares what it's like to be a Christian voice actor in a Muslim-majority country.
Conan explores the world of Egyptian voice acting and Youssef's rural life outside the city.
Youssef's warm, easygoing personality earns him the 'amiable' label despite his dramatic job title.
The Amiable Assassin.summary: Youssef, a voice actor living outside the city in Egypt, brings a rare combination of professional intrigue and personal candor to his conversation with Conan. The episode title plays on the paradox at the heart of Youssef's identity: his warm, disarming personality sits in comic contrast with the menacing characters he frequently voices for animation and media. Conan is immediately drawn in by this contradiction and spends considerable time exploring what it actually sounds like when Youssef shifts into character — the gap between the man on the phone and the villain in the recording booth. Beyond the craft of voice acting, Youssef opens up about living as a Christian in Egypt, a predominantly Muslim country. Rather than treating this as a source of conflict, Youssef describes his experience with nuance — belonging to a minority faith while feeling genuinely at home in his community and country. Conan navigates this topic with care and genuine curiosity, asking about the textures of daily life rather than reaching for easy narratives. The conversation about Youssef's rural life outside the city adds another layer: he describes the particular rhythms of Egyptian life away from Cairo, the relationship with neighbors, the landscape. The episode is a portrait of someone who defies expectation at every turn, and Conan clearly relishes finding another person whose life doesn't fit a simple category.

highlights_v2.title: Villain Voice From A Gentle Man
highlights_v2.summary: Conan asks Youssef to demonstrate the characters he voices, and the contrast between Youssef's naturally warm telephone voice and the menacing register he shifts into for animated villains produces an immediate comedic jolt. Conan reacts with theatrical alarm, insisting the voice came from a completely different person.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: That was not you. That was someone else who briefly possessed you.

highlights_v2.title: Christian In A Muslim Country
highlights_v2.summary: Youssef describes his experience as a member of Egypt's Coptic Christian community with an ease and warmth that surprises Conan. Rather than cataloging conflict, Youssef talks about the texture of belonging — shared neighborhoods, mutual respect, holidays that coexist — giving Conan a picture of complexity that defies the expected narrative.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: People ask me if it's difficult, and I have to think — it's just my life, and mostly it's a good one.

highlights_v2.title: Rural Egyptian Life Explained
highlights_v2.summary: Youssef paints a picture of his life outside the city that contrasts sharply with the Cairo that most outsiders imagine when they think of Egypt. He describes neighbors, quieter rhythms, and a different relationship with space and community. Conan, fascinated, keeps asking follow-up questions that reveal how little Western audiences know about Egyptian life beyond its famous landmarks.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Cairo is its own world — where I live, it feels like a different country entirely.

highlights: David from Colombia reveals the dangerous realities of his profession alongside his love of death metal.
Conan and David trade surprising fun facts about Colombia that defy common outsider assumptions.
David's dual identity as a professional in a risky field and a death metal fan delights Conan.
Fun Facts About Colombia.summary: David from Colombia arrives as one of the more surprising fan combinations Conan has encountered: a professional working in a genuinely dangerous field who spends his downtime listening to death metal. The tension between these identities — the serious, risk-laden work and the thundering escapism of extreme music — gives Conan a rich vein of material and David proves a confident, witty guide through both. The episode's structure bounces between the professional and personal with Conan steering each transition. When David describes the occupational hazards of his work, Conan reacts with exaggerated concern before pivoting to probe the death metal connection — why that genre specifically, what it provides, and how his colleagues react when they find out. This leads into the 'fun facts about Colombia' thread, where David takes visible pleasure in dismantling outsider assumptions about his country. Colombia's representation in foreign media has long been dominated by a narrow set of narratives, and David relishes the chance to replace those with surprising, specific, affectionate truths about Colombian culture, geography, and daily life. Conan, who responds well to being corrected and educated, lets David hold the floor and genuinely engages with each revelation. The episode builds toward an affectionate portrait of a country that consistently defies the image the world has assigned it, delivered by someone equally resistant to simple categorization.

highlights_v2.title: Death Metal And Dangerous Work
highlights_v2.summary: David reveals that after long days in a high-risk profession, his decompression of choice is death metal — a combination that startles Conan into a string of follow-up questions. David explains the logic with surprising clarity: extreme music provides a release that softer genres simply cannot. Conan finds this entirely reasonable and says so, while still mining it for comedy.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: After what I see at work, I don't want something calm — I want something that screams back at the world.

highlights_v2.title: Colombia Facts That Destroy Stereotypes
highlights_v2.summary: David walks Conan through a series of Colombia facts that directly contradict the images most Americans hold. Each revelation — about biodiversity, city culture, or social transformation — lands with satisfying force, and Conan responds with genuine surprise rather than polite noises. By the third or fourth entry, Conan starts guessing and keeps getting it wrong.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Everything you think you know is outdated by about twenty years.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Confronts His Own Assumptions
highlights_v2.summary: When David's Colombia corrections stack up, Conan pauses to inventory his own preconceptions and admits most of them came from movies or news coverage from before David was born. The moment of media-literacy honesty becomes a running bit where Conan keeps discovering that his mental map of Colombia is wildly out of date.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I formed my entire understanding of your country from films shot in the 1990s and I am so sorry.

highlights: Florencia from Argentina explains her guided yoga practice and how she tailors sessions to clients.
Conan learns about spatchcocking, an Argentine culinary specialty that gives the episode its memorable title.
The conversation veers into local Argentine food culture and Florencia's life as a yoga instructor.
Spatchcocking.summary: Florencia, a yoga instructor from Argentina, sits at the intersection of two subjects Conan finds equally absurd and fascinating: guided mindfulness practice and the aggressively physical culinary technique of spatchcocking. The episode opens with Florencia explaining her yoga work — how she reads clients, tailors sessions, and navigates the reality that people come to yoga carrying stress they can't always name. Conan, who presents himself as constitutionally unsuited to stillness, peppers her with questions about what happens when a student is impossible to relax. The conversation's pivot point arrives with spatchcocking: Florencia describes this Argentine culinary method — removing a bird's backbone to flatten it for faster, more even cooking — and Conan immediately recognizes that the word itself is the bit. The contrast between the serenity of yoga and the primal satisfaction of spatchcocking becomes the episode's comic spine. Conan digs into Argentine food culture with genuine appetite, asking about local ingredients, family traditions around cooking, and how asado fits into social life. Florencia navigates both halves of the conversation with warmth and dry humor, clearly comfortable with the absurdity of discussing spiritual practice and butchery in the same breath. The episode closes with both agreeing that there is perhaps more in common between a good yoga session and a perfectly spatchcocked bird than first appears — both require commitment, proper technique, and someone willing to apply firm pressure.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Encounters Spatchcocking
highlights_v2.summary: When Florencia introduces spatchcocking as part of Argentine culinary culture, Conan's reaction to the word alone derails the conversation for a full minute. He demands a thorough explanation, receives one, and then makes Florencia repeat it several times under the guise of wanting to be sure he's understood correctly. The word's phonetic comedy proves irresistible.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I just need you to say that word one more time. For clarity. That's all it is.

highlights_v2.title: Yoga For The Unrelaxable
highlights_v2.summary: Florencia describes the particular challenge of guiding students who arrive at yoga so wound up that the first twenty minutes of class are a losing battle. Conan immediately identifies himself as this type of student and asks what Florencia would do with him. Her answer — diplomatic but pointed — suggests he would require extensive patience and possibly a private session.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Some people, you can see them fighting the mat — they are not ready to surrender to peace.

highlights_v2.title: Argentine Food Culture Deep Dive
highlights_v2.summary: Conan steers into Argentine food traditions beyond spatchcocking, asking about asado's social role, family customs around the grill, and what a Sunday meal actually looks like. Florencia shifts into enthusiastic cultural ambassador mode, describing the rituals around Argentine cooking with the kind of specific, loving detail that makes Conan increasingly hungry and increasingly envious.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The asado is not just food — it's the reason everyone comes, and everyone stays for hours.

highlights: Kassem from Beirut describes a workplace romance that developed in unexpected circumstances.
Kassem shares his passion for boxing and what draws him to the sport in Lebanon.
Conan probes Kassem's worst fear, producing a revealing and comedic exchange.
Ink-A-Dink.summary: Kassem from Beirut brings a personality that immediately earns the 'amiable' energy Conan typically draws out of his fans, combined with topics — workplace romance, boxing, and fears — that let the conversation move from light to surprisingly candid. The episode opens with Kassem's workplace romance, which develops as a story with genuine arc: circumstances that made the situation unusual, the awkward navigation required, and the outcome. Conan, who finds workplace dynamics endlessly ripe for comedy, asks probing questions about the logistics and risks involved, and Kassem delivers with specificity that makes the story land. The boxing segment gives the episode its physical comedy dimension. Kassem describes his passion for the sport — what drew him to it, what he loves about training, and how boxing culture in Lebanon differs from the global image of the sport. Conan imagines Kassem in the ring with theatrical appreciation, probably making several jokes about his own inability to throw a punch. The final thread — Kassem's worst fear — is where the episode earns its emotional resonance. What begins as a setup for a punchline reveals something more genuine, and Conan's response balances comedy with actual attentiveness. Beirut's particular recent history hovers in the background of the conversation without overwhelming it — Kassem's resilience and humor are the foreground, and Conan honors that by not pushing the episode into harder territory than Kassem chooses to take it.

highlights_v2.title: The Workplace Romance Revealed
highlights_v2.summary: Kassem walks Conan through the full arc of a workplace romance that developed under circumstances that made it particularly complicated to navigate. The story has genuine dramatic structure — beginning, escalating tension, and an outcome Conan clearly didn't predict — and Kassem tells it with timing that suggests he's gotten good at the telling.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You try to be professional but at some point you are just a person and the person wins.

highlights_v2.title: Boxing In Beirut
highlights_v2.summary: Kassem describes his commitment to boxing with the kind of specificity that separates genuine passion from casual enthusiasm — the training regimen, the mental discipline, what the sport gives him that other exercise cannot. Conan responds by exploring what boxing culture looks like in Lebanon, a question Kassem answers with pride and some affectionate correction of outside assumptions.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Boxing is not about fighting — it's about control, and that's what people don't understand.

highlights_v2.title: Kassem's Worst Fear Surfaces
highlights_v2.summary: What starts as Conan's standard comedic probe into a fan's deepest fears turns unexpectedly candid when Kassem answers with genuine honesty rather than a quip. The fear he describes is specific and personal, and Conan's response shifts register accordingly — the comedy doesn't disappear but the attentiveness that makes Conan good at this format comes to the front.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I thought I'd make a joke but when you ask it like that — it's something I actually think about.

highlights: Dee, a hair stylist in Singapore, discusses the unique dating scene and culture of the city-state.
Conan enlists Dee's professional expertise to talk about his own famously unruly hair.
The episode takes a detour into Conan's long-lost leather jacket, which Dee may have a connection to.
Singapore Sona.summary: Dee, a hair stylist working in Singapore, gives Conan three distinct threads to pull and he pulls all of them with evident pleasure. The professional conversation about hair styling takes an immediate left turn toward Conan's own famous red quiff — Dee, as a working stylist, is better positioned than most fans to render an authoritative professional opinion, and she does. Conan receives this assessment with a combination of defensiveness and genuine curiosity about what could theoretically be done. The episode's Singapore dating scene segment covers terrain that clearly fascinates Conan: how a dense, multicultural city-state with a reputation for orderliness actually navigates romance, dating apps, and the peculiar dynamics of a small geographic footprint where you constantly run into everyone. Dee provides a specific, funny account of what this actually looks like in practice. The wildcard is the leather jacket. Conan's missing leather jacket has become a recurring bit across the podcast, and the revelation that Dee may have some connection to it — or that the conversation takes a turn in that direction — gives the episode a mystery-caper quality that Conan leans into fully. Whether the jacket is found, confirmed missing, or simply exists as a comedic ghost in the background, the thread lets Conan perform mock-outrage and detective work in equal measure. Dee proves an excellent counterpart throughout — sharp, funny, and unbothered by Conan's theatrical spiraling.

highlights_v2.title: Hair Stylist Judges Conan's Hair
highlights_v2.summary: Dee, speaking as a licensed professional, gives Conan a frank assessment of his signature hairstyle that manages to be simultaneously diplomatic and devastating. Conan presses for specifics — what would she do differently, where does he go wrong — and Dee delivers a genuine technical answer that both flatters and indicts his current approach.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You have the raw material to work with — what happens to it after that is the question.

highlights_v2.title: Singapore Dating Scene Decoded
highlights_v2.summary: Dee breaks down the specific dynamics of dating in Singapore — a city-state so compact that social circles collapse into each other, making dating apps a different experience than in larger cities. She describes the quirks of multicultural dating culture, the social expectations at play, and what makes Singapore's romantic landscape genuinely distinct from the Western template.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: When the country is this small, everyone knows someone you've dated — you have to be very strategic.

highlights_v2.title: The Mystery Of The Leather Jacket
highlights_v2.summary: Conan's ongoing lament about his long-lost leather jacket resurfaces and Dee's connection to the story — whether as a lead, a witness, or simply the recipient of Conan's elaborate conspiracy theory — gives the bit new momentum. Conan performs mock detective urgency while Dee maintains an expression of patient amusement at the depth of his commitment to this particular grievance.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I am following every thread on this jacket, and the thread has led me to Singapore.

highlights: Tara, a university lecturer in Bangkok, reveals that listening to Conan's podcast helped her find love.
Conan is visibly delighted to learn his comedy indirectly served as a matchmaking tool.
Tara's life as an expat academic in Thailand gives Conan plenty of material to riff on.
Let Him Do His Bits.summary: Tara, a university lecturer living in Bangkok, arrives with a story that immediately transforms Conan from interviewer into the subject of his own examination: she met her partner because of him. Specifically, it was Conan's podcast — his comedy, his bits, his willingness to be vulnerable and absurd — that brought two people into conversation, then connection, then love. The revelation hits Conan with the particular force that genuine unexpected praise always does, and he spends a good portion of the episode cycling through disbelief, delight, and mock-proprietary claims on the relationship. Tara is warm but clear-eyed about what actually happened: she and her partner bonded over a shared love of Conan's work, and that shared reference point became an entry into knowing each other. The episode title — 'Let Him Do His Bits' — captures what Tara apparently told her partner at some point, an instruction to give Conan space to perform rather than interrupt. This becomes a running joke about Conan's need for an audience that will let him operate. The Bangkok setting gives Conan additional material: Tara's life as an expat academic in Thailand, the particular culture of Thai universities, and the experience of building a life in a city known for its sensory intensity. Tara describes the expat community, her work with students, and the rhythms of Bangkok life with the specificity of someone who has genuinely settled rather than simply relocated. By the end, the episode is both a celebration of accidental connection and a gentle roast of Conan's self-image as matchmaker.

highlights_v2.title: Conan The Accidental Matchmaker
highlights_v2.summary: Tara reveals that her relationship began over a shared love of Conan's podcast, and Conan's reaction moves through several stages: disbelief, theatrical pride, and finally a proprietary claim on the couple's future. He begins planning what he'll say at the wedding before Tara can finish explaining how it actually happened.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I am responsible for this relationship and I will be expecting some acknowledgment going forward.

highlights_v2.title: Let Him Do His Bits Explained
highlights_v2.summary: Tara explains the origin of the episode's title — an instruction she apparently gave her partner about giving Conan room to run with a comedic thread. Conan is both touched and alarmed that this instruction exists, then leans into it by announcing that from now on this is his official podcast request of all listeners.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I just told him — stop interrupting, let him do his bits. He needs to finish the bit.

highlights_v2.title: Academic Life In Bangkok
highlights_v2.summary: Tara describes what university lecturing in Bangkok actually involves — the students she works with, the cultural differences in how Thai students engage with academic authority, and the particular rhythm of expat life in a city that never quite settles down. Conan is fascinated by the details of how foreign academics navigate Thai academic culture.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: My students are brilliant and they challenge me every day, just not always in the way a Western classroom would.

highlights: Waleson from São Paulo breaks down the challenges and absurdities of professional translation work.
Conan shares his favorite controversial historical figure, sparking a spirited discussion with Waleson.
The two explore what gets genuinely 'lost in translation' and which concepts resist crossing language barriers.
Lost In Translation.summary: Waleson, a professional translator from São Paulo, arrives as one of the more conceptually rich guests Conan encounters — someone whose entire job is to find the seam where one language ends and another begins, and to decide what lives on either side. The conversation opens on the practical realities of translation work: Waleson describes the types of projects he handles, the clients who are grateful when meaning survives the crossing, and the ones who argue with him about words they don't speak. Conan, who is fascinated by language, asks genuinely probing questions about whether translation is ultimately possible or whether something always escapes. This leads to a meditation on which concepts genuinely resist crossing language barriers — words that carry cultural cargo that a direct translation can't unpack. Waleson offers specific examples and Conan adds his own guesses, most of which are wrong in entertaining ways. The episode's wildcard comes from Conan's disclosure of his favorite controversial historical figure — a reveal that Waleson receives with the careful diplomatic neutrality of someone trained to process other people's statements without immediate personal reaction. Conan's choice, and Waleson's eventual response to it, forms the episode's most revealing exchange. The São Paulo background adds texture: Waleson describes navigating a megacity where the demand for translation services reflects the city's extraordinary cultural density. The episode ends feeling like a genuine intellectual conversation rather than a comedy interview that happened to include smart content.

highlights_v2.title: What Translation Cannot Cross
highlights_v2.summary: Waleson describes specific concepts and words that genuinely resist translation — not because of technical difficulty but because they carry cultural meaning that can't be transferred without a paragraph of footnotes. Conan begins guessing which concepts escape English and keeps landing on examples Waleson has already dealt with professionally, which validates his choices more than expected.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Some words are not words — they are a whole way of seeing, compressed into a sound.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's Controversial Historical Figure
highlights_v2.summary: Conan reveals his favorite controversial historical figure and Waleson's response is a masterclass in diplomatic processing — the careful pause, the measured engagement, the professional instinct to translate rather than react. When Waleson does offer his own view, it's specific and unexpected, and Conan discovers he has underestimated his guest's willingness to have opinions.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I wasn't expecting that — but actually, if you think about it from a certain angle, I understand why.

highlights_v2.title: Arguing With Translation Clients
highlights_v2.summary: Waleson describes the particular frustration of translation clients who dispute his word choices in a language they do not speak. Conan immediately recognizes the comedy and begins doing an impression of such a client — insisting on a different word for reasons that have nothing to do with accuracy — which Waleson confirms is an almost perfect reconstruction of real interactions.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: They hired me because I know the language, and then they argue with me about the language.

highlights: Avril, a reality TV producer in Dublin, explains the chaotic behind-the-scenes world of Irish dating shows.
Conan is put on the spot as Avril helps him craft a hypothetical online dating profile.
The episode dives into the peculiar quirks of the Irish dating app experience versus American norms.
Swipeout.summary: Avril, a reality TV producer working in Dublin, arrives with professional credentials that position her as both a comedy target and a genuine expert — someone who has spent considerable time engineering the conditions for romantic disaster and then filming it. Conan, delighted by this, immediately wants to understand the machinery: how reality dating shows actually function, what producers do to generate tension, how much of what appears onscreen reflects reality versus careful construction. Avril navigates these questions with the practiced candor of someone who has answered them before and has concluded that honesty is more interesting than defensiveness. The Irish dating app segment gives the episode its cultural texture. Avril describes the peculiarities of swiping in Ireland — a country small enough that app users frequently encounter people they already know, went to school with, or are connected to through family networks spanning multiple generations. This creates a romantic landscape that is simultaneously intimate and awkward in ways that don't translate directly to larger cultures. Conan, clearly fascinated, begins asking about scenarios that Avril confirms happen regularly. The episode's comedic peak arrives when Avril turns the tables and begins helping Conan construct a hypothetical dating profile — selecting photos, drafting bio language, and making recommendations about what to lead with and what to bury. Conan submits to this exercise with theatrical reluctance and genuine absorption, occasionally disagreeing with Avril's professional recommendations in ways that reveal more about him than he intends.

highlights_v2.title: Inside Reality TV Production
highlights_v2.summary: Avril describes the producer's role in a reality dating show with a candor that punctures the fantasy — the deliberate construction of scenarios, the editing that reshapes timelines, the decisions made in post-production that determine what story the audience receives. Conan responds by naming specific conventions of the genre and asking Avril to confirm or deny each one as manufactured.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You're not capturing chemistry — you're engineering the conditions where chemistry becomes possible, and sometimes that involves some help.

highlights_v2.title: Irish Dating App Peculiarities
highlights_v2.summary: Avril explains why dating apps function differently in Ireland than in larger countries — the social network is dense enough that every potential match comes pre-annotated with connections, histories, and family ties. The impossibility of genuine anonymity produces a dating culture that is both more socially careful and more dramatically explosive when things go wrong.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You swipe right and discover he's your mother's neighbor's son — and now your mother knows.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's Hypothetical Dating Profile
highlights_v2.summary: Avril the reality TV producer takes professional control of the conversation and begins workshopping a dating profile for Conan — selecting which qualities to lead with, which to minimize, and what photos to use. Conan objects to most of her recommendations, which Avril absorbs with the patient professionalism of someone used to working with difficult talent.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I would not lead with the television career — it sets expectations that are hard to manage in person.

highlights: Jack, a charity worker, connects with Conan about the rewards and frustrations of nonprofit work.
Conan and Jack explore what motivates someone to dedicate their career to charitable causes.
The episode title's proclamation style hints at a royal or civic theme Jack brings to the conversation.
Hear Ye Hear Ye!.summary: Jack, a charity worker from Abingdon in Oxfordshire, England, brings the particular combination of genuine civic commitment and English self-deprecation that Conan finds irresistible. The episode title's proclamation style — medieval herald announcing something of civic importance — fits both Abingdon's historic character as an ancient English market town and the nature of Jack's work, which involves making public cases for causes that require champions. The conversation about charity work explores territory that this segment rarely reaches: the practical realities of nonprofit life, not just the nobility of the mission. Jack describes the gap between what motivates someone to enter charitable work and what the day-to-day actually involves — the fundraising asks, the bureaucratic friction, the donors who attach conditions, the moments when the work feels genuinely meaningful versus the moments when it feels like paperwork with good intentions. Conan responds to this honesty with appreciation, having clearly been expecting easier inspiration-speak. The Abingdon setting provides cultural material: the town's history, its relationship to the broader Thames Valley, and what English market-town life actually looks like in the twenty-first century. Jack describes his community with the particular wry affection that characterizes English people talking about English places — acknowledging both the charm and the strangeness of living somewhere that considers itself unremarkable while sitting inside layers of history. The episode has a warmth that comes from Conan meeting someone who chose purpose over ambition and is neither self-congratulatory nor self-pitying about it.

highlights_v2.title: Charity Work's Unglamorous Reality
highlights_v2.summary: Jack gives Conan an honest account of what nonprofit work involves beyond the inspiring mission statement — the grant applications, the difficult donor conversations, the organizational politics of allocating limited resources. Conan, who expected more straightforward inspiration, responds to the candor with evident respect and several specific follow-up questions about the friction points.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The mission is beautiful — but the mission still needs a spreadsheet and a board that agrees on the numbers.

highlights_v2.title: Life In An Ancient English Town
highlights_v2.summary: Jack describes Abingdon — one of England's oldest market towns — with the dry affection of someone who has thought carefully about what it means to live somewhere that history settled into and never entirely left. Conan responds by romanticizing the idea of ancient English town life, which Jack gently corrects by describing what Monday mornings actually look like.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: It's a very old town that mostly gets on with being a very normal town, which is perhaps the most English thing about it.

highlights_v2.title: What Motivates A Charity Worker
highlights_v2.summary: Conan presses Jack on the origin story — what sent him toward charity work rather than a better-paying path — and Jack's answer is both honest and specific enough to avoid generic inspiration. He names the moment or circumstance that tilted him in this direction, and Conan's response suggests he finds the specificity more moving than a polished mission statement would have been.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I didn't choose it because it was noble — I chose it because it was the thing I felt I could actually do.

highlights: Moataz from Alexandria, Egypt met his wife entirely through an online video game and married her virtually.
Conan is fascinated and amused by the full story of Moataz's in-game courtship and virtual wedding ceremony.
The episode explores how Moataz's romantic story defied convention and crossed digital and real-world boundaries.
Conapotamia.summary: Moataz from Alexandria, Egypt holds the distinction of having one of the most genuinely unusual love stories in the podcast's history: he met his wife entirely through an online video game and they got married — in a ceremony conducted inside the virtual world. The title 'Conapotamia' blends Conan's name with Mesopotamia, the ancient cradle of civilization, placing the host at the center of a new origin myth while also gesturing at Moataz's Egyptian location and the ancient resonances of the region. Conan's reaction to the full story unfolds across the episode: initial disbelief, a battery of logistics questions, and eventually a kind of genuine wonder at what it means to build a relationship, and then a life, across the digital-physical boundary. The courtship sequence is the episode's dramatic core — Moataz describes how in-game interaction evolved into something more serious, the challenges of maintaining a relationship across digital and real-world registers, and the decision to formalize it with a virtual ceremony. Conan, who could simply play this for absurdism, instead treats it with a mix of comedy and respect. He asks about the wedding in detail: what happened, who attended (avatars? real people via video?), how it was officiated, and whether it felt real. Moataz's answers consistently disrupt easy categorization — the relationship is genuine, the connection profound, the geography of where it happened less important than the reality of what grew there. The episode ends as a meditation on where human connection lives and whether the vessel it travels through determines its validity.

highlights_v2.title: The In-Game Courtship Explained
highlights_v2.summary: Moataz walks Conan through how an online video game became the setting for meeting his future wife — from initial in-game interaction to the slow escalation of something more personal. Conan asks the logistical questions most people would think: what game, what circumstances, when did it stop being about the game. Moataz's answers are specific and surprisingly romantic.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: At some point the game was the excuse — we were both there for the conversation.

highlights_v2.title: The Virtual Wedding Ceremony
highlights_v2.summary: Conan's reaction to the virtual wedding ceremony escalates through several phases: confusion about logistics, genuine curiosity about what it felt like, and finally a kind of respect for the commitment the choice represented. Moataz describes the ceremony in detail — who officiated, what happened, how the real-world relationship followed — and Conan finds it increasingly hard to maintain the bit of treating it as absurd.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I was nervous — more nervous than I expected to be for something that was also a game, and less like a game than I expected.

highlights_v2.title: Where Real Relationships Live
highlights_v2.summary: The episode's philosophical turn comes when Conan stops treating the story as unusual and starts asking what it actually means — whether the medium through which two people meet shapes the relationship they build. Moataz's answer, grounded in the specific reality of his marriage, becomes the episode's most memorable exchange: a defense of digital connection made by someone whose life proves it.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: She is my wife — not my virtual wife. She is my wife, full stop.

highlights: Ellen from Tullamore juggles law school while working at a whiskey distillery in the Irish midlands.
Conan bonds with Ellen over Irish whiskey heritage and confesses how he personally takes his dram.
The family connection referenced in the title points to a generational tie to the distillery business.
Keepin' It In The Family.summary: Ellen from Tullamore in the Irish midlands is doing two things simultaneously that Conan finds independently impressive and collectively delightful: she is studying law and working at a whiskey distillery. The episode's title — 'Keepin' It In The Family' — points at the generational connection to the distillery business that makes Ellen's position there something more than just a job. Tullamore is home to Tullamore D.E.W., one of Ireland's most recognized whiskey brands, and Ellen's family tie to that tradition gives the conversation an authenticity that Conan leans into immediately. The law school thread explores the particular challenge of studying for a demanding professional qualification while working — the time management, the exhaustion, the way law school thinking begins to colonize daily life. Ellen describes the combination with the matter-of-fact energy of someone who has stopped finding it remarkable and started finding it just Tuesday. Conan, who romanticizes the idea of someone pursuing two serious things at once, presses her on why both rather than one. The whiskey education segment is where the episode finds its most specific comedy: Conan asks how Ellen takes her dram, which leads to a tutorial that Conan receives with the solemnity of someone determined to do whiskey correctly. Ellen's expertise — the distillery vocabulary, the proper method, the regional differences in Irish whiskey culture — gets translated through Conan into increasingly elaborate attempts to sound knowledgeable. The family dimension, when it emerges, adds emotional weight: the tradition of craft passed across generations, the particular pride of working in a building your family helped build.

highlights_v2.title: Law School And Distillery Work
highlights_v2.summary: Ellen describes the daily reality of balancing law school coursework with shifts at a whiskey distillery — a combination that Conan finds both exhausting to contemplate and somehow very Irish. She explains what each demands, how she manages the transition between the two worlds, and what happens when the skills bleed into each other in unexpected directions.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I've started thinking of the distillation process in terms of legal arguments — both require patience and getting the steps exactly right.

highlights_v2.title: How Conan Takes His Dram
highlights_v2.summary: Ellen gives Conan a whiskey education — how to assess, hold, and drink Irish whiskey properly — and Conan receives this instruction with theatrical seriousness before revealing his actual drinking approach, which Ellen diplomatically describes as needing work. The tutorial escalates as Conan attempts to demonstrate his understanding and Ellen gently corrects each step.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: That is technically drinking whiskey, yes — but we would probably do a few things differently.

highlights_v2.title: Family Legacy At The Distillery
highlights_v2.summary: Ellen describes the generational connection her family has to the Tullamore distillery — a history that transforms her current work from employment into continuation of something older. Conan, who responds strongly to inherited craft and family tradition, asks specifically about what it feels like to work in a building shaped by that history and Ellen's answer has genuine warmth.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: There are parts of the building where I know someone in my family stood before me — and that matters more than I expected it to.

highlights: Simon from Slovenia introduces Conan to a specialty coffee shop located inside an unusual underground museum.
Conan is charmed by the niche Slovenian coffee culture Simon describes with genuine enthusiasm.
The conversation reveals how Slovenia punches well above its size in the specialty coffee world.
Underground Museum Coffee.summary: Simon, a specialty coffee enthusiast from Ljubljana, Slovenia, calls in to introduce Conan to one of the most unusual coffee shop setups he's ever encountered: a specialty cafe located inside an underground museum in the Slovenian capital. The episode opens with Conan expressing genuine surprise that Slovenia has a distinctive coffee culture at all, given that the country rarely registers in American pop-culture consciousness. Simon explains with obvious pride that Ljubljana's coffee scene is quietly elite, drawing in travelers and locals alike who seek out single-origin brews and meticulous pour-over technique. The underground museum setting becomes a comedic thread throughout — Conan keeps circling back to the absurdity of stopping to smell an Ethiopian pour-over between exhibits on Slovenian medieval history. Simon patiently explains that the pairing is actually quite normal in Ljubljana's cultural scene, where food and art intermingle naturally. Conan's reaction oscillates between mockery and genuine admiration, eventually admitting that the concept sounds better than most coffee shops he's visited in major American cities. The conversation also touches on Slovenia's small size and how it consistently outperforms expectations in food and drink culture, with Simon noting that the country has more specialty roasters per capita than many of its neighbors. Conan ends the episode by making Simon promise he'll mail him a bag of Slovenian specialty coffee, a joke that lands warmly and closes the episode on an affectionate, curious note.

highlights_v2.title: Museum Basement Coffee Shop
highlights_v2.summary: Simon describes a specialty coffee shop tucked inside a Ljubljana underground museum, a concept Conan struggles to process. Conan jokes that in America the closest equivalent is a vending machine outside a gift shop. Simon insists the quality is genuinely world-class, not a tourist gimmick.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You're telling me the best espresso in the city is next to a display case of Roman artifacts?

highlights_v2.title: Slovenia's Secret Coffee Dominance
highlights_v2.summary: Simon makes the case that Slovenia punches far above its weight in specialty coffee culture, citing the density of quality roasters relative to the country's small population. Conan responds with exaggerated disbelief, then genuine curiosity, asking why no one talks about this. Simon attributes it to Slovenian modesty.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: We don't brag. We just quietly drink better coffee than most of Europe.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Demands Coffee by Mail
highlights_v2.summary: Near the end of the episode Conan announces he wants Simon to mail him a bag of Slovenian specialty coffee, pitching it as a reasonable international transaction. Simon laughs and tries to explain customs regulations while Conan waves them off as bureaucratic obstacles to great coffee. The bit ends with a handshake-style verbal agreement.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Whatever customs says, just put 'friendship' on the label and I'll handle the rest.

highlights: Allan, a crime scene investigator in Missouri, describes the most surprising scenes she has investigated.
Conan turns the tables and asks Allan to assess which member of his team would make the best CSI.
Allan walks Conan through the less glamorous realities of crime scene work that TV shows omit.
C.S.I. Kansas City.summary: Allan, a crime scene investigator based in Kansas City, Missouri, gives Conan a frank and often surprising look at what the job actually involves, starting with the most startling scenes she has worked and ending with Conan demanding she assess his team's criminal investigation potential. The episode begins with Conan doing his best CSI: Miami impression before Allan gently corrects essentially every assumption he had about the work. She details the unglamorous logistics of real crime scene investigation — the paperwork, the waiting, the smell — compared to the slick sixty-minute resolutions TV shows portray. Conan is visibly delighted by each myth she debunks, prodding her for increasingly specific examples of scenes that defied expectation. Allan describes a case involving what appeared to be a routine scene that turned out to be far more elaborate, threading the needle between giving real detail and protecting case confidentiality. The comedy peaks when Conan pivots to asking which member of his team — himself, Matt Gourley, or Sona Movsesian — would make the most skilled crime scene investigator. Allan approaches this with the seriousness of a professional assessment, walking through each candidate's likely strengths and weaknesses. Her verdict, delivered with a straight face, produces an audible reaction from Conan. The episode balances genuine educational content about a misunderstood profession with the comedic tension of Conan being told he would make a terrible investigator. Allan comes across as sharp, dry, and completely unintimidated by Conan's goofiness.

highlights_v2.title: TV vs. Real Crime Scenes
highlights_v2.summary: Allan systematically dismantles every forensic trope Conan references from CSI and similar shows. She explains that real investigations involve extensive waiting, documentation, and odors that television carefully omits. Conan's reaction shifts from entertained to mildly horrified as the glamour dissolves.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The show wraps in an hour. My paperwork alone takes longer than that.

highlights_v2.title: Allan's Most Surprising Scene
highlights_v2.summary: Allan describes a case that appeared routine on arrival but revealed unexpected complexity once she began processing the scene. She carefully navigates case confidentiality while still conveying why it stood out. Conan listens with unusual attentiveness, following up with specific questions rather than pivoting to jokes.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You walk in thinking you know what happened. You almost never do.

highlights_v2.title: Rating Conan's Team as Investigators
highlights_v2.summary: Conan asks Allan to rank him, Matt Gourley, and Sona Movsesian by likely effectiveness as CSIs. Allan takes this seriously, running through each person's observable traits as if conducting a personnel evaluation. Her deadpan conclusion about Conan's unsuitability becomes the comedic high point of the episode.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I'd say Sona has the instincts. Conan would contaminate the scene within thirty seconds.

highlights: Carles, a video game designer, gives Conan an insider look at what the job actually involves day-to-day.
Conan is challenged to invent a drag persona for himself, producing an outrageous and specific answer.
Carles and Conan bond over the creative process behind game design and Conan's gaming history.
Red Ass-fault.summary: Carles, a video game designer from Spain, calls in to talk shop about the day-to-day realities of working in the games industry, and the conversation quickly escalates when Conan is challenged to invent a drag persona for himself. The episode opens with Carles explaining what a game designer actually does — which is considerably more mundane than most people imagine, involving spreadsheets, balance testing, and player psychology rather than sitting in a cool chair making explosions happen. Conan is initially surprised by the analytical nature of the work, having assumed designers spent their days playing games for fun. Carles corrects this warmly but firmly, describing the grinding iteration cycles behind even a simple game mechanic. The conversation pivots into Conan's gaming history, where Conan admits to some embarrassingly dated references that Carles generously engages with rather than mocking. The two bond over what makes a game emotionally compelling versus what makes it merely technically impressive. The episode's comedic centerpiece arrives when Carles asks Conan to design his own drag persona — name, aesthetic, and signature move. Conan commits fully to the bit, producing a detailed and specific drag alter ego with a name that sounds like it was workshopped by a team of professional comedians. Carles responds with enthusiastic feedback, suggesting what kind of stage presence this persona would need to work. The playfulness of the drag conversation balances nicely against the more substantive career discussion, giving the episode a satisfying dual-track structure. Carles comes across as creative, thoughtful, and a perfect sparring partner for Conan's improvisational instincts.

highlights_v2.title: Game Design's Unglamorous Reality
highlights_v2.summary: Carles explains that professional game design involves far more spreadsheet work and statistical balancing than the public imagines. Conan is genuinely surprised that playtesting a mechanic hundreds of times is a core part of the job. The conversation demystifies an industry often seen through the lens of its finished, polished products.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The fun part takes five percent of the time. The rest is asking why something feels wrong.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Invents His Drag Persona
highlights_v2.summary: Carles asks Conan to design a drag alter ego — complete with name, look, and signature move. Conan leans in completely, producing a persona that is simultaneously ridiculous and oddly specific. Carles evaluates the concept with genuine creative engagement, suggesting the persona would need a stronger entrance.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: She's called Crimson O'Brien. She enters to a foghorn. It's very niche.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's Embarrassingly Old Gaming Taste
highlights_v2.summary: Conan reveals his all-time favorite games, which turn out to be deeply dated references that confuse Carles generationally. Rather than mocking Conan, Carles thoughtfully connects the older titles to design principles that still hold up. The exchange becomes a gentle lesson in video game history.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The games you love say a lot about when you stopped paying attention.

highlights: Jackie from Ontario works at the Toronto Humane Society and shares stories from her time studying baboons.
Conan gets practical advice from Jackie on tricks to make a cat actually like you.
Jackie's dual expertise in domestic shelter animals and wild primate behavior gives Conan plenty to work with.
Welcome to the Jungle.summary: Jackie, an animal specialist from Ontario who works at the Toronto Humane Society and has a research background in baboon behavior, calls in to give Conan a wide-ranging tour of animal expertise that spans from the primate savanna to the domestic shelter cat. The episode opens with Conan establishing Jackie's credentials with the kind of incredulous delight he reserves for fans who have genuinely unusual expertise — someone who has studied baboons in the field is not what he expected from a Toronto caller. Jackie explains how her academic background in primate behavior eventually led her toward applied animal welfare work at the Humane Society, a transition that sounds logical once explained but that Conan insists sounds like a significant career detour. The baboon stories dominate the first half of the episode. Jackie describes the complex social hierarchies of baboon troops, the surprising ways baboons express friendship, and at least one encounter that she frames as genuinely alarming in retrospect. Conan listens intently and asks follow-up questions that reveal he has done no preparation but has good instincts for what the audience wants to hear. The second half pivots to practical advice: Jackie gives Conan concrete, research-backed tips on how to make a cat actually like you, a challenge that apparently defeats most cat owners. Conan engages with these tips earnestly before admitting he probably still couldn't pull it off. The episode ends with Jackie leaving Conan with a specific behavioral exercise to try, and Conan promising to report back, a callback promise he makes with obvious sincerity.

highlights_v2.title: Baboon Social Hierarchies Explained
highlights_v2.summary: Jackie walks Conan through the surprisingly nuanced social structure of baboon troops, including how alliances form, shift, and occasionally collapse in ways that parallel human office politics. Conan latches onto this comparison immediately and runs it further than Jackie intended. The segment is both genuinely informative and steadily funnier.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: A baboon troop and a writers' room have more in common than I'd like to admit.
