highlights: Emotional health coach Chris pivots the session to analyzing the psychological wellbeing of Conan's dog.
Conan describes his dog's behavioral quirks and Chris offers a surprisingly earnest therapeutic assessment.
Chris explains the most common emotional issues he encounters when coaching clients through life crises.
The Emotional Health of My Dog.summary: Chris, an emotional health coach from Wilmette, Illinois, calls in expecting to discuss his work helping humans navigate life crises — and finds the conversation pivoting almost immediately to the psychological wellbeing of Conan's dog. Conan, who has discussed his dogs with evident affection across many episodes, launches into a detailed description of his dog's behavioral quirks, separation anxiety tendencies, and general emotional presentation. Chris, to his credit, meets the assignment with complete professional earnestness — listening carefully and offering a surprisingly thorough therapeutic assessment of the animal's probable inner life. The comedic engine of the episode is the contrast between Chris's genuine clinical framework being applied to a dog who cannot consent to therapy. Conan pushes into the specifics: does the dog show signs of anxious attachment? Is the behavior at the door when Conan leaves evidence of distress or just habit? Chris draws on his understanding of human emotional patterns and maps them onto canine behavior with more success than Conan expected, which only makes it funnier. The conversation also covers Chris's actual work — coaching humans through significant emotional challenges, the most common patterns he sees in clients, and the tools he uses to help people process difficult transitions. But the dog section is clearly where the episode lives. Conan's willingness to be genuinely invested in the emotional health of his pet, delivered without irony, is what makes the whole thing work.

highlights_v2.title: Dog Gets A Therapy Session
highlights_v2.summary: The conversation pivots from Chris's human coaching practice to an impromptu therapeutic assessment of Conan's dog, based entirely on Conan's description of its behavior. Chris applies his clinical framework to the animal with complete earnestness, diagnosing probable anxious attachment and noting signs of stress in the dog's described routines. Conan is both delighted and slightly alarmed by the accuracy.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Based on what you're describing, that dog has some work to do. And honestly, so do you.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Describes His Dog's Quirks
highlights_v2.summary: Conan delivers an unexpectedly earnest and detailed account of his dog's behavioral patterns — the separation anxiety signs, the greeting rituals, the strange compulsions — with evident genuine concern. Chris listens attentively and asks follow-up questions a therapist would ask a human client. The juxtaposition of clinical rigor and canine subject matter generates sustained comedy.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: He does this thing when I leave — I don't want to say it's crying, but it's crying.

highlights_v2.title: Common Emotional Coaching Patterns
highlights_v2.summary: Chris walks Conan through the most recurring emotional patterns he encounters when coaching clients through major life transitions — loss, identity shifts, career upheaval. Conan engages seriously before inevitably steering the conversation back toward whether these frameworks apply to dogs. Chris handles the pivot with professional grace.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Most people come in thinking the problem is external. The work is helping them see what's actually internal.

highlights: Leather craftsperson Emma reveals she specializes in making custom bondage harnesses and bespoke sex gear.
Conan explores the surprisingly technical artistry and craftsmanship behind Emma's unusual leather work.
Emma describes her most memorable custom commission and the clients who request the most elaborate designs.
Gonzo's Nose.summary: Emma, a leather craftsperson based in the United States, calls in to reveal that her specialty is not belts or boots but custom bondage harnesses and bespoke sex gear — and the conversation becomes one of the more memorable Conan fan episodes for exactly the reasons you'd expect. The RSS description confirms the subject matter directly: Conan speaks with Emma about her work making leather bondage harnesses and what she calls 'the wonderful world of custom sex gear.' Conan, never one to shy away from an unexpected subject, leans into the conversation with his characteristic mix of enthusiasm and mock-scandalized energy. Emma brings genuine craftsperson's pride to the discussion, explaining that the technical demands of her work — stitching, hardware selection, tension distribution, material durability — are every bit as rigorous as any other fine leather craft. Conan, who has probably never thought about the engineering requirements of a bondage harness, is genuinely fascinated by the skill involved. Emma describes her client base and the highly specific, sometimes elaborate custom commissions she receives — the requests that require the most creative problem-solving and the most unusual materials sourcing. The episode title, a reference to the Muppet's most distinctive physical feature, suggests either a visual pun on leather crafting or a specific memorable moment involving the shape of a commission. Throughout, Emma's matter-of-fact professionalism provides a perfect comedic counterweight to Conan's escalating delight.

highlights_v2.title: The Craft Behind Bondage Harnesses
highlights_v2.summary: Emma explains the genuine technical complexity of making custom leather bondage harnesses — precision stitching, hardware load ratings, tension distribution across the body — framing her work with the same craftsperson's pride as any fine leatherworker. Conan, initially focused on the subject matter, finds himself genuinely impressed by the engineering rigor involved.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The hardware has to be rated for actual weight loads. This isn't decorative — it has to perform.

highlights_v2.title: Most Elaborate Custom Commission
highlights_v2.summary: Emma describes her most memorable custom commission — a request requiring unusual materials, complex construction, and considerable creative problem-solving. Conan responds with escalating delight as the specifics emerge, and Emma's total professional composure throughout makes the exchange funnier than any comedic framing could.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The client had a very specific vision. My job is to make the vision real, regardless of what the vision is.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Discovers Leather Artistry
highlights_v2.summary: Conan steers the conversation toward the artistic and aesthetic dimensions of Emma's work — the design choices, the material selections, and the question of whether custom sex gear can be considered fine craft. Emma argues yes, with conviction. Conan, having arrived expecting shock comedy, leaves with what might be genuine respect for the trade.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I came in expecting weird and I'm leaving impressed. I didn't expect to be impressed.

highlights: Tarot reader Patti lays out a personalized card spread and delivers Conan's fortune live on the podcast.
Conan receives his reading with a mixture of skepticism and visible delight at the cards he draws.
Patti explains her methodology and the most startlingly accurate readings she's given to disbelieving clients.
The High Priestess.summary: Patti, a tarot reader from the United States, calls in not just to discuss her practice but to give Conan a live, personalized card reading on the air — and the RSS confirms this is exactly what happens. The episode opens with Patti explaining her methodology: how she approaches a reading, what the different card positions mean, and how she interprets combinations rather than treating individual cards as fixed predictions. Conan, whose skepticism is calibrated perfectly for this kind of segment — just committed enough to play along, just irreverent enough to keep things funny — submits to the process with evident enjoyment. As Patti lays out Conan's spread and begins interpreting, the comedy comes from Conan's reactions: his visible delight when a card seems flattering, his mock offense when one suggests difficulties ahead, and his genuine curiosity about what the symbolism actually means. Patti holds her own throughout, neither defensive about skeptics nor willing to oversell the mystical dimension. The most memorable stretch comes when Conan's reading turns up cards that Patti finds genuinely interesting — combinations she describes as unusual or significant — and Conan's shift from ironic amusement to something closer to real attention is one of the episode's quiet pleasures. Patti also shares stories of her most startlingly accurate readings for disbelieving clients — the moments when the cards said something so precise it changed a skeptic's mind. The episode title, referencing the High Priestess card itself, suggests it appeared in Conan's reading.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Gets A Live Reading
highlights_v2.summary: Patti lays out a personalized tarot spread for Conan live on the podcast and begins interpreting the cards in real time. Conan submits to the process with a mix of ironic amusement and genuine curiosity, reacting with visible pleasure when cards seem favorable and mock offense when they suggest challenges ahead. The live reading format gives the episode an unusual spontaneous energy.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: That card doesn't mean what you want it to mean, Conan. I'm sorry.

highlights_v2.title: Skeptic Turns Genuinely Curious
highlights_v2.summary: Midway through Conan's reading, Patti identifies a combination of cards she describes as unusual or significant — and Conan's tone shifts noticeably from performed skepticism to something resembling real attention. The moment where a committed non-believer leans in despite themselves is the episode's quiet emotional peak. Patti handles the shift without overselling it.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You can feel the moment when someone stops performing skepticism and actually starts listening.

highlights_v2.title: Readings That Changed Skeptics' Minds
highlights_v2.summary: Patti shares her most startlingly accurate readings — the cases where the cards produced information so specific or on-target that confirmed non-believers walked out shaken. Conan, freshly emerged from his own reading, listens with more openness than he started with. The stories function as the episode's case for taking Patti's practice seriously.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I've had people call me weeks later. They don't know what to do with what happened in that session.

highlights: Taxidermist Maureen from Michigan describes the most bizarre animal preservation requests she's received.
Conan asks Maureen exactly how and where she would pose a taxidermied version of him for display.
Maureen walks through her process, making Conan increasingly uncomfortable with the anatomical specifics.
Rest In Pee Wee.summary: Maureen, a taxidermist from Michigan, calls in to discuss her unusual profession — and the conversation takes its most memorable turn when Conan asks Maureen to design and pose a taxidermied version of him. The RSS description confirms this is the episode's central premise: Conan speaks with Maureen about taxidermy and specifically how and where she would pose a taxidermied Conan. Maureen opens by demystifying the craft — explaining the preservation process, the sculpting involved, and the surprisingly artistic decisions that go into presenting an animal in a naturalistic or thematic pose. Conan, immediately interested in the mechanics, asks about the most bizarre preservation requests she's received from clients: the pets preserved in unusual poses, the wild animals requested in improbable settings, and the commissions that tested the boundaries of her skills. Maureen's descriptions make Conan visibly uncomfortable in the best possible way. The episode's comic peak arrives when Conan submits to the hypothetical: Maureen is tasked with determining exactly how she would pose a taxidermied Conan O'Brien and where she would display the result. She approaches the assignment with professional seriousness — considering his height, his distinctive features, the pose that would best capture his essence — and her anatomical specificity makes Conan increasingly squirmy. The title, a gentle tribute to Pee Wee Herman, suggests either the name of a beloved pet or a thematic reference to the small, the quirky, and the preserved.

highlights_v2.title: Design A Taxidermied Conan
highlights_v2.summary: Conan asks Maureen to determine exactly how she would pose and display a taxidermied version of him, and she accepts the assignment with full professional commitment. Her consideration of his height, distinctive features, and the ideal setting for display — delivered with anatomical specificity — makes Conan progressively more uncomfortable. It is the episode's undisputed comedic peak.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You'd want to showcase the height, obviously. And the hair. The pose would need to suggest motion — maybe mid-monologue.

highlights_v2.title: Bizarre Animal Preservation Requests
highlights_v2.summary: Maureen describes the most unusual preservation commissions she's received — pets posed in elaborate thematic arrangements, wild animals requested in improbable settings, and requests that pushed the limits of her technical skills. Conan, initially approaching the subject with curiosity, grows increasingly squeamish as the specifics accumulate.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: People have very specific ideas about how they want their pets to spend eternity. Very specific.

highlights_v2.title: The Craft Behind Taxidermy
highlights_v2.summary: Maureen walks Conan through the actual process of taxidermy — the preservation techniques, the sculpting that underpins the final form, and the artistic decisions that determine how an animal is presented. Conan is surprised to find genuine artistry at the heart of what he'd assumed was a purely technical process. The groundwork Maureen lays here makes the later Conan-posing conversation even funnier.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Every pose is a decision about what story you want the animal to tell. It's not so different from sculpture.

highlights: Professional organizer Rebecca from Seattle reveals the most chaotically disorganized homes she's ever tackled.
Conan confesses exactly how he disposes of his unwanted belongings — Rebecca does not approve.
Rebecca gives Conan a verbal organizational assessment; the prognosis is not flattering.
Social Morays.summary: Rebecca, a professional organizer from Seattle, calls in to discuss her work helping people reclaim order from domestic chaos — and quickly pivots to subjecting Conan to an organizational assessment he does not pass. The RSS description makes the episode's dynamic explicit: Conan talks to Rebecca about working as a professional organizer and how Conan disposes of his unwanted belongings. Rebecca opens by describing the scope of her work — from basic decluttering to full household systems overhauls — and the most chaotically disorganized homes she's ever encountered on the job. The descriptions are vivid enough that Conan begins to seem uncertain about his own living situation. The episode's comic engine ignites when Conan confesses how he actually disposes of his unwanted belongings — a method or combination of methods that Rebecca clearly finds professionally objectionable. Her reaction, caught between diplomatic tact and genuine professional horror, drives the conversation into some of its funniest territory. She proceeds to give Conan a verbal organizational assessment based on his self-reported habits, and the prognosis is not encouraging. Rebecca's advice is practical and direct — the kind of guidance she'd give any client — delivered with the knowledge that Conan is unlikely to implement any of it. The episode title, a pun on 'social mores' and moray eels, suggests the conversation also touches on the social and psychological dimensions of clutter — what it says about a person, and the unspoken rules people have around their stuff.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Confesses His Disposal Methods
highlights_v2.summary: Conan reveals exactly how he gets rid of unwanted belongings, and Rebecca's reaction makes clear that his approach does not meet professional organizing standards. Her attempt to maintain diplomatic composure while registering genuine professional concern produces the episode's funniest sustained beat. Conan, characteristically, seems proud of his system.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: That is... not a system. What you're describing is not a system.

highlights_v2.title: The Most Chaotic Homes Rebecca Tackled
highlights_v2.summary: Rebecca describes the most severely disorganized homes she's ever worked in — the cases that required not just reorganization but a complete reimagining of how a person relates to their possessions. The details are vivid and escalating. Conan listens with growing self-recognition, though he stops short of identifying himself with any particular example.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: There are people who genuinely cannot see the clutter anymore. It just becomes the environment.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Receives A Failing Assessment
highlights_v2.summary: Rebecca conducts a verbal organizational assessment of Conan based on his self-reported habits, and the results are not flattering. She delivers her findings with the measured honesty of a professional who has learned to soften bad news without obscuring it. Conan accepts the verdict with exaggerated wounded dignity.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Based on everything you've told me, you are what we call a 'high-functioning' situation. Emphasis on 'functioning.'

highlights: Visually impaired rock climber Lux from London explains the techniques and trust required to climb without sight.
Conan is pressed to confess the single dumbest and most physically dangerous thing he's ever done.
Lux describes what it feels like reaching a summit having navigated the entire ascent with a visual impairment.
Lux et Veritas.summary: Lux, a visually impaired rock climber from London, calls in to describe a pursuit that most sighted people would find intimidating — and Conan, in exchange, is pressed to confess the single dumbest and most physically dangerous thing he's ever done. The RSS description confirms this bilateral structure: Conan chats with Lux about rock climbing with a visual impairment and the dumbest, most physically dangerous thing Conan has ever done. Lux opens by explaining the techniques that make climbing without sight possible — how a guide climber communicates route information through a system of verbal cues, the heightened reliance on tactile feedback from rock surfaces, and the way spatial awareness is built through feel and memory rather than visual mapping. Conan, who is clearly moved by the engineering of this trust relationship, asks increasingly specific questions about how Lux and a guide synchronize on difficult routes. The conversation reaches an emotional high point when Lux describes what it feels like to reach a summit having navigated the entire ascent without vision — the particular quality of that accomplishment when the achievement is built on a kind of information gathering the rest of the world takes for granted. In exchange, Conan delivers his confession: the dumbest and most physically dangerous thing he's ever done, which lands with the combination of self-deprecating humor and genuine stupidity the premise promises. The episode title, Latin for 'light and truth,' is a Yale motto reference that doubles as an elegant irony given Lux's visual impairment.

highlights_v2.title: Climbing Without Sight Explained
highlights_v2.summary: Lux walks Conan through the techniques that make rock climbing with a visual impairment possible — the verbal communication system with a guide climber, the reliance on tactile feedback from rock surfaces, and the spatial awareness built through feel and memory alone. Conan's questions grow increasingly specific as his genuine fascination overtakes his hosting instincts.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You build the route in your head through your hands. By the time you're at the top, you know every inch of it differently than anyone who watched it.

highlights_v2.title: Reaching The Summit Without Sight
highlights_v2.summary: Lux describes what it feels like to reach the top of a climb having navigated the entire ascent without vision — the specific quality of that accomplishment and what the summit means when you've built it from a fundamentally different kind of information. Conan listens without interrupting, which for him is a significant tribute to the weight of the moment.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Sighted climbers see the summit coming. I feel it arrive. That's a different kind of knowing.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's Dumbest Dangerous Moment
highlights_v2.summary: In exchange for Lux's climbing disclosures, Conan confesses the single dumbest and most physically dangerous thing he's ever done — a story that combines genuine stupidity with the self-awareness of a man who has had time to process the full absurdity of his past choices. Lux, who takes calculated physical risks professionally, is given standing to judge. She does.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I knew it was dangerous. That was, I will admit, part of the appeal at the time.

highlights: Small animal vet Jonathan describes the most dramatic emergency cases he's handled in his practice.
Conan is asked which animal he would transform into if given the chance — his answer surprises Jonathan.
Jonathan explains what owners most commonly misunderstand about their pets' health and behavior.
The Mafia Puppy Doctor.summary: Jonathan, a small animal veterinarian from the United States, calls in to talk about his practice — and the conversation pivots on one of the more creative hypotheticals Conan poses to a fan: which animal would you transform into if given the chance? The RSS description confirms this as the episode's central conceit: Conan talks to Jonathan about being a small animal vet and what kind of animal Conan would turn into if he had the chance. Jonathan opens by describing the daily reality of his practice — the emergency cases, the routine examinations, and the particular emotional weight of working with animals who cannot describe their symptoms. The most dramatic cases Jonathan has handled emerge in vivid detail: the emergencies that required improvisation, the surgeries that went unexpectedly, the recoveries that defied expectation. Conan responds to the medical specifics with his characteristic mix of fascination and gentle horror. The conversation turns to the most common misunderstandings owners have about their pets — the behavioral patterns people misread, the health issues they dismiss, and the beliefs about animal cognition that veterinary science has since debunked. Then comes Conan's assignment: Jonathan asks which animal Conan would choose to become, and his answer — delivered with the commitment of someone who has apparently thought about this — surprises Jonathan enough to generate a genuine reaction. The episode title's reference to the Mafia and puppies suggests either a memorable case from Jonathan's practice or a specific comedic moment involving both subjects.

highlights_v2.title: Most Dramatic Vet Emergency Cases
highlights_v2.summary: Jonathan describes the most dramatic emergencies he's handled in his small animal practice — cases requiring improvisation under pressure, unexpected surgical complications, and recoveries that surprised even him. Conan responds with a mix of genuine admiration and wide-eyed discomfort at the medical specifics. The descriptions give the episode an unexpected intensity.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: In those moments you're not thinking about anything except the animal in front of you. Everything else goes quiet.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Picks His Animal Form
highlights_v2.summary: Jonathan asks Conan which animal he would transform into if given the choice, and Conan's answer — delivered with evident prior conviction — catches Jonathan off guard. His follow-up reasoning is characteristically Conan: part self-mythology, part genuine reflection on what he envies about a particular creature's existence. Jonathan's reaction suggests the answer was not what most people say.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I've thought about this. More than I probably should have. And I have an answer.

highlights_v2.title: What Owners Get Wrong About Pets
highlights_v2.summary: Jonathan explains the most common misunderstandings owners bring into his practice — behavioral patterns people misread as affection or spite, health warning signs that get dismissed, and beliefs about animal cognition that the science has since overturned. Conan, a self-described devoted dog owner, takes some of the corrections personally.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The number of people convinced their cat is punishing them for something — that's not what's happening. That's never what's happening.

highlights: Designer Phil from Minneapolis creates fake products for a fake grocery store, and explains the business logic.
Conan and Phil brainstorm increasingly absurd fake products that would fit naturally on fake store shelves.
Phil describes which of his fake product designs proved most convincing to unsuspecting real-world observers.
Phil The D!@k Whittler.summary: Phil, a designer from Minneapolis, calls in to explain his singular professional niche: he creates fake products for a fake grocery store. The RSS description confirms this is exactly the premise — Conan talks with Phil about designing fake products for a fake grocery store, an occupation that sounds like a setup but turns out to be a genuine and surprisingly thoughtful creative practice. Phil explains the business logic behind his work: film and television productions, commercial shoots, and staged environments require grocery shelves stocked with products that look completely real but don't actually exist — no real brand names, no licensed logos, but all the visual language of authentic consumer packaging. Phil designs the labels, names, and visual identities of these fictional products with enough craft that they pass for real to anyone not reading closely. Conan, immediately fascinated by the conceptual territory, begins asking about the decision-making process: how does Phil choose names that feel generically authentic? What makes a fake product look real? The conversation's most entertaining stretch involves Conan and Phil brainstorming increasingly absurd fake products that would fit naturally on the shelves of a fake store — the kind of riffing session that clearly delights both participants. Phil also reveals which of his fictional product designs have proved most convincing to real-world observers — the ones people have actually tried to find in stores. The episode title's censored wordplay suggests Phil's portfolio includes at least one memorably shaped design that became a signature piece.

highlights_v2.title: Designing Products That Don't Exist
highlights_v2.summary: Phil explains the craft behind creating fake products for fake grocery stores — designing labels, names, and visual identities that replicate the entire language of consumer packaging without referencing any real brand. Conan is immediately gripped by the conceptual puzzle of making something look generically authentic. Phil walks through the specific design decisions that make or break the illusion.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Authenticity in fake products is about hitting every visual convention without triggering any specific memory. It can't look like anything you've seen before.

highlights_v2.title: Brainstorming Increasingly Absurd Products
highlights_v2.summary: Conan and Phil begin riffing together on fake products that would fit naturally on the shelves of a fictional grocery store, and the brainstorm escalates into sustained absurdity. Both participants clearly enjoy the creative exercise, and the product names and concepts they generate get progressively more elaborate. The collaborative energy is the episode's most infectious stretch.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: If the name makes you pause for just a second and think 'wait, is that real?' — that's the sweet spot.

highlights_v2.title: Designs That Fooled Real People
highlights_v2.summary: Phil reveals which of his fictional product designs have been convincing enough that real people tried to find them in actual stores — the cases where the illusion fully collapsed the line between fake and real. Conan is delighted by this as both a design achievement and an absurdist outcome. Phil describes what these designs had in common that made them so persuasive.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I've had people get genuinely frustrated that they couldn't find it. That's when you know you did your job.

highlights: Optometrist Sara from Westerly, Rhode Island describes the most horrifying eye ailment she's encountered professionally.
Conan reacts with escalating horror as Sara details increasingly graphic eye-related medical cases.
Sara assesses Conan's eye health habits and finds them predictably wanting for someone his age.
An Eye for an Eye.summary: Sara, an optometrist from Westerly, Rhode Island, calls in to discuss her work — and the conversation pivots quickly from professional practice to an increasingly graphic tour of the worst eye ailments Sara has encountered in her career. The RSS description notes that Conan speaks with Sara about the worst eye ailment she's ever seen in her work as an optometrist, making clear that this is the episode's driving question. Sara opens by establishing the scope of optometry — routine examinations, corrective lens fittings, and the early detection of conditions that can indicate systemic disease. But Conan steers toward the extreme end of her professional catalog almost immediately. Sara delivers her most harrowing cases with the calm specificity of a medical professional: the conditions that were either the most visually disturbing, the most medically alarming, or the most emotionally affecting to treat. Conan's reactions are the episode's comedic backbone — his escalating expressions of horror as each case tops the last, his attempts to maintain composure when the details become genuinely difficult, and his periodic requests for Sara to slow down or back up when something particularly graphic lands. The conversation takes a turn when Sara turns her clinical eye on Conan himself — assessing his eye health habits based on his self-reported behaviors and finding them predictably inadequate for a man of his age. Her professional verdict is delivered with gentle authority. The episode title, a twist on the famous phrase, nods to the reciprocal structure: Sara describes eye ailments, Conan receives an eye health assessment.

highlights_v2.title: The Most Horrifying Eye Ailment
highlights_v2.summary: Sara describes the single worst eye ailment she's encountered professionally — a case that combines medical severity with visceral horror in a way that makes Conan visibly struggle to maintain composure. She delivers the clinical details with optometrist's calm, which makes the gap between her tone and the content even more affecting. Conan's reaction is the episode's most quoted moment.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: I need you to understand that what I'm about to describe is something I think about more than I'd like to.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's Escalating Horror
highlights_v2.summary: As Sara works through her catalog of graphic eye cases, Conan's reactions build from curiosity to discomfort to what can only be described as performative suffering — periodically asking Sara to pause, slow down, or spare him the final detail. Sara, unmoved, continues. The contrast between her professional matter-of-factness and Conan's escalating response drives the episode's sustained comedy.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You're going to tell me anyway, aren't you. You're not going to spare me any of this.

highlights_v2.title: Sara Assesses Conan's Eye Health
highlights_v2.summary: Sara turns her clinical judgment on Conan's self-reported eye health habits and finds them wanting — screen time, sleep patterns, and eye care routines that suggest a man not taking the longevity of his vision seriously. She delivers her assessment with gentle professional authority. Conan accepts the verdict while indicating he will not be changing any of his habits.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: For someone your age with your habits, I'd say your eyes are doing their best under difficult conditions.

highlights: Minor league pro wrestler Donovan reveals he has built a Conan O'Brien-inspired wrestling persona.
This was the very first episode of 'Conan O'Brien Needs a Fan,' making Donovan the original fan caller.
A second fan, Leigh-Anne from Toronto, joins as Conan discusses intrusive mothers and being yourself.
Fanhausen.summary: In the very first episode of 'Conan O'Brien Needs a Fan,' Conan speaks with Donovan, a minor league pro wrestler from Michigan who has built an entire wrestling persona directly inspired by Conan O'Brien. Donovan explains how he modeled his character on Conan's lanky, pale, self-deprecating style and general absurdist energy, channeling it into a ring persona that has been gaining traction on the independent circuit. Conan, clearly tickled by the idea of being the muse for a wrestling villain, probes Donovan on exactly which of his qualities translated best into a heel persona — and the answer involves both his hair and his perceived smugness. The conversation is a genuinely warm one: Conan is both flattered and mildly horrified that someone has immortalized him in spandex. Donovan walks through what performing as a Conan-adjacent character actually looks like in practice — the entrance music, the character's catchphrases, and the crowd reactions. Conan asks whether being a Conan clone helps or hurts in the ring, and Donovan's answer suggests the gimmick is working. The episode also features a second fan, Leigh-Anne from Toronto, who joins to discuss intrusive mothers and the importance of always being yourself — a tonal shift that gives the inaugural episode a dual-track structure that would define the segment going forward. As the first ever CONAF caller, Donovan occupies a special place in the show's history, one that became dramatically more significant when he later debuted in the WWE as 'Danhausen.'

highlights_v2.title: Conan Becomes a Wrestling Character
highlights_v2.summary: Donovan reveals he built his entire minor league wrestling persona around Conan O'Brien — appearance, attitude, and all. Conan reacts with a mixture of flattery and mock alarm, pressing Donovan on which specific Conan qualities made for the best wrestling villain material. The answer involves his pale complexion, tall frame, and perceived pomposity.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: You took what I am and made it a character — which implies it wasn't already.

highlights_v2.title: The Very First Fan Call
highlights_v2.summary: This conversation marks the historic first episode of 'Conan O'Brien Needs a Fan,' making Donovan the original fan caller in the segment's run. Conan acknowledges the inaugural nature of the call with characteristic self-awareness, noting that the whole enterprise could collapse immediately. The moment has an improvisational energy — neither host nor guest quite knows what the format is yet.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: This is the first one of these — so if it goes badly, that's on you.

highlights_v2.title: Ring Persona vs. Real Conan
highlights_v2.summary: Donovan breaks down how the wrestling character 'Danhausen' performs in front of live crowds versus how the real Conan actually comes across. Conan pushes to understand if the gimmick gets cheered or booed, leading to a funny exchange about whether being Conan-like is inherently villainous. Donovan's diplomatic answer — that it depends on the crowd — lands perfectly.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The character works because people aren't sure if they should love him or hate him — kind of like you.

highlights: Architectural historian Cooper from Ohio explains the process of nominating buildings to the historic registry.
Conan learns what architectural category or building type he would be if he were a structure.
Cooper's expertise in preservation history leads to a playful evaluation of Conan's historical significance.
Peeping Cooper.summary: Cooper, an architectural historian from Ohio, calls in to explain a profession that most people do not know exists — nominating buildings to the National Historic Register. He walks Conan through what the process actually involves: research into a building's historical significance, the preparation of detailed nominations, and the bureaucratic journey through state and federal review. Conan, who has opinions about buildings only in the sense that he has opinions about everything, immediately wants to know what makes something worth preserving versus not. Cooper explains the criteria — architectural distinction, historical association, cultural significance — and Conan begins stress-testing each one with increasingly absurd hypotheticals. The episode's central comedic premise arrives when Conan asks what kind of building he would be if he were a structure, and Cooper, to his enormous credit, takes the question seriously. His answer draws on genuine architectural vocabulary — discussing Conan's stylistic excess, his period-appropriate prominence, and his somewhat ungainly proportions — and arrives at a classification that delights Conan while also mildly roasting him. The conversation also touches on the tension between preservation and development, and Cooper's obvious passion for the field gives the episode an unexpectedly substantive spine. Conan is clearly charmed by Cooper's combination of deep expertise and willingness to apply that expertise to completely frivolous questions. The title 'Peeping Cooper' plays on both his architectural voyeurism — studying buildings from the outside — and the general impression that architectural historians are always looking at things the rest of us walk past without noticing.

highlights_v2.title: What Building Would Conan Be
highlights_v2.summary: Conan poses the question every architectural historian secretly dreads: if Conan O'Brien were a building, what kind would he be? Cooper treats this as a legitimate professional exercise, pulling out actual architectural categories and applying them to Conan's physical and temperamental characteristics. The resulting classification — delivered with scholarly seriousness — is both accurate and devastating.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Architecturally speaking, you'd be something grand in concept, slightly impractical in execution, and impossible to ignore.

highlights_v2.title: Nominating Buildings to History
highlights_v2.summary: Cooper explains the actual mechanics of getting a building onto the National Historic Register — the research, the nomination documents, the review process. Conan is genuinely surprised by how labor-intensive and judgment-dependent the process is, pressing Cooper on who ultimately decides what counts as historically significant. The answer reveals more human subjectivity in the process than Conan expected.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Someone has to argue for every building that gets saved — it doesn't happen on its own.

highlights_v2.title: Conan's Historical Significance Evaluated
highlights_v2.summary: Cooper applies his preservation expertise to Conan himself, assessing whether Conan O'Brien would meet the threshold for historic designation if he were a physical structure. The criteria — architectural distinction, association with significant events, cultural importance — map onto Conan's career in ways both flattering and pointed. Cooper's conclusion is diplomatically worded but contains a clear verdict.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Under the criteria we use, you'd qualify — but the review board would have notes.

Jücs'd.summary: Jon, a juice bar entrepreneur from Reno, Nevada, calls in and immediately buries the lede: he leads with the fact that he has webbed toes. Not the juice bar. The toes. This sequencing is either a deeply considered personal branding strategy or a pure instinct for what makes a good opening, and either way it works brilliantly with Conan, who cannot move past the webbed toes for a significant portion of the episode. Jon apparently deploys this fact in every social situation as his opening line, a choice that has presumably made his social life considerably more interesting than average. Once Conan has processed the toes (or accepted that he cannot fully process them), the conversation turns to Jücs'd — Jon's juice bar venture in Reno, a business that is clearly a genuine passion project but which, in the shadow of the webbed toes reveal, occupies a slightly different register than Jon might have intended. The business details are real: the concept, the menu approach, the challenges of launching a small food business in a mid-sized market. But Conan, infected by the spirit of the webbed toes, becomes increasingly invested in how Jon should be marketing the business — suggestions that are both funnier and more genuinely strategic than expected. By the end, Conan has appointed himself Jücs'd's unofficial marketing consultant, a role he takes seriously enough that it feels like something might actually come of it.

highlights_v2.title: Webbed Toes Are the Brand
highlights_v2.summary: Jon opens by revealing his webbed toes — a fun fact he leads with in every social situation — and Conan's response sets the episode's tone: genuine astonishment, followed by the realization that this is one of the best opening lines in fan-call history. The toes dominate the episode for longer than any toe-related discussion has any right to, and it is exactly the right choice.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Every single time. First thing. Webbed toes. It works.

highlights_v2.title: The Juice Bar Is Also a Thing
highlights_v2.summary: Jon eventually pivots to his actual purpose for calling — his juice bar, Jücs'd — and the transition from webbed-toe conversation to small-business discussion is a comedic gear shift the episode handles beautifully. Jon's genuine enthusiasm for the venture is clear, and the business itself sounds interesting, but the episode has been changed by what came before it. The juice bar is legitimately compelling; it's just standing in the toes' shadow.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: The juice bar is real. The toes are also real. Both of these things exist in Reno.

highlights_v2.title: Conan Becomes Unofficial Marketing Consultant
highlights_v2.summary: Conan, seized by entrepreneurial sympathy and ambient creative energy, starts offering Jücs'd marketing suggestions with increasing specificity and commitment — leading to a bit where the host is more invested in the fan's business outcomes than the fan expected. The suggestions land somewhere between genuinely usable and completely chaotic, which is exactly where Conan marketing advice should live.
highlights_v2.notable_quote: Lead with the toes. Put them on the cup. 'Jücs'd — Made by a Man with Webbed Toes.' I'm telling you.
